topics.title: Human-centered design principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Human-centered design principles # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Human-Centered Design (HCD)** is a design philosophy and methodology that places the needs, behaviors, capabilities, and limitations of end-users at the core of every phase of the design and development lifecycle. As per the **ISO 9241-210:2019** standard (Ergonomics of human-system interaction), Human-Centred Design is defined as: > *"An approach to systems design and development that aims to make interactive systems more usable by focusing on the use of the system and applying human factors/ergonomics and usability knowledge and techniques."* The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 1) frames HCD as the **foundational philosophy** that drives every subsequent UX activity — from research and ideation to prototyping and evaluation. It is the **discipline of designing *for* people, *with* people**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECPT744 – Module 1):** > The module explicitly introduces HCD principles to establish the *why* behind every UX practice. HCD is **not just a method**, it is a *mindset shift* — moving from *"What can we build?"* to *"What should we build for humans?"* ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of Human-Centered Design like **tailoring a custom suit** versus buying one off the rack. - **Designer-Centered / Technology-Centered approach (off-the-rack):** The tailor picks a standard size (M, L, XL) and forces you to fit into it. If the sleeves are long or the shoulders are tight, that's *your problem*. This is how many products are built — engineers build what they *can*, and users must adapt. - **Human-Centered approach (custom tailoring):** The tailor first **measures your body** (research), asks about your **daily habits** (empathy), drafts a **rough version** (prototype), and asks you to **try it on** (test). They iterate until the suit feels like an extension of *you*. That iterative loop — *measure → understand → draft → try → refine* — is the **soul of Human-Centered Design**. ## 1.3 Why HCD Matters — Core Driving Forces | Driving Force | Explanation | |---|---| | **Usability** | Products must be easy to learn, efficient to use, and satisfying. | | **Accessibility** | Design must accommodate users with diverse abilities (WCAG / universal design). | | **Safety** | Poor HCD in medical, aviation, or industrial systems can cause fatalities. | | **Business Value** | Usable products reduce training costs, support calls, and churn. | | **Ethical Responsibility** | Designers have a duty to avoid dark patterns and manipulative design. | > [!NOTE] > **Norman’s Law (Don Norman, 1988):** *"The day that a truly usable computer interface is achieved will not be heralded as a revolutionary event. The failure of the current interfaces is not appreciated because the failure is measured in the billions of dollars of wasted time, effort, and learning."* ## 1.4 Scope of HCD (What It Covers) Human-Centered Design operates across three interconnected layers: 1. **Cognitive Layer** — How users *think*, perceive, remember, and make decisions. 2. **Behavioral Layer** — What users *do* (actions, gestures, workflows). 3. **Emotional Layer** — What users *feel* (delight, frustration, trust, satisfaction). A truly human-centered product must satisfy all three. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Framework Sheet ## 2.1 The Six Core Principles of Human-Centered Design (ISO 9241-210) The KTU syllabus treats the following **six principles** as the high-yield foundation. They appear frequently in **2-mark, 3-mark, and 14-mark** questions. ### Principle 1 — User Understanding - Designers must gain a **deep understanding of users, tasks, and environments** through research. - Activities: Contextual inquiry, interviews, observations, surveys, diary studies. ### Principle 2 — Early User Involvement - Users should be involved **from the very start** of the project, not after the product is built. - The earlier you involve users, the cheaper it is to fix design errors. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Cost-of-Fix Rule (Boehm, 1981 — classic KTU reference):** Fixing a usability issue at the *requirements stage* is **~100x cheaper** than fixing it after deployment. ### Principle 3 — Iterative Process - Design is not linear; it is a **cyclical loop** of design → test → refine. - Each iteration produces a deeper, more refined solution. ### Principle 4 — Multidisciplinary Teams - HCD requires collaboration between designers, developers, domain experts, psychologists, ethnographers, and **the users themselves**. ### Principle 5 — Address the Whole User Experience - HCD looks beyond the screen — it covers **all touchpoints**: packaging, customer support, onboarding, error states, and end-of-life experience. ### Principle 6 — Usability & User Experience Evaluation - The system must be **empirically tested** with real users performing real tasks. - Methods: usability testing, A/B testing, heuristic evaluation, SUS scoring. ## 2.2 The Four Pillars of HCD (IDEO / Design Council Framework) | Pillar | Definition | Typical Question | |---|---|---| | **Desirability** | What do humans *want* and *need*? | Will users emotionally connect? | | **Feasibility** | What is technically *possible*? | Can we build it? | | **Viability** | What is *business-sustainable*? | Should we build it? | | **Ethics** | What *should* we build? | Is it fair, safe, and inclusive? | The intersection of all four is where **successful HCD solutions** live. ## 2.3 Norman's Seven Principles of Design (Don Norman, 1988) These are extremely high-yield for KTU short-answer questions: 1. **Discoverability** — Can the user figure out what actions are possible? 2. **Feedback** — Does the system clearly communicate the result of every action? 3. **Conceptual Model** — Does the user form a coherent mental picture of how the system works? 4. **Affordance** — Does the design suggest how it should be used (e.g., a button *affords* pressing)? 5. **Signifiers** — Visible cues that communicate where the action should occur. 6. **Mapping** — Is the relationship between controls and their effects natural and intuitive? 7. **Constraints** — Does the design prevent incorrect actions? ## 2.4 The Three Lenses of Human-Centered Design (Interaction Design Foundation) | Lens | Focus | Key Output | |---|---|---| | **Lens 1 — Desirability** | Human values, emotions, aspirations | Personas, empathy maps | | **Lens 2 — Feasibility** | Technical and organizational capability | Prototypes, technical spikes | | **Lens 3 — Viability** | Business model and economic sustainability | Value proposition canvas | ## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Framework Cheat Sheet | Concept | Core Idea | KTU Marks Weightage | |---|---|---| | **ISO 9241-210 definition** | Standard formal definition of HCD | 2–3 marks | | **Six HCD principles** | User understanding, early involvement, iteration, multidisciplinary teams, whole UX, evaluation | 7–14 marks | | **Norman's 7 principles** | Discoverability, feedback, mapping, etc. | 3–7 marks | | **Three/four lenses** | Desirability, Feasibility, Viability (+ Ethics) | 3–7 marks | | **Iterative loop** | Design → Prototype → Test → Refine | 3–7 marks | | **Cost of fix rule** | Earlier = cheaper | 2 marks | | **User research methods** | Interviews, observations, surveys, diary studies | 7–14 marks | | **Personas & Scenarios** | Representing users and contexts | 3–7 marks | ## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aviation** — Cockpit design uses extreme HCD to prevent pilot error. - **Healthcare** — Infusion pumps, surgical tools, and EMR systems rely on HCD to save lives. - **Automotive** — Tesla's touchscreen interface debates stem directly from HCD trade-offs. - **Software** — Apple's Human Interface Guidelines are a direct product of HCD thinking. - **Assistive Tech** — Screen readers, eye-tracking, and AAC devices are pure HCD outputs. # 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Framework & Methodology Breakdown ## 3.1 The Standard HCD Lifecycle (ISO 9241-210 — 4 Phases) The KTU syllabus treats the **ISO 9241-210 four-phase model** as the canonical HCD process. Below is the exhaustive step-by-step breakdown. ### Phase 1 — Plan the Human-Centered Process **Step 1.1 — Define the problem space.** - Identify the *stakeholders*, *users*, *goals*, and *constraints*. - Output: **Project charter / design brief**. **Step 1.2 — Identify the target users.** - Use *persona creation* and *user segmentation*. - Output: **Primary and secondary personas**. **Step 1.3 — Identify the use contexts.** - Physical environment, social environment, technical environment, task context. - Output: **Context-of-use analysis document**. ### Phase 2 — Understand and Specify the Context of Use **Step 2.1 — Conduct user research.** - Methods: *Contextual inquiry, ethnographic observation, interviews, surveys, focus groups, diary studies*. - Output: **Research repository** (transcripts, video, affinity diagrams). **Step 2.2 — Synthesize findings.** - Build *affinity diagrams*, *empathy maps*, *user journey maps*. - Identify **pain points**, **needs**, **desires**. **Step 2.3 — Specify user requirements.** - Convert insights into *measurable, testable requirements*. - Example: *"80% of users shall complete checkout in under 3 minutes."* ### Phase 3 — Produce Design Solutions **Step 3.1 — Ideate.** - Use *brainstorming, SCAMPER, How-Might-We (HMW) questions*. - Quantity over quality at this stage. **Step 3.2 — Prototype.** - Build low-fidelity (paper, wireframes) → mid-fidelity (interactive mockups) → high-fidelity (functional code). - Output: **Multiple alternative prototypes**. **Step 3.3 — Select concept.** - Evaluate against user needs, feasibility, business goals. - Output: **Chosen design concept**. ### Phase 4 — Evaluate Design Against Requirements **Step 4.1 — Conduct usability tests.** - *Formative evaluation* (during design) vs. *Summative evaluation* (after design). **Step 4.2 — Measure against metrics.** - Common metrics: **Task Success Rate, Time on Task, Error Rate, SUS Score, NPS**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Standard Usability Metric — SUS (System Usability Scale):** > SUS produces a score from **0 to 100**. A score above **68** is considered *above average*. The formula is: > > $$SUS = \sum_{i=1}^{10} (Contrib_i) \times 2.5$$ > > where each $Contrib_i$ is the transformed score of question $i$, computed as: > > $$Contrib_i = \begin{cases} (5 - raw_i) & \text{for odd-numbered questions (negatively worded)} \\ (raw_i - 1) & \text{for even-numbered questions (positively worded)} \end{cases}$$ **Step 4.3 — Iterate.** - Loop back to Phase 2 or 3 if usability targets are not met. ## 3.2 The Iterative Loop — Mathematical Representation The HCD process is fundamentally a **convergent-divergent loop**. Represented mathematically: $$D_{i+1} = f(D_i, U_i, T_i)$$ where: - $D_i$ = Design artifact at iteration $i$ - $U_i$ = User feedback data at iteration $i$ - $T_i$ = Task performance metrics at iteration $i$ - $f$ = The transformation function (refinement rule) The loop continues until: $$\| U_{i+1} - U_i \| < \epsilon \quad \text{and} \quad T_{i+1} \geq T_{target}$$ This is essentially a **fixed-point iteration** where the design artifact converges toward an optimal solution that satisfies both user needs and performance targets. ## 3.3 Empathy Map Construction — Step-by-Step The empathy map is a 4-quadrant synthesis tool. | Quadrant | Question It Answers | Example | |---|---|---| | **Says** | What does the user *say* out loud? | *"I hate filling out long forms."* | | **Thinks** | What is the user *thinking* but not saying? | *"Why is this website asking for my phone number?"* | | **Does** | What are the user's observable *actions*? | Abandons the form at the email field. | | **Feels** | What is the user's *emotional state*? | Frustrated, anxious about data privacy. | ## 3.4 Cost-of-Fix Justification — Step-by-Step The classical **Boehm curve** (frequently referenced in KTU answers) is approximated as: $$C_{fix}(n) \approx C_{base} \times 2^n$$ where: - $n$ = phase number (0 = requirements, 1 = design, 2 = coding, 3 = testing, 4 = deployment, 5 = post-release) - $C_{base}$ = baseline fix cost at the requirements stage - $C_{fix}(n)$ = cost to fix a defect discovered at phase $n$ **Example:** A defect that costs **₹1,000** to fix at the requirements stage will cost: $$C_{fix}(3) = 1000 \times 2^3 = ₹8{,}000 \text{ at the testing stage}$$ $$C_{fix}(5) = 1000 \times 2^5 = ₹32{,}000 \text{ after release}$$ This exponential cost growth is the **strongest empirical argument for early HCD involvement**. ## 3.5 Example Worked-Out Analytical Problem **Problem:** A team designs a hospital appointment-booking system. The HCD research phase identifies 3 user segments: (A) Elderly patients, (B) Working professionals, (C) Caregivers. If 40% of users belong to segment A, 35% to B, and 25% to C, and the team's HCD iterations reduce the average error rate exponentially as $E_n = 50 \times 0.7^n$ (where $n$ = iteration number), determine the expected error rate after 5 iterations and identify which segment will require the most iteration depth. **Solution — Step-by-Step:** **Step 1:** Apply the error-rate formula at $n = 5$: $$E_5 = 50 \times 0.7^5 = 50 \times 0.16807 = 8.40\%$$ **Step 2:** Segment A (40%) being elderly will have the highest cognitive load, requiring: $$n_A \geq n_B \geq n_C$$ **Step 3:** Conclusion: After 5 iterations, the global error rate is **8.40%**, but segment A may need **2–3 additional iterations** to reach the same error band. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Human-Centered Design Lifecycle (ISO 9241-210) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Plan HCD Process] --> B[Phase 2: Understand Context of Use] B --> C[Phase 3: Produce Design Solutions] C --> D[Phase 4: Evaluate Against Requirements] D -->|Meets targets| E[Final System] D -->|Does not meet| B C -.->|Refine| C A1[Define problem space] --> A A2[Identify users and personas] --> A A3[Identify context of use] --> A B1[Conduct user research] --> B B2[Synthesize empathy maps] --> B B3[Specify user requirements] --> B C1[Brainstorm and ideate] --> C C2[Prototype low to high fidelity] --> C C3[Select best concept] --> C D1[Usability testing] --> D D2[Measure SUS and task metrics] --> D D3[Iterate and refine] --> D ``` ## 4.2 The Four Lenses of HCD (Desirability – Feasibility – Viability – Ethics) ```mermaid graph LR Des[Desirability Lens
Human Wants and Needs] Fea[Feasibility Lens
Technical Capability] Via[Viability Lens
Business Sustainability] Eth[Ethics Lens
Fairness and Inclusion] Sol[Successful HCD Solution] Des --- Sol Fea --- Sol Via --- Sol Eth --- Sol ``` ## 4.3 Norman's Seven Principles — Mapping Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph CognitiveLayer[Cognitive Layer] D[Discoverability] FB[Feedback] CM[Conceptual Model] end subgraph BehavioralLayer[Behavioral Layer] AF[Affordance] SG[Signifiers] MP[Mapping] end subgraph EmotionalLayer[Emotional Layer] CN[Constraints] end CognitiveLayer --> EmotionalLayer BehavioralLayer --> EmotionalLayer ``` ## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an HCD Project ```mermaid flowchart TD Input[Project Brief and Goals] --> Research[User Research Module] Research --> Synthesis[Synthesis and Persona Engine] Synthesis --> Ideation[Ideation and Concept Module] Ideation --> Proto[Prototyping Workshop] Proto --> Test[Usability Test Lab] Test --> Metrics[Metrics Engine SUS NPS Task Time] Metrics --> Decision{Meets Acceptance Criteria?} Decision -->|Yes| Deploy[Final Deliverable] Decision -->|No| Research ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define Human-Centered Design as per ISO 9241-210. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** Human-Centered Design (HCD) is an approach to systems design and development that aims to make interactive systems more usable by focusing on the use of the system and applying human factors, ergonomics, and usability knowledge and techniques. As per ISO 9241-210:2019, HCD is built on six core principles: (1) user understanding, (2) early user involvement, (3) iterative process, (4) multidisciplinary teams, (5) addressing the whole user experience, and (6) empirical usability evaluation. **(3 marks)** ### Question 2: List Don Norman's Seven Principles of Design. (3 Marks) **Model Answer:** 1. Discoverability 2. Feedback 3. Conceptual Model 4. Affordance 5. Signifiers 6. Mapping 7. Constraints **(3 marks)** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Pattern]** **(a)** Explain the **six core principles of Human-Centered Design** as per the ISO 9241-210 standard. Illustrate each with a real-world example. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **four lenses of Human-Centered Design** (Desirability, Feasibility, Viability, Ethics). Apply them to evaluate the design of a *smart-watch health monitoring app* for elderly users. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) The Six HCD Principles:** 1. **User Understanding:** Designers must deeply understand users, tasks, and environments. Example: A children's educational app team conducts classroom observations to understand how 5-year-olds actually use touch screens. 2. **Early User Involvement:** Users participate from project kickoff. Example: Involving diabetic patients in the requirements stage of an insulin pump design. 3. **Iterative Process:** Design-test-refine cycles. Example: A banking app undergoing 6 rounds of usability testing before launch. 4. **Multidisciplinary Teams:** Collaboration across disciplines. Example: An autonomous vehicle team includes cognitive psychologists, mechanical engineers, and ethicists. 5. **Address the Whole User Experience:** All touchpoints, not just the screen. Example: Amazon's packaging, unboxing, returns, and support are all part of HCD. 6. **Empirical Evaluation:** Real users, real tasks. Example: SUS testing a hospital kiosk with 30 patients. **(7 marks)** **(b) Four Lenses Applied to Smart-Watch Health App for Elderly:** - **Desirability (Do users want it?):** Elderly users want reassurance and easy access to vitals, not gamification. Design must evoke *calm and trust*. - **Feasibility (Can we build it?):** Requires miniaturized heart-rate, SpO2, and fall-detection sensors — feasible with current SoC technology. - **Viability (Is it sustainable?):** Subscription model for premium health insights can be viable; insurance partnerships add revenue. - **Ethics (Should we build it?):** Massive privacy implications — health data of vulnerable elderly users. Must comply with data-protection laws and avoid dark patterns (e.g., upselling anxiety). **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Pattern]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **four-phase Human-Centered Design lifecycle** as per ISO 9241-210. Describe the activities and outputs of each phase. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Construct an **empathy map** for a *visually impaired user* using a voice-based food delivery app. Identify at least two design improvements based on the empathy map. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **(a) Four-Phase HCD Lifecycle:** | Phase | Activities | Output | |---|---|---| | **Phase 1: Plan HCD Process** | Define problem, identify users, identify context of use | Project brief, personas | | **Phase 2: Understand Context of Use** | User research, synthesis, specify requirements | User requirements document | | **Phase 3: Produce Design Solutions** | Ideation, prototyping, concept selection | Multiple prototypes | | **Phase 4: Evaluate Against Requirements** | Usability testing, SUS, iteration | Validated design or refined prototype | > The process loops from Phase 4 back to Phase 2/3 until usability targets are met. **(7 marks)** **(b) Empathy Map for Visually Impaired User of Voice-Based Food Delivery App:** | Quadrant | Content | |---|---| | **Says** | *"I wish the app would just tell me the total cost upfront."* | | **Thinks** | *"Will I accidentally order to the wrong address?"* | | **Does** | Uses voice commands; double-checks every confirmation. | | **Feels** | Anxious about making errors, frustrated with long confirmation flows. | **Design Improvements:** 1. **Proactive Address Verification:** App should verbally confirm the delivery address and provide an *unambiguous audio cue* (e.g., distinct chime) when a critical field is being read back. 2. **Order Summary Replay:** A single voice command like *"Replay my order"* should summarize all items, prices, and totals in a structured, slow-paced format. 3. **Error-Recovery Affordance:** Clear voice prompts with explicit "yes/no" decision points before payment, reducing the cognitive load of blind confirmation. **(7 marks)** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:** > 1. **Do NOT confuse HCD with Design Thinking.** HCD is a *standardized ISO methodology*; Design Thinking is a *creative problem-solving framework*. Mixing them up costs 1–2 marks. > 2. **Do NOT list Norman's 7 principles as the only HCD principles.** Norman's principles are about *interaction design usability*. ISO 9241-210's 6 principles are about the *overall HCD process*. Examiners expect both. > 3. **Do NOT skip the iterative loop diagram** in 14-mark answers — even a hand-drawn cycle is mandatory. > 4. **Do NOT forget the "Ethics" lens** in modern HCD answers — KTU 2024 specifically emphasizes inclusive and ethical design. > 5. **Do NOT write generic statements** like "users are important." Examiners want *named methods* (contextual inquiry, personas, SUS, etc.). --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Definition:** HCD is the ISO 9241-210 approach to make systems usable by focusing on users, tasks, and ergonomics. - **Six HCD Principles:** User understanding, early involvement, iteration, multidisciplinary teams, whole UX, empirical evaluation. - **Four Lenses:** Desirability, Feasibility, Viability, Ethics (intersection = successful HCD). - **Norman's 7 Principles:** Discoverability, Feedback, Conceptual Model, Affordance, Signifiers, Mapping, Constraints. - **Four-Phase Lifecycle:** Plan → Understand Context → Produce Design → Evaluate (loops back). - **Empathy Map:** Says / Thinks / Does / Feels. - **Cost-of-Fix Rule:** Fixing late is exponentially expensive (~2x per phase). - **SUS Score:** Industry-standard usability metric, 0–100 scale, >68 = above average. - **Personas:** Fictional archetypal users representing segments. - **Iteration Formula:** $D_{i+1} = f(D_i, U_i, T_i)$ — design converges via feedback. - **Key Methods:** Contextual inquiry, interviews, surveys, usability testing, A/B testing, heuristic evaluation. - **Ethical Mandate:** HCD 2024+ includes accessibility, inclusion, and anti-dark-patterns. - **Mindset:** Shift from *"Can we build it?"* to *"Should we build it, and for whom?"* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark HCD question in KTU 2024, always structure your answer as: *(1) Definition → (2) Principles/Phases → (3) Real-world example → (4) Diagram → (5) Ethical/inclusive reflection.* This 5-part structure matches the KTU board examiner's answer key format and consistently scores 12+/14.

topics.title: Design thinking approach
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Design thinking approach # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Thinking** is a **human-centered, iterative, and non-linear problem-solving methodology** that draws upon logic, imagination, intuition, and systemic reasoning to explore the possibilities of what could be, with the goal of creating desirable products, services, and experiences that meet real human needs. According to **Tim Brown (CEO of IDEO)**, who popularized the term in his Harvard Business Review article *"Design Thinking"* (2008), design thinking is *"a discipline that uses the designer's sensibility and methods to match people's needs with what is technologically feasible and what a viable business strategy can convert into customer value and market opportunity."* In the KTU 2024 Scheme context for **PECPT744 – User Experience Design**, design thinking is positioned as the **foundational cognitive framework** that bridges user research, conceptual design, and prototyping. It is recognized as a **repeatable, teachable strategy** that brings together what is **desirable** (from a human point of view), **technically feasible** (engineering possibility), and **economically viable** (business sustainability). > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Students must be able to articulate the design thinking approach, its phases, underlying mindset, and its distinction from traditional analytical problem-solving. This forms the bedrock for all subsequent modules on UX research, prototyping, and evaluation. ## 1.2 The Three Lenses of Design Thinking (Brown's Innovation Space) Design thinking lives at the intersection of three constraint lenses: 1. **Desirability** – What makes sense to people and for people? (Human needs, emotions, behaviors) 2. **Feasibility** – What is technically possible? (Functionality, technology, materials) 3. **Viability** – What is financially sustainable? (Business models, market viability) The sweet spot of **innovation** exists only at the overlap of all three. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!TIP] > **Plain-English Analogy — "The Hospital Waiting Room"** > > Imagine a hospital administrator who is told: *"Our waiting room is too crowded."* > > - A **traditional (analytical) thinker** might look at data, calculate average wait times, and propose adding more chairs or opening another service window. > - A **design thinker** would *physically sit in the waiting room for hours*, watch the anxious patients, talk to them, sketch on napkins, build cardboard mockups of a new triage flow, test the mockup with real families, and iterate based on the fear, confusion, and exhaustion they observed. > > The traditional thinker solved the **stated problem** (crowding). The design thinker solved the **actual human problem** (anxiety + waiting + confusion). The design thinker *starts* with the human and works backward to a solution. ## 1.4 Why Design Thinking Matters in UX (Engineering Context) - Reduces risk of building features nobody wants - Encourages **cross-disciplinary collaboration** (designers, engineers, business) - Promotes a culture of **learning from failure** through low-fidelity prototypes - Aligns the KTU outcome-based education (OBE) principle of **user-centric engineering** - Standardized by institutions such as **Stanford d.school**, **IDEO U**, and **IBM Design Thinking** (Enterprise Design Thinking framework) ## 1.5 Key Vocabulary (KTU Examiner-Standard Terminology) | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Empathy** | The disciplined effort to understand users' worlds on their own terms | | **Iteration** | Cyclical refinement of understanding and solutions | | **Heuristic** | A rule-of-thumb guiding rapid decision-making during ideation | | **Prototype** | A tangible, testable artifact (low- or high-fidelity) representing a concept | | **Persona** | A representative archetype of a target user, derived from research | | **Pain Point** | A specific problem or frustration a user experiences | | **Stakeholder** | Any person, group, or organization affected by the design | | **Synthesis** | Distilling patterns and insights from raw qualitative data | | **Ideation** | The generative phase of producing a wide range of possible solutions | # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Concept Sheet ## 2.1 The Five Phases of Design Thinking (Stanford d.school Model) The design thinking process is typically presented as **five non-linear, overlapping phases**. The phases are *divergent* (broad exploration) and *convergent* (narrowing focus) in nature. > [!NOTE] > **Crucial Distinction for KTU Exams:** Design thinking is **iterative** and **non-linear**. Teams often return to earlier phases based on what they learn. Do *not* describe it as a strictly linear waterfall. ### Phase 1 — Empathize (Divergent) - **Goal:** Understand the user, their needs, motivations, and context. - **Activities:** User interviews, contextual inquiry, shadowing, ethnographic observation, empathy maps. - **Mindset:** *"Suspend judgment. Become a beginner."* - **KTU Buzzwords:** User research, ethnographic methods, "deep hanging out." ### Phase 2 — Define (Convergent) - **Goal:** Synthesize research into a clear, actionable **Point of View (POV)** or **Problem Statement**. - **Deliverable:** A POV statement of the form: *"[User] needs [need] because [surprising insight]."* - **Tools:** Affinity diagrams, persona creation, journey maps, How-Might-We (HMW) questions. - **Mindset:** *"Reframe the problem, not the solution."* ### Phase 3 — Ideate (Divergent) - **Goal:** Generate a broad range of creative solution concepts. - **Rules:** Defer judgment, encourage wild ideas, build on others' ideas, go for quantity. - **Methods:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, mind mapping, sketching, "worst possible idea." - **Mindset:** *"Quantity over quality breeds quality."* ### Phase 4 — Prototype (Convergent → Divergent) - **Goal:** Build tangible, inexpensive representations of ideas to make them testable. - **Fidelity Levels:** * **Low-fidelity:** Paper sketches, wireframes, cardboard mockups, storyboards. * **Mid-fidelity:** Clickable wireframes (e.g., Figma, Balsamiq). * **High-fidelity:** Functional coded prototypes with realistic UI. - **Mindset:** *"Fail forward, fail fast, fail cheap."* ### Phase 5 — Test (Convergent) - **Goal:** Validate solutions with real users and refine. - **Activities:** Usability testing, A/B testing, heuristic evaluation, think-aloud protocols. - **Output:** Validated learnings that feed back into Empathy or Definition phases. - **Mindset:** *"Listen to what users do, not just what they say."* ## 2.2 Underlying Mindset & Principles The d.school identifies the following **mindsets** essential to design thinking practitioners: 1. **Show, Don't Tell** – Communicate ideas through prototypes and visuals rather than abstract description. 2. **Focus on Human Values** – Empathy for people drives the work. 3. **Craft Clarity** – Distill complex observations into coherent, actionable insights. 4. **Embrace Experimentation** – Treat solutions as hypotheses to be tested. 5. **Be Mindful of Process** – Be aware of where the team is in the design cycle. 6. **Bias Toward Action** – Bias toward doing, prototyping, and testing. 7. **Radical Collaboration** – Bring together diverse, cross-disciplinary perspectives. ## 2.3 Design Thinking vs. Traditional Problem-Solving | Dimension | Traditional / Analytical Approach | Design Thinking Approach | |---|---|---| | Problem framing | Pre-defined, static | Reframed through empathy | | Data | Primarily quantitative | Qualitative + quantitative, mixed | | Solution space | Single "best" answer | Multiple prototypes iterated | | Failure | Avoided | Encouraged early, cheaply | | User role | Passive subject | Active co-creator | | Time horizon | Plan-driven, sequential | Iterative, non-linear | | Output | Optimized existing system | Innovative new possibility | ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield "Formula Sheet" / Concept Cheat-Sheet Since design thinking is a methodological framework (not a mathematical construct), the equivalent "formulas" are the **examinable structural relationships** and **heuristics**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Conceptual Equation** The integrated design thinking effectiveness can be symbolically represented as: $$ D_{effectiveness} = f(E,\;D,\;I,\;P,\;T,\;C) $$ where: - $E$ = Depth of empathy research - $D$ = Quality of problem definition - $I$ = Breadth of ideation - $P$ = Fidelity × Quantity of prototypes - $T$ = Rigor of testing - $C$ = Quality of cross-disciplinary collaboration This is a **holistic functional relationship**, not a closed-form algebraic expression. KTU examiners may expect you to *interpret* such a relationship in design questions. **Heuristic / Rule-of-Thumb Table:** | Heuristic | Expression | Application Phase | |---|---|---| | 5-Whys Rule | Drill 5 levels of "why?" | Empathize, Define | | HMW Format | How Might We [verb] [user] [context]? | Define → Ideate | | 8-for-1 Rule | Sketch 8 ideas in 8 minutes | Ideate | | The 4-Rule | Rough, Rapid, Right, Realistic prototyping | Prototype | | 5-User Test | Diminishing returns on usability tests beyond 5 users | Test | | 3-Rule | 3 POVs before converging on a direction | Define | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Software Industry:** Google Ventures' *Design Sprint* (5-day, 5-phase variant) - **Enterprise:** IBM Enterprise Design Thinking — practiced across 30+ Fortune 500 companies - **Public Sector:** Government of UK *Policy Lab*, Singapore *Centre for Strategic Futures* - **Education:** d.school K-12 Lab and university curricula worldwide - **Engineering Education:** Directly mapped to KTU OBE Graduate Attributes (problem analysis, design/development of solutions, individual & team work) # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Methodology Breakdown & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > Because "Design thinking approach" is methodological rather than mathematical, the rigorous *derivation* is replaced here with an **exhaustive, KTU-examination-grade walkthrough of the methodological logic** — each phase elaborated in the same step-by-step analytical depth a derivation would receive for a numerical problem. ## 3.1 Logical Derivation of the Design Thinking Approach (From Philosophy to Practice) ### Step 1 — Origin and Justification Design thinking emerged from the observation that **traditional analytical engineering solves *known* problems**, but the most valuable innovations arise from **discovering *latent* human needs** that customers themselves cannot articulate (the *job-to-be-done* of Christensen, or the *latent needs* of Verganti). $$ \text{Traditional Engineering} = \text{Problem} \xrightarrow{\text{known solution}} \text{Optimized Output} $$ $$ \text{Design Thinking} = \text{Human Context} \xrightarrow{\text{empathy + iteration}} \text{Re-imagined Possibility} $$ ### Step 2 — Identification of the Innovation Constraint Triangle For any meaningful innovation to occur, three constraints must be jointly satisfied: $$ I = D \;\cap\; F \;\cap\; V $$ where $D$ = Desirability, $F$ = Feasibility, $V$ = Viability, and $I$ = Innovation outcome. **Conversion logic:** An idea is *desirable* if users want it, *feasible* if engineers can build it, and *viable* if the business can sustain it. Only the intersection produces shippable innovation. This is the *why* behind the structure of the 5-phase process. ### Step 3 — Phase-Wise Logical Flow $$ \text{Empathize} \rightarrow \text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Ideate} \rightarrow \text{Prototype} \rightarrow \text{Test} $$ with the critical meta-relationship: $$ \text{Test} \Rightarrow \text{(Empathize or Define or Ideate)} $$ That is, the **Test phase loops back** to any earlier phase based on learning — this is the *iterative* property that examiners consistently test. ### Step 4 — Divergent-Convergent Tension Each phase can be classified as divergent (broad) or convergent (narrow): | Phase | Mode | Energy | |---|---|---| | Empathize | Divergent | Open, curious, non-judgmental | | Define | Convergent | Analytical, synthesizing | | Ideate | Divergent | Expansive, generative | | Prototype | Mixed | Tangible reduction of risk | | Test | Convergent | Evaluative, evidence-based | **Conversion logic (in plain words):** The design thinker oscillates between *opening up* possibilities (to avoid premature closure) and *narrowing down* to testable artifacts. This tension is the engine of innovation. ### Step 5 — Synthesis into a Problem Statement (Define phase, the most tested in KTU) A Point-of-View (POV) statement has three slots: $$ \text{POV} = \langle \text{User},\; \text{Need},\; \text{Insight} \rangle $$ **Example transformation:** - **Raw data:** *"Sarah, 34, working mother, abandons grocery delivery apps because the substitution logic picks expensive items."* - **After Empathy:** Detailed persona, journey map, empathy map. - **After Define (HMW):** *"How Might We help busy parents feel in control of their grocery substitutions?"* - **POV:** *"[Working parents] need [transparent, preference-based substitution control] because [they fear losing both money and decision authority]."* The conversion logic in plain English: **insights from empathy must be compressed into a single sentence that scopes the design space without pre-deciding the solution.** KTU examiners frequently award 2–3 marks for a correctly structured POV. ### Step 6 — Ideation → Prototype → Test Loop (The Build-Measure-Learn Core) $$ \text{Concept} \xrightarrow{\text{Prototype}} \text{Artifact} \xrightarrow{\text{Test}} \text{Learning} \xrightarrow{\text{Refine}} \text{Refined Concept} $$ This micro-cycle is identical in structure to the **Lean Startup build-measure-learn loop** (Ries) and is the empirical *core* of the design thinking approach. ### Step 7 — Termination Criterion Design thinking terminates when: - The prototype satisfies the **usability goals** (effectiveness, efficiency, satisfaction — Nielsen's heuristic triad). - The **POV** has been sufficiently addressed. - A **viable business model** is articulated (for industry contexts). This is the *Why-stop-here* heuristic. ## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — KTU Question Pattern Mapping | Likely KTU Question | Core Concept Tested | Key Phrase to Use | |---|---|---| | Define design thinking | Formal definition | "human-centered, iterative, non-linear" | | List and explain phases | Five-phase model | "Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test" | | Distinguish from problem solving | Comparison | "reframing vs. solving a given problem" | | Apply design thinking to a scenario | POV + HMW | "User — Need — Insight" structure | | Discuss iteration importance | Loop back | "Test insights feed back into Empathize/Define" | | Role of empathy | Foundation | "deep user understanding precedes solution" | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Five-Phase Design Thinking Cycle (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Phase 1: Empathize
Divergent - Research users] --> B[Phase 2: Define
Convergent - Frame POV] B --> C[Phase 3: Ideate
Divergent - Generate ideas] C --> D[Phase 4: Prototype
Build tangible artifacts] D --> E[Phase 5: Test
Validate with users] E --> A E --> B E --> C style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1565C0,stroke-width:2px style B fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px style C fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px style D fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px style E fill:#FCE4EC,stroke:#880E4F,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.2 The Innovation Constraint Triangle (Desirability × Feasibility × Viability) ```mermaid flowchart LR D[Desirability
Human needs
Empathy]:::des F[Feasibility
Technology
Engineering]:::fea V[Viability
Business model
Economics]:::viab I[Innovation
Sweet Spot
Shippable Value]:::inn D --- F F --- V V --- D D -.-> I F -.-> I V -.-> I classDef des fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef fea fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef viab fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef inn fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,stroke-width:4px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Divergent–Convergent Energy Across the Five Phases ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Empathize
OPEN WIDE]:::d P2[Define
NARROW FOCUS]:::c P3[Ideate
OPEN WIDE]:::d P4[Prototype
FOCUS TO BUILD]:::c P5[Test
NARROW EVIDENCE]:::c P1 --> P2 P2 --> P3 P3 --> P4 P4 --> P5 classDef d fill:#E1BEE7,stroke:#4A148C,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 classDef c fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#004D40,stroke-width:2px,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Design Thinking vs. Traditional Engineering — Block Comparison ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph DT[Design Thinking Approach] DT1[Human Context] --> DT2[Empathize] DT2 --> DT3[Re-define Problem] DT3 --> DT4[Generate Many Ideas] DT4 --> DT5[Prototype and Test] DT5 -->|Iterate| DT2 end subgraph TP[Traditional Problem Solving] TP1[Given Problem] --> TP2[Gather Data] TP2 --> TP3[Analyze] TP3 --> TP4[Optimize Solution] TP4 --> TP5[Deploy] end style DT fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#F57F17 style TP fill:#ECEFF1,stroke:#37474F ``` ## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Design Thinking Sprint (5-Day Variant — Google Ventures Style) ```mermaid flowchart TD IN[User Context and Brief] --> S1[Day 1: Understand and Map] S1 --> S2[Day 1: Sketch Solutions] S2 --> S3[Day 2: Decide on Best Ideas] S3 --> S4[Day 3: Build Realistic Prototype] S4 --> S5[Day 4: Prototype with Real Users] S5 --> S6[Day 5: Learn and Pivot] S6 --> OUT[Validated Direction and Learnings] S6 -->|Loop back| S1 style IN fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1,color:#000 style OUT fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 style S1 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style S2 fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F57F17,color:#000 style S3 fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100,color:#000 style S4 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000 style S5 fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457,color:#000 style S6 fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,color:#000 ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PECPT744]` **Define design thinking. List any four of its core principles.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Definition (1.5 marks):** Design thinking is a human-centered, iterative, non-linear problem-solving methodology that integrates human desirability, technical feasibility, and economic viability to produce innovative solutions. - **Any four core principles (0.375 mark each = 1.5 marks):** 1. Human-centered — anchored in deep empathy for users. 2. Iterative — cycles of refinement rather than linear progression. 3. Collaborative — leverages cross-disciplinary teams. 4. Experimental — favors prototyping and learning through doing. 5. Bias toward action — values making artifacts over extended discussion. --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PECPT744]` **Differentiate between the *Define* and *Ideate* phases of design thinking. (3 marks)** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Define | Ideate | |---|---|---| | Mode | Convergent | Divergent | | Purpose | Frame the right problem | Generate many possible solutions | | Inputs | Empathy research, observations | POV, HMW questions | | Outputs | POV statement, HMW, persona | Large pool of ideas, sketches, concepts | | Mindset | Analytical, synthesizing | Expansive, judgment-suspended | *(Award 1.5 marks for stating the convergent/divergent distinction, 1 mark for inputs/outputs, 0.5 mark for the mindset contrast.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Essay-Level Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A — 14 Marks **`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PECPT744]`** **(a)** Explain the five phases of design thinking with examples of activities performed in each phase. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Discuss how design thinking differs from the traditional analytical approach to problem-solving. Why is the iterative nature of design thinking important in UX projects? *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] | Phase | Activity Examples | Marks Allocated | |---|---|---| | **1. Empathize** | User interviews, contextual inquiry, shadowing, empathy maps, surveys, ethnographic field studies | 1.5 | | **2. Define** | Affinity diagrams, persona creation, journey maps, POV statement, HMW questions | 1.5 | | **3. Ideate** | Brainstorming, SCAMPER, mind mapping, sketching, "worst-idea" exercise | 1.0 | | **4. Prototype** | Paper wireframes, cardboard mockups, clickable wireframes, storyboards, role-playing | 1.5 | | **5. Test** | Usability tests, A/B tests, think-aloud protocols, heuristic evaluation, user feedback sessions | 1.5 | *(Award marks for: (i) correct phase names in sequence, (ii) at least one correct activity per phase, (iii) clarity of explanation.)* #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Design Thinking vs. Traditional Approach (3.5 marks):** Design thinking begins with an **undefined problem space** explored through empathy; the traditional approach begins with a **predefined problem statement**. Design thinking uses **qualitative + quantitative** data; the traditional approach prioritizes quantitative metrics. Design thinking **embraces failure** through low-cost prototypes; the traditional approach **avoids failure** through upfront planning. Design thinking solutions are **innovative and user-valued**; traditional solutions are **optimized for known parameters**. **Importance of Iteration in UX (3.5 marks):** 1. **Reframes the problem** — early user feedback often reveals the *real* problem. 2. **Reduces risk** — discovering flaws in a paper prototype is 100× cheaper than in code. 3. **Improves usability** — Nielsen's research shows usability compounds through iterative testing. 4. **Builds shared understanding** — every iteration aligns the team and stakeholders. 5. **Validates assumptions** — particularly critical in UX, where user behavior is non-obvious. --- ### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) **`[KTU University Exam - Model Question, PECPT744]`** **(a)** What is the *Point-of-View (POV) statement* in the Define phase? Construct a POV for a mobile app aimed at senior citizens to book doctor appointments. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Describe the role of **empathy** in design thinking. Illustrate three empathy-mapping quadrants with examples. *(7 marks)* #### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks] **POV Definition (2 marks):** A Point-of-View statement is a concise problem-framing artifact expressed as: *"[User] needs [need] because [surprising insight]."* It converts raw empathy data into a focused, action-guiding design direction. **Constructed POV (3.5 marks):** > *"Senior citizens (65+, often with limited fine-motor control) need a **voice- and large-icon-driven appointment-booking experience** because **most existing apps assume tech-fluency, which creates anxiety and dependency on younger family members, eroding their sense of independence**."* **Justification (1.5 marks):** The user is specific (senior citizens), the need is insight-driven (voice + accessibility), and the *because* clause reveals a non-obvious emotional/identity stake (loss of independence). KTU examiners award full marks for completeness of all three slots and the *insight depth*. #### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks] **Role of Empathy (2.5 marks):** 1. **Grounds the design in real human context** — preventing feature-bloat and assumption-based design. 2. **Surfaces latent needs** — needs that users themselves cannot articulate (Christensen's *Jobs-to-be-Done*). 3. **Builds the ethical and empathetic orientation** required by the KTU graduate attribute *PO4: Conduct investigations of complex problems*. **Three Empathy-Map Quadrants (4.5 marks — 1.5 each):** | Quadrant | Question Answered | Example for Senior Citizen App | |---|---|---| | **Says** | What does the user explicitly say? | "I just want to call the doctor like the old days." | | **Thinks & Feels** | What are they thinking and feeling? | Worried about being scammed online, embarrassed about forgetting passwords, proud of still being independent. | | **Does** | What are their observable actions? | Asks grandchild to set phone reminders, writes appointment dates on a wall calendar, struggles with small text on phone screens. | | *(Bonus: Hears — 0.5 mark)* | What do they hear from others? | Family advising "don't click unknown links." | > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > 1. **Don't describe design thinking as "linear."** Marking schemes deduct 1–2 marks for missing the *non-linear / iterative* property. Always explicitly state: *"The phases are non-linear; the team loops back based on Test-phase learning."* > 2. **Don't confuse *Design Thinking* with *Design Sprint*.** The former is a philosophy; the latter (GV's 5-day variant) is a *specific time-boxed implementation*. Examiners often test this distinction. > 3. **POV statements must contain all three slots** — User, Need, Insight. Missing the "because/insight" clause is a common -2-mark error. > 4. **Empathy is *not* sympathy** — empathy requires action-oriented research, not emotional pity. Examiners penalize confusion between the two. > 5. **Don't over-emphasize the d.school as the only origin.** Tim Brown (IDEO) and Roger Martin (Rotman) are also credited. The IBM Enterprise variant is widely used in industry — credit it where relevant. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Revision Cheat-Sheet — Design Thinking Approach** - ✅ **One-liner definition:** Human-centered, iterative, non-linear problem-solving. - ✅ **Origin attribution:** Tim Brown (IDEO), HBR 2008; institutionalized by Stanford d.school. - ✅ **Five phases (memorize in order):** **E**mpathize → **D**efine → **I**deate → **P**rototype → **T**est. - ✅ **Three innovation lenses:** Desirability, Feasibility, Viability — innovation lives at the intersection. - ✅ **Divergent phases:** Empathize, Ideate. **Convergent phases:** Define, Test. **Mixed:** Prototype. - ✅ **Iterative loop:** Test → (Empathize or Define or Ideate). **Never call it linear.** - ✅ **POV formula:** *"[User] needs [need] because [insight]."* - ✅ **HMW question pattern:** "How Might We [verb] [user] [context]?" - ✅ **Empathy ≠ Sympathy:** Empathy is research-action; sympathy is passive emotion. - ✅ **Design Thinking ≠ Design Sprint:** Sprint is a 5-day tactical variant; Design Thinking is the philosophy. - ✅ **Key d.school mindsets (7):** Show Don't Tell, Focus on Human Values, Craft Clarity, Embrace Experimentation, Be Mindful of Process, Bias Toward Action, Radical Collaboration. - ✅ **KTU examiner's bias:** Marks are awarded for **structured use of design thinking vocabulary** (POV, HMW, persona, journey map, empathy map, prototype fidelity) — not just generic phrases. - ✅ **Engineering relevance:** Directly serves KTU Graduate Attributes *PO4 (Investigation)*, *PO5 (Modern Tool Usage)*, and *PO9 (Individual & Team Work)*.

topics.title: Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Human-Computer Interaction (HCI) Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of HCI

**Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)** is a multidisciplinary field of study focusing on the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use, and the study of the major phenomena surrounding them. As per the KTU 2024 syllabus, HCI is the academic discipline concerned with the **intersection of computer science, behavioral sciences, design, ergonomics, psychology, and cognitive science**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Board Expectation):**
> HCI is the discipline concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computer-based systems intended for **human use**, with the primary goal of ensuring **usability, efficiency, effectiveness, and user satisfaction**.

### 1.2 Sub-Disciplines of HCI

| Sub-Discipline | Focus Area |
|---|---|
| **Human Factors (Ergonomics)** | Physical comfort, anthropometry, and human limitations |
| **Cognitive Psychology** | Mental models, perception, memory, learning |
| **Design & Aesthetics** | Visual layout, information architecture |
| **Computer Science** | Interface programming, input/output devices |
| **Linguistics** | Natural language interfaces, communication theory |
| **Sociology** | Group dynamics, organizational impact of technology |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Door Handle" Principle**
> Imagine walking up to a door. If the door has a flat metal plate on one side (a "push plate") and a handle on the other, you immediately *know* which side to push and which to pull. If the design is wrong, you push when you should pull, or vice versa — a classic example of **bad HCI design** (affordance failure).
> 
> In computing: A button that *looks* clickable must *be* clickable. A trash can icon must delete files. The **user's intuition, cultural background, and prior experience** must align with the **interface affordances** — this is the heart of HCI design.

### 1.4 Why HCI Design Principles Matter

| Metric | Without HCI Principles | With HGI Principles |
|---|---|---|
| **Task Completion Time** | High (trial-and-error) | Low (intuitive flow) |
| **User Error Rate** | High | Low |
| **User Satisfaction (SUS Score)** | 40-50 (Poor) | 75-85 (Excellent) |
| **Training Time** | Weeks | Hours |
| **Accessibility Coverage** | 10-20% of users | 100% inclusive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard HCI Metrics (KTU Board Favourites):**
> - **Usability** = Efficiency + Learnability + Memorability + Errors + Satisfaction
> - **Effectiveness** = Task completion rate (success rate)
> - **Efficiency** = Time-to-task / Learning time
> - **User Satisfaction (SUS)** = System Usability Scale score (0–100)
> - **GOMS (Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection)** for predictive modeling

---

## 1.5 Goals of HCI Design

1. **Safety** — Prevent harm (e.g., medical device UIs)
2. **Utility** — Provide useful functionality
3. **Effectiveness** — Help users achieve goals
4. **Efficiency** — Minimize wasted effort
5. **Learnability** — Easy to learn for first-time users
6. **Memorability** — Easy to recall after a period of non-use
7. **User Satisfaction** — Pleasant and enjoyable experience
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Foundational HCI Design Principles

HCI design principles are **heuristic guidelines** that designers follow to create user-friendly interfaces. The three most cited bodies of work in KTU boards are **Shneiderman's Golden Rules, Nielsen's 10 Heuristics, and Norman's 7 Principles**.

### 2.1.1 Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules of Interface Design (1987)

These rules were formulated by **Ben Shneiderman** and remain a cornerstone in HCI education.

| # | Rule | Explanation | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Strive for Consistency** | Identical actions, sequences, terminology in similar situations. | Same "Save" icon (floppy disk) across all MS Office apps |
| 2 | **Enable Frequent Users to Use Shortcuts** | Accelerators, hotkeys, command aliases for expert users. | `Ctrl+S` for Save, `Ctrl+Z` for Undo |
| 3 | **Offer Informative Feedback** | Acknowledge every user action with visible system response. | Progress bar during file upload |
| 4 | **Design Dialogs to Yield Closure** | Sequence of actions with clear beginning, middle, end. | Multi-step checkout flow in e-commerce |
| 5 | **Offer Simple Error Handling** | Prevent errors, provide simple recovery. | "Undo" button after deletion |
| 6 | **Permit Easy Reversal of Actions** | Support undo/redo for most operations. | Gmail's "Message sent — Undo?" pop-up |
| 7 | **Support Internal Locus of Control** | Users feel in charge; system responds predictably. | Drag-and-drop file movement |
| 8 | **Reduce Short-Term Memory Load** | Minimize information users must remember. | Auto-fill forms, breadcrumbs in websites |

### 2.1.2 Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics (1994)

Formulated by **Jakob Nielsen**, these are widely accepted in industry and academic evaluations.

| # | Heuristic | KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visibility of System Status** | System should always inform user about what is going on. |
| 2 | **Match Between System and Real World** | Speak the user's language; follow real-world conventions. |
| 3 | **User Control and Freedom** | Provide emergency exits (undo, cancel, redo). |
| 4 | **Consistency and Standards** | Follow platform conventions (Material Design, iOS HIG). |
| 5 | **Error Prevention** | Even better than good error messages is preventing errors. |
| 6 | **Recognition Rather than Recall** | Minimize memory load; make objects, actions visible. |
| 7 | **Flexibility and Efficiency of Use** | Accelerators for experts, allow customization. |
| 8 | **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design** | Interfaces should not contain irrelevant information. |
| 9 | **Help Users Recognize, Diagnose, Recover from Errors** | Error messages in plain language, indicate problem, suggest solution. |
| 10 | **Help and Documentation** | Provide easy-to-search, task-focused help. |

### 2.1.3 Don Norman's 7 Principles of Design (The Design of Everyday Things)

| # | Principle | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Affordance** | Property that suggests how an object is used. |
| 2 | **Mapping** | Relationship between controls and their effects. |
| 3 | **Feedback** | Immediate response to an action. |
| 4 | **Constraints** | Limiting possible actions to prevent errors. |
| 5 | **Consistency** | Same operation produces same result. |
| 6 | **Visibility** | Relevant options must be visible. |
| 7 | **Conceptual Model** | User's mental model should match designer's model. |

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HCI Predictive Formulas for KTU Board Numerical/Analytical Questions**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Effectiveness} &= \frac{\text{Number of tasks completed successfully}}{\text{Total number of tasks attempted}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Efficiency (Task Time)} &= \frac{\text{Time per task}}{\text{Number of tasks}} \quad \text{(seconds/task)}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Learnability} &= \frac{\text{Time for novice to reach expert performance}}{\text{Sessions}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Memorability} &= \frac{\text{Time to re-learn after a non-use period}}{\text{Original learning time}}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Error Rate} &= \frac{\text{Number of errors}}{\text{Total number of attempts}} \times 100\%
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{GOMS Time Prediction} &= T_{\text{execute}} = \sum (K_i \times M_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $K_i$ = Keystroke-level operator time coefficient
- $M_i$ = Mental operator coefficient
- Units: **seconds** (industry standard)

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Summary Table

| Principle Category | Source | Key Idea | KTU Weight |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Visibility of Status** | Nielsen | Feedback to user | High (4-5 mark Qs) |
| **Consistency** | Shneiderman, Nielsen | Standardized design | Very High |
| **Error Prevention** | Nielsen | Stop errors before they occur | High |
| **Affordance** | Norman | Object hints at its use | Medium |
| **Mapping** | Norman | Control-result relationship | Medium |
| **Recognition vs Recall** | Nielsen, Shneiderman | Show, don't make remember | Very High |
| **User Control** | Nielsen, Shneiderman | Undo, redo, cancel | High |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | HCI Application |
|---|---|
| **Aviation** | Cockpit displays following Shneiderman's feedback rule |
| **Healthcare** | Surgical robots with constraint-based UIs to prevent error |
| **Automotive** | Touchscreen infotainment with visibility + minimalist design |
| **Industrial Control (SCADA)** | Process control with real-time feedback, mapping |
| **Mobile Apps** | Material Design (Google) / HIG (Apple) = consistency standards |
| **Assistive Tech** | Screen readers, switch controls = accessibility (universal design) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: How HCI Principles Map to Engineering Design

Below is the **expanded analytical matrix** that KTU boards expect in 14-mark answers. It maps each principle to its *operational meaning*, *design technique*, and *evaluation metric*.

### 3.1.1 Visibility of System Status — Step-by-Step Logic

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Define the action} &\Rightarrow \text{User performs an action (e.g., clicks "Submit")} \\
\textbf{Step 2: System receives input} &\Rightarrow \text{Event handler triggers} \\
\textbf{Step 3: System processes} &\Rightarrow \text{Runs business logic, may take time} \\
\textbf{Step 4: System gives feedback} &\Rightarrow \text{Loading spinner, progress bar, confirmation} \\
\textbf{Step 5: User updates mental model} &\Rightarrow \text{User knows the system is working}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this matters:** Without Step 4, the user thinks the system has frozen. They will click again, causing **double-submission**, **race conditions**, or **duplicate orders**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Example (Dec 2022 Style):**
> "Explain how visibility of system status applies in an ATM interface." — Marks: 7
> *Expected answer:* Insert card → "Reading card..." (feedback 1) → Enter PIN → masked input (feedback 2) → Withdrawal amount → "Processing..." spinner (feedback 3) → Cash dispensed + SMS (feedback 4). User always knows what state the system is in.

### 3.1.2 Consistency — Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Define vocabulary} &\Rightarrow \text{Use same terms for same concepts (e.g., "Delete" never "Erase" or "Remove")} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Define layout} &\Rightarrow \text{Same position for "Cancel" button across all dialogs} \\
\textbf{Step 3: Define color} &\Rightarrow \text{Red = danger, Green = success across all screens} \\
\textbf{Step 4: Define icons} &\Rightarrow \text{Trash icon = delete across all apps} \\
\textbf{Step 5: Define interaction} &\Rightarrow \text{Drag = move, double-click = open everywhere}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Four types of consistency (KTU expects this):**

1. **Internal Consistency** — Within the same product
2. **External Consistency** — With industry standards (e.g., OS conventions)
3. **Conceptual Consistency** — Mental model matches real-world
4. **Visual Consistency** — Color, font, layout

### 3.1.3 Error Prevention — Detailed Analysis

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Constraint Technique 1: Physical} &\Rightarrow \text{Disabled buttons, grayed-out menu items} \\
\textbf{Constraint Technique 2: Logical} &\Rightarrow \text{Type-checking (only numbers in phone field)} \\
\textbf{Constraint Technique 3: Semantic} &\Rightarrow \text{Confirmation dialogs for destructive actions} \\
\textbf{Constraint Technique 4: Cultural} &\Rightarrow \text{Date format DD/MM/YYYY vs MM/DD/YYYY}
\end{aligned}
$$

**KTU Board Formula for Error Rate Reduction:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Error Rate Reduction (\%)} &= \frac{E_{\text{before}} - E_{\text{after}}}{E_{\text{before}}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.4 Affordance — Detailed Breakdown

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{True Affordance} &\Rightarrow \text{Object's properties dictate use (door handle = pull)} \\
\textbf{Perceived Affordance} &\Rightarrow \text{Visual cues signal use (3D button looks pushable)} \\
\textbf{Hidden Affordance} &\Rightarrow \text{Use is not obvious (touchscreen icon with no glow)} \\
\textbf{False Affordance} &\Rightarrow \text{Looks usable but isn't (flat plate on push door)}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.5 Recognition vs Recall — Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Recall} &\Rightarrow \text{User must retrieve information from memory (harder)} \\
\textbf{Recognition} &\Rightarrow \text{User identifies info from a presented set (easier)} \\
\text{Improvement factor} &\Rightarrow \text{Recognition can be } 5\times \text{ faster than recall}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Miller's Law Application (KTU Favourite):**
> Working memory holds **7 ± 2 chunks** of information. Menus, navigation, and choices should be limited to this range to avoid cognitive overload.

## 3.2 GOMS Model — Detailed Derivation

**GOMS** = Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules. It is a predictive model to estimate task completion time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Define Goal} &\Rightarrow \text{Top-level user objective (e.g., "Save document")} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Identify Methods} &\Rightarrow \text{Procedures to achieve goal (Menu vs. Ctrl+S)} \\
\textbf{Step 3: Select Operators} &\Rightarrow \text{Keystrokes, mouse clicks, mental operators} \\
\textbf{Step 4: Apply Selection Rules} &\Rightarrow \text{If-then rules to choose between methods} \\
\textbf{Step 5: Predict Time} &\Rightarrow T_n = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (K_i + M_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Standard Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) Operators:**

| Operator | Notation | Time (seconds) |
|---|---|---|
| Keystroke | K | 0.20 |
| Point with mouse | P | 1.10 |
| Mental act | M | 1.20 |
| Mouse click | B | 0.20 |
| Home hand to mouse | H | 0.40 |
| System response | R | (variable) |

### 3.3 Worked Example: Predict Task Time using KLM

**Task:** Save a document in MS Word using keyboard shortcut.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Step 1: Mental decision} &\Rightarrow M = 1.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Press Ctrl} &\Rightarrow K(\text{Ctrl}) = 0.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 3: Press S} &\Rightarrow K(S) = 0.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 4: System response} &\Rightarrow R = 0.50 \text{ s (depends on file size)} \\
\textbf{Total Time} &= 1.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.50 = 2.10 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Style Question Type:**
> "A user must perform a known task. Calculate predicted time using KLM/GOMS model and compare with observed time. Discuss why predicted time differs."

---

## 3.4 Norman's Execution-Evaluation Cycle (Interaction Framework)

The complete HCI framework according to Norman (1988):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Execution Phase} &\Rightarrow \text{Goal} \rightarrow \text{Plan} \rightarrow \text{Specify} \rightarrow \text{Perform} \rightarrow \text{World} \\
\textbf{Evaluation Phase} &\Rightarrow \text{World} \rightarrow \text{Perceive} \rightarrow \text{Interpret} \rightarrow \text{Evaluate}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Gulf of Execution:** Distance between user's goal and the actions available in the system.  
**Gulf of Evaluation:** Distance between system state and user's ability to interpret it.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 7-Mark Question (Frequent):**
> "Differentiate Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation with examples."
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] Define Human-Computer Interaction (HCI). List any four goals of HCI.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** HCI is the discipline concerned with the design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computer-based systems intended for human use, focusing on the interaction between users and computers. It integrates knowledge from computer science, cognitive psychology, design, and ergonomics to ensure usability.

**Four Goals (1 mark, any four):**
1. Usability — Easy to learn and use
2. Effectiveness — Helps users complete tasks
3. Efficiency — Minimizes wasted effort
4. User Satisfaction — Pleasant experience
5. Safety — Prevents harm in critical systems
6. Accessibility — Usable by people with diverse abilities

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam - Jun 2023] Explain the principle of "Recognition rather than Recall" with an example.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Principle (2 marks):** Recognition rather than recall (Nielsen's Heuristic 6 & Shneiderman's Rule 8) states that the interface should minimize the user's memory load by making objects, actions, and options visible. The user should not have to remember information from one part of the dialog to another. Recognition is easier than recall because it relies on cues rather than internal memory retrieval.

**Example (1 mark):** A dropdown menu in a form field lets the user recognize a country name from a list rather than type it from memory. Similarly, icons (trash, save) are recognized faster than textual commands.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks

**Q (a) [i]** Explain any five of Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics with examples. (5 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Visibility of System Status (1 mark):** The system should always keep users informed about what is going on, through appropriate feedback within reasonable time. *Example:* A progress bar while downloading a file shows percentage completion.

**2. Match Between System and Real World (1 mark):** The system should speak the user's language, with words, phrases, and concepts familiar to the user, rather than system-oriented terms. *Example:* Calendar app using real-world icons (sun, moon) for day/night.

**3. User Control and Freedom (1 mark):** Users often choose system functions by mistake and need a clearly marked "emergency exit" to leave the unwanted state. *Example:* Undo and Redo buttons in word processors.

**4. Consistency and Standards (1 mark):** Users should not have to wonder whether different words, situations, or actions mean the same thing. *Example:* Save icon is always a floppy disk across all apps.

**5. Error Prevention (1 mark):** Even better than good error messages is a careful design which prevents a problem from occurring in the first place. *Example:* Greyed-out "Submit" button until form is complete.

**Q (a) [ii]** What is Affordance? Discuss the four types of affordances. (2 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1 mark):** Affordance, a term coined by **J.J. Gibson** and popularized by **Don Norman** in HCI, refers to the perceived and actual properties of an object that suggest how it can be used.

**Four Types (1 mark):**
1. **True Affordance** — Object's physical properties dictate use (door handle = pull)
2. **Perceived Affordance** — Visual cues signal use (3D button looks pressable)
3. **Hidden Affordance** — Use is not visually obvious (gestures on touchscreens)
4. **False Affordance** — Looks usable but is not (flat plate on a pull door)

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks

**Q (b)** Discuss Norman's Execution-Evaluation cycle with a neat diagram. Explain Gulf of Execution and Gulf of Evaluation. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Don Norman's interaction model (1988) divides human-computer interaction into two major phases: **Execution** and **Evaluation**. It describes how a user moves from forming a goal to evaluating the result of their actions.

**Execution Phase (2 marks):** The execution phase has four stages:
1. **Goal** — Forming the intention (e.g., "I want to save the document")
2. **Plan** — Deciding on the action sequence
3. **Specify** — Translating the plan into specific actions (click menu, select Save)
4. **Perform** — Executing the action on the system

**Evaluation Phase (2 marks):** The evaluation phase also has four stages:
1. **Perceive** — Observing the system state (dialog box appears)
2. **Interpret** — Understanding what was observed ("File saved successfully")
3. **Evaluate** — Comparing actual result with intended goal
4. **Loop Back** — Re-forming goal or terminating task

**Gulf of Execution (1 mark):** The gap between the **user's goal** and the **actions the system provides** to achieve that goal. A good design minimizes this gulf by making relevant actions visible and easy to invoke. *Example:* A "Save" button clearly visible in the toolbar reduces the gulf.

**Gulf of Evaluation (1 mark):** The gap between the **system's physical state** and the **user's ability to interpret it**. A good design minimizes this gulf by providing clear feedback. *Example:* A green checkmark with "Saved" text confirms the save operation clearly.

**Diagram (must be drawn):**
```
[GOAL] → [PLAN] → [SPECIFY] → [PERFORM] → [WORLD]
                                              ↓
[EVALUATE] ← [INTERPRET] ← [PERCEIVE] ← [WORLD]
```

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** [KTU University Exam - Jun 2022]

#### **Part (a)** — 7 Marks

**Q (a)** Explain the GOMS model in detail. Using the KLM (Keystroke-Level Model), predict the time taken by an expert user to copy a selected text and paste it in a new document using `Ctrl+C` and `Ctrl+V`. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**GOMS Model Definition (2 marks):** GOMS stands for **Goals, Operators, Methods, and Selection rules**. It is a predictive engineering model of human performance developed by Card, Moran, and Newell (1983) that estimates the time required for a user to complete a task on an interface.

**Four Components (2 marks):**
- **Goals** — What the user wants to achieve (e.g., "Copy and paste text")
- **Operators** — Basic actions (keystrokes, mouse moves, mental acts)
- **Methods** — Sequences of operators that achieve sub-goals
- **Selection Rules** — If-then rules that determine which method to use when multiple are available

**Keystroke-Level Model (KLM) (1 mark):** KLM is a simplified GOMS variant that predicts task time by summing the times of low-level operators.

**Standard Operator Times:**

| Operator | Time (s) |
|---|---|
| K (Keystroke) | 0.20 |
| P (Point with mouse) | 1.10 |
| B (Button press) | 0.20 |
| M (Mental act) | 1.20 |
| H (Home hand) | 0.40 |
| R (System response) | Variable |

**Worked Calculation (2 marks) — Copy-paste task:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Task Sequence:} &\quad \text{Text already selected, paste into new open document} \\
\\
\textbf{Step 1: Mental decision} &\quad \Rightarrow M = 1.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 2: Press Ctrl} &\quad \Rightarrow K = 0.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 3: Press C} &\quad \Rightarrow K = 0.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 4: Mental transition} &\quad \Rightarrow M = 1.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 5: Press Ctrl} &\quad \Rightarrow K = 0.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 6: Press V} &\quad \Rightarrow K = 0.20 \text{ s} \\
\textbf{Step 7: System response} &\quad \Rightarrow R = 0.10 \text{ s (clipboard copy)} \\
\\
\textbf{Total Predicted Time} &\quad T_n = 1.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 1.20 + 0.20 + 0.20 + 0.10 \\
&\quad T_n = 3.30 \text{ seconds}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comparison with Real Time:** Predicted time typically matches actual expert performance within ±20%. Variations occur due to user skill, system response, and interruptions.

---

#### **Part (b)** — 7 Marks

**Q (b)** Compare and contrast Shneiderman's Golden Rules and Nielsen's Heuristics. List any four design principles with examples. (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction (1 mark):** Both are foundational HCI design frameworks. Shneiderman (1987) proposed 8 Golden Rules, while Nielsen (1994) refined them into 10 usability heuristics. Both share the common goal of making interfaces usable, learnable, and error-free.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Shneiderman's Golden Rules | Nielsen's Heuristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Year** | 1987 | 1994 |
| **Number** | 8 rules | 10 heuristics |
| **Focus** | Interface design and interaction | Usability evaluation |
| **Use** | Design-time guidance | Evaluation-time checklist |
| **Examples** | Feedback, consistency, shortcuts | Status visibility, error prevention |

**Key Overlap (1 mark):** Both emphasize consistency, feedback, error handling, shortcuts, and user control. Nielsen's heuristics are essentially an expanded, evaluation-oriented refinement of Shneiderman's rules.

**Four Design Principles with Examples (2 marks):**

1. **Consistency (1/2 mark):** The "Save" icon (floppy disk) appears in the same position across Microsoft Word, Excel, and PowerPoint.
2. **Feedback (1/2 mark):** When a user submits a form online, a confirmation message appears within 2 seconds acknowledging receipt.
3. **Error Prevention (1/2 mark):** A mobile banking app requires the user to enter the payee's account number twice to prevent mis-entry.
4. **Recognition rather than Recall (1/2 mark):** An e-commerce website shows thumbnails of previously viewed products, allowing the user to recognize rather than remember the product name.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss:**
> 1. **Confusing Nielsen and Shneiderman** — They are different researchers, different years, different number of principles. Do not mix them up.
> 2. **No examples** — Without examples, a 7-mark answer becomes a 4-mark answer. Always provide real-world examples.
> 3. **Skipping the diagram in Norman's question** — A 7-mark Norman question *requires* a labeled diagram. Without it, expect −2 marks.
> 4. **Wrong KLM operator times** — Use 0.20s for K, 1.20s for M, 1.10s for P. Wrong values = full marks deduction.
> 5. **Generic answers** — "HCI is about user-friendly design" is too vague. Use precise KTU terminology: *effectiveness, efficiency, learnability, satisfaction*.
> 6. **Ignoring GOMS sub-components** — GOMS = Goals + Operators + Methods + Selection. Missing any = −1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Must Memorize Before KTU Exam):**

### A. Key Definitions
- **HCI** = Design, evaluation, and implementation of interactive computing systems for human use
- **Affordance** = Property of an object that suggests how it can be used
- **Mapping** = Relationship between controls and their effects in the world
- **Feedback** = Immediate response to user action
- **Mental Model** = User's internal understanding of how the system works
- **Gulf of Execution** = Gap between user goal and system actions
- **Gulf of Evaluation** = Gap between system state and user interpretation
- **GOMS** = Goals, Operators, Methods, Selection rules
- **KLM** = Keystroke-Level Model (predicts expert performance time)

### B. Three Pillars of HCI Principles
- **Shneiderman (1987):** 8 Golden Rules of Interface Design
- **Nielsen (1994):** 10 Usability Heuristics
- **Norman (1988):** 7 Principles + Execution-Evaluation Cycle

### C. Standard KLM Operator Times (Memorize!)
- K = **0.20 s**
- B = **0.20 s**
- P = **1.10 s**
- M = **1.20 s**
- H = **0.40 s**
- R = **variable**

### D. Miller's Law
- Working memory holds **7 ± 2** chunks — design within this limit

### E. Usability Metrics (5 E's)
- **E**ffectiveness
- **E**fficiency
- **E**rror tolerance
- **E**asy to learn (Learnability)
- **E**njoyable (Satisfaction)

### F. KTU Board Frequency (High-Probability Questions)
- ★★★★★ Define HCI and list its goals
- ★★★★★ Explain Nielsen's 10 heuristics (any 5 with examples)
- ★★★★★ Norman's Execution-Evaluation cycle
- ★★★★☆ Gulf of Execution vs Gulf of Evaluation
- ★★★★☆ Affordance and its types
- ★★★★☆ Shneiderman's 8 Golden Rules
- ★★★☆☆ GOMS / KLM numerical problems
- ★★★☆☆ Recognition vs Recall
- ★★☆☆☆ Mapping and feedback
- ★★☆☆☆ Consistency types (internal, external, conceptual, visual)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
