**(a)** Explain Nielsen's 10 usability heuristics for user interface design. For each heuristic, provide **one** real-world example of a UI implementation. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| # | Heuristic | Example UI Implementation |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Visibility of system status | "Uploading 65%..." progress bar in Google Drive |
| 2 | Match between system and real world | Trash can icon for "Delete" instead of cryptic "RM" |
| 3 | User control and freedom | "Undo" button after sending an email in Gmail |
| 4 | Consistency and standards | All primary buttons on Amazon are orange across all pages |
| 5 | Error prevention | "Are you sure you want to permanently delete?" confirmation |
| 6 | Recognition rather than recall | Recently used files dropdown in Microsoft Word |
| 7 | Flexibility and efficiency of use | Ctrl+Shift+N to open incognito in Chrome |
| 8 | Aesthetic and minimalist design | Apple iOS Settings — generous white space, grouped options |
| 9 | Help users recognize, diagnose, recover from errors | "This password must contain at least 8 characters" inline |
| 10 | Help and documentation | Contextual "?" tooltips in Adobe Photoshop |

*(1 mark heuristic + brief description, 6 heuristics in full = 6 marks, summary paragraph for remaining 4 = 1 mark = 7 marks)*

**(b)** Conduct a **heuristic evaluation** of a mobile food delivery app (e.g., Swiggy/Zomato) and identify any **three usability violations**. Suggest a design fix for each. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Violation | Heuristic Broken | Description | Suggested Fix |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Visibility of system status** | After placing an order, the "Track Order" map sometimes freezes with no indication. | Add a spinning loader + "Fetching live location..." toast. |
| 2 | **Error prevention** | Address entry allows free-text input, leading to failed deliveries. | Replace with **autocomplete address API** (Google Places) and validated pincode field. |
| 3 | **Consistency and standards** | The "Add to cart" button is green on the home page but blue in restaurant pages. | Standardize the CTA color across **all pages using a design system**. |
| 4 | **User control and freedom** | No way to cancel an order after 2 minutes of placement. | Add a **5-minute cancellation window** with one-tap cancel. |

*(3 marks violation identification + 3 marks design fixes + 1 mark conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks):** Cognitive Laws Application `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**(a)** Derive **Fitts's Law** and **Hick's Law** mathematically. State two engineering applications of each in modern UI design. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Fitts's Law Derivation:**
Fitts observed that the time to move to a target depends on the **Index of Difficulty (ID)**, defined via Shannon's information theory:

$$
ID = \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

Empirically, movement time $T$ is linear in $ID$:

$$
T = a + b \cdot ID
$$

Substituting:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2\!\left(\dfrac{D}{W} + 1\right)
$$

**Applications:**
1. **Large primary CTAs** — increasing button width $W$ reduces $ID$ and therefore click time.
2. **Edge-aligned menus** — placing controls at screen edges makes $D \to 0$, infinite target width, near-zero acquisition time.

**Hick's Law Derivation:**
With $n$ equally probable choices, the information content is $H = \log_2(n)$. Empirically, decision time is linear in information content:

$$
T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)
$$

**Applications:**
1. **Progressive disclosure** in settings menus — instead of 50 options at once, show 5 categories.
2. **Mega-menus** in e-commerce — group products into nested sub-menus to flatten decision trees.

*(2 marks each derivation + 1 mark each application = 6 marks + 1 mark synthesis = 7 marks)*

**(b)** Apply **four** UX design principles to redesign a poorly designed **ATM interface** that currently shows 12 buttons in a 3×4 grid with cryptic labels (e.g., "MINI", "PIN", "FAST"). Propose a redesigned layout and justify with the principles. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Redesigned ATM Layout:**

| Row | Button 1 | Button 2 | Button 3 |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Withdraw Cash** | **Deposit Cash** | **Check Balance** |
| 2 | **Transfer Money** | **Pay Bills** | **Change PIN** |
| 3 | **Mobile Recharge** | **Mini Statement** | **Help / Contact** |

**Justification by Principle:**

1. **Match Between System and Real World (Heuristic 2):** Replace "MINI" with "**Mini Statement**" — user-friendly language.
2. **Recognition Rather than Recall (Heuristic 6):** Group related tasks (cash-related on top row) to reduce cognitive load.
3. **Hick's Law:** Limit visible options to **9** (close to Miller's $7 \pm 2$); use a "More" button for advanced features.
4. **Aesthetic and Minimalist Design (Heuristic 8):** Generous white space, consistent typography, high-contrast primary action color.

*(2 marks redesigned layout + 4 marks four principle justifications + 1 mark conclusion = 7 marks)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse Affordance and Signifier** — examiners specifically test this distinction. State that *affordance* is intrinsic; *signifier* is the visible cue.
> 2. **Do NOT omit examples** — for a 7-mark question, each principle MUST have a UI example. A definition alone earns only 30% of the marks.
> 3. **Do NOT use programming code** in this course's answer sheets — the question is conceptual; pseudocode and algorithms are penalized.
> 4. **Always state the cognitive theory** behind Nielsen's heuristics (e.g., working memory limits, mental models) to fetch full marks.
> 5. **Cite the formula** for Fitts's and Hick's Law in derivation questions — partial credit is given for the equation alone.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Bulletins — Print This Section Before the Exam:**

- **Nielsen's 10 Heuristics** are the **most-tested framework** in KTU Module 2; memorize all 10 with one example each.
- **Norman's 6 Principles** (Affordance, Signifier, Mapping, Feedback, Constraints, Conceptual Model) are the cognitive foundation; **Nielsen = "what"**, **Norman = "why"**.
- **Fitts's Law** $\Rightarrow$ **Large, close targets**; formula: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(D/W + 1)$.
- **Hick's Law** $\Rightarrow$ **Fewer choices**; formula: $T = a + b \cdot \log_2(n+1)$.
- **Miller's Law** $\Rightarrow$ $7 \pm 2$ chunks — limit menu items and form fields.
- **Jakob's Law** $\Rightarrow$ Users prefer familiar patterns — mimic industry conventions.
- **Von Restorff Effect** $\Rightarrow$ Different items are remembered best — highlight CTAs with contrast.
- **Goal Gradient Effect** $\Rightarrow$ Users accelerate near goal completion — show progress bars.
- **Postel's Law** $\Rightarrow$ Be liberal in what you accept — forgiving input forms.
- **Tesler's Law** $\Rightarrow$ Design must absorb complexity, not push it to users.
- **Heuristic Evaluation** uses **3–5 evaluators** to identify ~75–90% of usability issues.
- **SUS score** (System Usability Scale) $\geq 68$ is the industry benchmark for "above average" usability.
- **WCAG 2.1** four pillars: Perceivable, Operable, Understandable, Robust.
- **Don Norman's three levels of emotional design**: Visceral (look), Behavioral (use), Reflective (meaning).
- **Always pair a principle with an example** — this is the #1 mark-fetching strategy in KTU board exams.
- **Differentiate Affordance vs. Signifier** at least once in every answer — it is a high-probability 2-mark question.
- **Aesthetic-Usability Effect**: Users perceive aesthetically pleasing designs as more usable, even when they are not — a recurring distractor in MCQ sections.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User journey mapping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** User journey mapping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **User Journey Mapping (UJM)** is a strategic UX research and design artifact that visually narrates the end-to-end sequence of interactions, emotions, touchpoints, and decision points a specific user persona experiences while attempting to accomplish a defined goal with a product, service, or system.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme (PECPT744) framework, a User Journey Map is positioned as a **diagrammatic synthesis tool** that bridges qualitative user research findings with actionable design strategy. It captures the **"why"** behind user behavior by mapping the cognitive, emotional, and physical journey across time.

A formal definition in the context of the syllabus:

$$\text{User Journey Map} = f(\text{Persona}, \text{Scenario}, \text{Stages}, \text{Touchpoints}, \text{Emotions}, \text{Pain Points}, \text{Opportunities})$$

Where every variable is a discrete research-derived layer, and the function $f$ denotes the structured visualization of their interaction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** User Journey Maps are *not* the same as User Flows (which are task-oriented, linear, and UI-focused) or Sitemaps (which are information architecture). A Journey Map is **experience-oriented**, **narrative**, and **emotionally rich**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a documentary filmmaker following a single traveler from Kerala who is navigating the Cochin International Airport for the first time, trying to catch a flight to Delhi. You don't just record the physical path from "entering the airport" to "boarding the plane." You record:

- **What they see** (signage, screens, queues)
- **What they feel** (confused at immigration, relieved at security clearance, anxious at the gate)
- **What they do** (ask staff, check phone, follow signs)
- **What they think** ("Will I make it in time?", "Where is gate 12?")
- **Where they struggle** (no English signs, long queues, unclear directions)

That **documentary — captured frame by frame, emotion by emotion, touchpoint by touchpoint** — is your **User Journey Map**.

In UX design, the "traveler" is the **user persona**, the "airport" is the **product or service**, and the "documentary frames" are the **stages of the journey**.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Recall Mnemonic — "CUSTOMER" stands for:**
> **C**ontext → **U**ser persona → **S**tages → **T**ouchpoints → **O**bjectives → **M**ood/Emotions → **E**xpectations → **R**esolutions

## 1.3 Core Terminology Snapshot (Bold Constants/Metrics)

- **Persona**: A research-based archetypal user model (semi-fictional).
- **Touchpoint**: Any channel or interface where the user interacts with the product.
- **Moment of Truth (MOT)**: Critical interaction points that determine satisfaction.
- **Pain Point**: Friction, obstacle, or negative emotion in the journey.
- **Opportunity**: A design intervention point to resolve a pain point.
- **Emotional Curve / Arc**: A plotted graph of user sentiment over time.
- **Channel**: The medium of interaction (web, mobile, in-store, phone, email).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Components of a User Journey Map

A KTU-grade journey map is composed of **seven canonical layers** stacked vertically (or rendered as swim lanes). Each layer answers a specific research question:

| # | Layer | Research Question Answered | Data Source |
|---|-------|---------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Persona & Scenario** | *Who* is the user and *what* are they trying to do? | User interviews, surveys |
| 2 | **Phases / Stages** | *What high-level steps* does the journey contain? | Task analysis, contextual inquiry |
| 3 | **Actions / Behaviors** | *What is the user doing* at each step? | Observation, diary studies |
| 4 | **Touchpoints** | *Where/with what* is the user interacting? | Analytics logs, user reports |
| 5 | **Thoughts / Mindset** | *What is the user thinking*? | Think-aloud protocols |
| 6 | **Emotions / Sentiment** | *How does the user feel*? | Sentiment coding, facial coding |
| 7 | **Pain Points & Opportunities** | *Where does it break, and how can we fix it*? | Affinity diagramming, HMW questions |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each layer must be **evidence-backed** — no assumptions. This is the most common reason KTU examiners deduct marks: students present journey maps with invented emotions rather than research-derived ones.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind User Journey Mapping

Journey Maps serve **four strategic functions** in UX practice (and KTU exam answers should reflect this):

1. **Empathy Building** — Forces teams to inhabit the user's experience, not just their task.
2. **Cross-functional Alignment** — Provides a *single shared artifact* for designers, developers, PMs, and stakeholders to align on.
3. **Gap Identification** — Surfaces the *delta* between the *expected journey* (designed) and the *actual journey* (lived).
4. **Opportunity Prioritization** — Enables evidence-based prioritization of design interventions using an *impact-vs-frequency* lens.

## 2.3 Types of User Journey Maps (KTU Classification)

| Type | When Used | Key Output |
|------|-----------|------------|
| **Current State Map** | To diagnose an existing experience | "As-is" baseline of the journey |
| **Future State Map** | To envision a redesigned experience | "To-be" target experience |
| **Day-in-the-Life Map** | To understand holistic lifestyle context | Long, lifestyle-anchored journey |
| **Service Blueprint** | To extend journey into backstage operations | Adds frontstage/backstage layers |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Metrics Cheat Sheet

In UX practice, journey maps are quantified through several metrics. The KTU board often expects familiarity with these:

| Metric / Formula | Definition | Unit / Range |
|------------------|------------|--------------|
| $\text{CES} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} E_i}{n}$ | **Customer Effort Score** — average emotional load per stage | Scale: 1 to 7 (Likert) |
| $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | **Net Promoter Score** — Promoters minus Detractors | $-100$ to $+100$ |
| $\text{CSAT} = \frac{\text{Positive Responses}}{\text{Total Responses}} \times 100$ | Customer Satisfaction | $0$ to $100$ |
| $\text{Drop-off Rate} = \frac{U_{\text{in}} - U_{\text{out}}}{U_{\text{in}}} \times 100$ | Funnel attrition at each stage | $0$ to $100$ |
| $\text{TSAT} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (E_i \cdot w_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i}$ | **Touchpoint-Specific Average Touchpoint satisfaction** (weighted) | Scale: 1 to 5 |
| $\text{Completion Rate} = \frac{\text{Tasks Completed}}{\text{Tasks Attempted}} \times 100$ | Task success rate at a stage | $0$ to $100$ |

> [!WARNING]
> In the markdown table above, note that we have used **`\vert`**-style notation OR plain inline parentheses. **Do not use the pipe symbol `|` inside tables** — it breaks the KTU Markdown rendering pipeline.

Where:
- $E_i$ = Emotion score at stage $i$
- $n$ = Total number of stages
- $w_i$ = Weight of touchpoint $i$ (importance factor)
- $U_{\text{in}}$, $U_{\text{out}}$ = Users entering and exiting a stage

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

User Journey Mapping is industry-critical in:

- **SaaS product design** (mapping onboarding journeys)
- **Healthcare UX** (mapping patient diagnostic journeys)
- **Fintech** (mapping KYC to first transaction)
- **Government digital services** (e-Governance platforms in India, e.g., DigiLocker, UMANG)
- **IoT ecosystems** (mapping multi-device interaction journeys)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Methodology & Analytical Derivation

## 3.1 The 7-Step Process to Construct a User Journey Map (KTU Examiner's Gold Standard)

### **Step 1 — Define the Persona and Scenario**

Select a single, research-validated persona and a clearly bounded scenario.

$$\text{Scenario} = (\text{User Goal}, \text{Context}, \text{Trigger})$$

**Example (B.Tech project context):**
> *"Priya, a 3rd-year B.Tech student in Kerala, wants to register for the KTU exam using the University ERP portal during her lunch break."*

The trigger is an SMS notification from the University; the goal is "registered before the deadline"; the context is mobile-only with intermittent WiFi.

---

### **Step 2 — Identify the High-Level Phases**

Decompose the scenario into **4 to 7 high-level phases**. For a typical digital product:

$$\text{Phases} = \{\text{Awareness}, \text{Consideration}, \text{Onboarding}, \text{Usage}, \text{Support}, \text{Loyalty}\}$$

For the KTU ERP example:

$$\text{Phases} = \{\text{Login}, \text{Profile Verification}, \text{Exam Selection}, \text{Payment}, \text{Confirmation}\}$$

**Logic:** Each phase is a *macro goal* the user is trying to achieve, not a UI screen.

---

### **Step 3 — Map Actions and Behaviors**

For each phase, list the discrete **actions** the user performs.

For "Payment" phase:
- Opens payment gateway
- Enters card details
- Waits for OTP
- Confirms transaction

**Conversion logic:** Actions are the *verbs* the user performs; phases are the *nouns* of intent.

---

### **Step 4 — Identify Touchpoints**

List the **channels and surfaces** involved in each action.

For the same phase:
- Touchpoints: ERP web UI, SMS OTP service, bank 3D-secure page, email confirmation

**Logic:** Touchpoints can be **digital**, **physical**, or **human-mediated** (e.g., calling the helpdesk).

---

### **Step 5 — Capture Thoughts and Mindset**

Use verbatim quotes from research or synthesized cognitive states.

For the "Payment" phase:
- Thought: *"Will my parents' card work? Is this site even secure?"*

This layer captures the **internal narrative**.

---

### **Step 6 — Plot the Emotional Arc**

Assign an emotion score $E_i$ on a scale of $-3$ to $+3$ per phase:

| Score | Emotional Label |
|-------|-----------------|
| $+3$ | Delighted |
| $+2$ | Pleased |
| $+1$ | Satisfied |
| $0$ | Neutral |
| $-1$ | Frustrated |
| $-2$ | Disappointed |
| $-3$ | Distressed |

**The Emotional Curve** is then plotted as a **line graph** of $E_i$ vs. Phase index.

**Example computation for a 5-phase journey:**

$$E_{\text{phase}} = [-1, +1, -2, 0, +2]$$

The arithmetic mean emotional score across the journey:

$$\bar{E} = \frac{(-1) + 1 + (-2) + 0 + 2}{5} = \frac{0}{5} = 0$$

A neutral average — but the variance is high, signaling **emotional volatility** that warrants design intervention.

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(E_i - \bar{E})^2 = \frac{1 + 1 + 4 + 0 + 4}{5} = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0$$

A high emotional variance ($\sigma^2 = 2.0$) signals an **inconsistent experience** — a key insight for design.

---

### **Step 7 — Surface Pain Points and Opportunities**

For each phase, derive:
- **Pain Points** (negative emotions, obstacles, delays)
- **Opportunities** (HMW — "How Might We..." questions)

For the "Payment" phase:
- Pain Point: *"OTP arrives 2 minutes late on BSNL network."*
- Opportunity (HMW): *"How might we allow fallback authentication via email if SMS OTP fails within 60 seconds?"*

This step converts the journey map from a *descriptive artifact* into a *prescriptive design tool*.

---

## 3.2 Service Blueprint Extension (Advanced)

For systems with backend processes, the journey map is extended into a **Service Blueprint** with four additional layers:

$$\text{Blueprint} = \{\text{Customer Actions}, \text{Frontstage}, \text{Backstage}, \text{Support Processes}\}$$

- **Frontstage**: Visible to the user (UI, frontline staff)
- **Backstage**: Invisible to the user but supports the experience (server, database, internal team)
- **Support Processes**: Back-office systems enabling the backstage

The **Line of Visibility** separates frontstage from backstage; the **Line of Internal Interaction** separates backstage from support processes.

---

## 3.3 Validation Heuristics — Is the Journey Map "Good"?

Apply these **KTU-rubric criteria**:

1. **Persona-driven** — not generic "user" language
2. **Research-grounded** — every emotion is sourced
3. **Goal-aligned** — each phase advances the user's goal
4. **Completeness** — covers pre-, during-, and post-experience
5. **Actionability** — surfaces at least 3 concrete HMWs

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Building a User Journey Map (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1 Define Persona and Scenario] --> B[Step 2 Identify High-Level Phases]
    B --> C[Step 3 Map Actions and Behaviors]
    C --> D[Step 4 Identify Touchpoints]
    D --> E[Step 5 Capture Thoughts and Mindset]
    E --> F[Step 6 Plot Emotional Arc]
    F --> G[Step 7 Surface Pain Points and Opportunities]
    G --> H{Map Validated Against Research?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Convert HMWs into Design Requirements]
    H -- No --> J[Return to Step 1 and Refine]
    I --> K[Hand-off to Design and Prototyping Phase]
```

## 4.2 Layered Architecture — User Journey Map Swim-Lane Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Awareness] --> P2[Phase 2 Onboarding] --> P3[Phase 3 Usage] --> P4[Phase 4 Support] --> P5[Phase 5 Loyalty]
    L1[Layer 1 Persona and Scenario]
    L2[Layer 2 Actions and Behaviors]
    L3[Layer 3 Touchpoints]
    L4[Layer 4 Thoughts and Mindset]
    L5[Layer 5 Emotions and Sentiment Score]
    L6[Layer 6 Pain Points]
    L7[Layer 7 Opportunities and HMWs]
    L1 --- P1
    L2 --- P1
    L3 --- P1
    L4 --- P1
    L5 --- P1
    L6 --- P1
    L7 --- P1
```

## 4.3 Service Blueprint Extension Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph LOB[Line of Visibility]
        CA[Customer Actions]
        FA[Frontstage Interactions]
    end
    subgraph LOI[Line of Internal Interaction]
        BA[Backstage Processes]
    end
    SP[Support Processes and Systems]
    CA --> FA --> BA --> SP
```

## 4.4 Emotional Curve Visualization (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Stage 1 Awareness: E plus 1] --> S2[Stage 2 Onboarding: E minus 1]
    S2 --> S3[Stage 3 Usage: E plus 2]
    S3 --> S4[Stage 4 Support: E minus 2]
    S4 --> S5[Stage 5 Loyalty: E plus 3]
    S5 --> INSIGHT[Critical Insight: Steep drop at Support stage requires intervention]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define User Journey Mapping and differentiate it from a User Flow.**

**Model Answer:**

User Journey Mapping is a visual representation of the complete experience a user has while interacting with a product or service to achieve a specific goal. It captures actions, thoughts, emotions, touchpoints, and pain points across time.

| Aspect | User Journey Map | User Flow |
|--------|------------------|-----------|
| **Focus** | Experience & emotion | Task completion |
| **Perspective** | Outside-in (user-centric) | Inside-out (system-centric) |
| **Layers** | Multi-dimensional (7 layers) | Linear (steps only) |
| **Output** | Narrative + insights | Functional diagram |

User flows answer *"how does the system work?"*; journey maps answer *"how does the user feel?"*.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**List any four components of a User Journey Map and briefly explain the role of "Touchpoints."**

**Model Answer:**

The four components are: (i) Persona & Scenario, (ii) Phases/Stages, (iii) Actions/Behaviors, (iv) Touchpoints. (Other valid components: Thoughts, Emotions, Pain Points, Opportunities.)

**Touchpoints** are the specific points of interaction between the user and the product/service at each stage. They can be digital (website, app, email), physical (store, kiosk), or human-mediated (support agent). Touchpoints are critical because they represent *moments of truth* where the user's perception of the brand/product is shaped. Identifying all touchpoints helps designers ensure consistency and quality across the entire ecosystem.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Adapted]**

**(a)** Explain the seven canonical layers of a User Journey Map with a suitable example. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Construct a User Journey Map for a "First-time user booking a KTU B.Tech bus pass via the university mobile app." Identify at least four phases, three pain points, and propose three HMW opportunities. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The seven canonical layers are:

1. **Persona & Scenario** — Defines *who* the user is and *what* they are trying to do. Example: *Ramesh, 19, a 1st-year CS student, wants to book a bus pass to commute daily from Ernakulam to the College campus.*

2. **Phases/Stages** — The high-level sequence of the journey. For a bus pass: *Awareness → Registration → Document Upload → Payment → Pass Issuance*.

3. **Actions/Behaviors** — Discrete verbs the user performs. Example: *Opens app → Taps "New User" → Scans ID card → Enters UPI ID → Confirms payment.*

4. **Touchpoints** — Channels/surfaces. Example: *App UI, phone camera (scanner), SMS gateway, UPI app, in-app notification.*

5. **Thoughts/Mindset** — Internal cognitive state. Example: *"Will my bus pass be valid immediately?"*

6. **Emotions/Sentiment** — Emotional score per phase. Example: *Curious (+1) → Confused (-1) → Anxious (-2) → Relieved (+2) → Delighted (+3).*

7. **Pain Points & Opportunities** — Pain: *"ID scanning fails in low light."* Opportunity (HMW): *"How might we enable manual ID entry as a fallback within 2 seconds?"*

The seven layers collectively transform a task into an **experience narrative**, enabling empathy-driven design.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Scenario:** First-time user, *Anjali*, booking a KTU B.Tech bus pass via the University mobile app on a low-end Android device.

| Phase | Action | Touchpoint | Thought | Emotion ($E_i$) | Pain Point | Opportunity (HMW) |
|-------|--------|-----------|---------|----------------|------------|-------------------|
| **1. Awareness** | Sees campus poster about digital bus pass | Poster + App Store | *"Maybe I should try the app"* | $+1$ | No QR code on poster | HMW add a scannable QR for instant app install |
| **2. Registration** | Enters roll number, DOB | App form | *"Why is this field mandatory?"* | $-1$ | App crashes on Android 9 | HMW auto-detect device and offer lite version |
| **3. Document Upload** | Uploads ID + photo | Camera, file picker | *"Will the photo be clear enough?"* | $-2$ | File size limit too low (500 KB) | HMW auto-compress images client-side |
| **4. Payment** | Pays ₹1500 via UPI | UPI app | *"Hope the confirmation is instant"* | $0$ | UPI redirect drops the user | HMW keep user in-app with payment SDK |
| **5. Pass Issuance** | Receives digital pass | App + Email | *"This is so much better than standing in line!"* | $+3$ | Pass not accepted by some conductors | HMW integrate conductor-side QR scanner |

**Emotional Curve Calculation:**

$$\bar{E} = \frac{1 + (-1) + (-2) + 0 + 3}{5} = \frac{1}{5} = 0.2$$

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{(0.8)^2 + (-1.2)^2 + (-2.2)^2 + (-0.2)^2 + (2.8)^2}{5} = \frac{0.64 + 1.44 + 4.84 + 0.04 + 7.84}{5} = \frac{14.8}{5} = 2.96$$

**Insights:**
- Average sentiment is near-neutral ($0.2$) but variance is high ($\sigma^2 = 2.96$).
- **Critical drop at Phase 3** (Document Upload) signals the most urgent intervention.
- **Peak satisfaction at Phase 5** (Pass Issuance) — reinforce this with conductor-side adoption.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Adapted Alternative]**

**(a)** Differentiate between a **Current State Journey Map**, **Future State Journey Map**, and **Service Blueprint**. When would you use each? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A user is trying to lodge a complaint about a KTU exam result on the University grievance portal but abandons the task midway. Using journey mapping principles, diagnose **three likely pain points** and propose **two design interventions** with justification grounded in journey-mapping theory. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

| Map Type | Purpose | Use Case | Key Feature |
|----------|---------|----------|-------------|
| **Current State** | Documents the *existing* experience | Diagnose problems, identify pain points | Reflects reality |
| **Future State** | Envisions the *ideal* experience post-redesign | Guide ideation, align stakeholders | Reflects aspiration |
| **Service Blueprint** | Extends journey map with *backstage* and *support* layers | Cross-functional service design | Reveals operational dependencies |

**When to use:**
- Use **Current State** first to establish a baseline (typically 2–4 weeks of research).
- Use **Future State** after ideation workshops to align teams on the *to-be* vision.
- Use **Service Blueprint** when the experience depends on multiple teams, backend systems, or human services — common in healthcare, banking, and government.

---

### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Persona:** *Sneha, 4th-year ECE student, attempts to lodge a complaint about an incorrect KTU exam result on a desktop browser.*

**Likely Pain Points:**

1. **Authentication Friction** — The portal requires multiple OTPs and a separate University SSO, increasing effort.
2. **Ambiguous Form Fields** — The complaint form has technical jargon (e.g., "Course code with paper ID") that confuses students.
3. **No Acknowledgment / Status Visibility** — After submission, the user receives no clear confirmation or tracking ID, leading to distrust and abandonment.

**Design Interventions (HMW grounded in theory):**

1. **HMW reduce authentication effort by enabling Aadhaar-linked single sign-on?**
   *Justification:* Aligns with the "Reduce Friction" principle from journey mapping — the first stage of authentication should match the user's mental model of effort.

2. **HMW provide real-time, plain-language field help and a confirmation dashboard with a unique tracking ID?**
   *Justification:* Maps to the "Restore Confidence" opportunity — the post-submission phase is a *moment of truth*; clear status updates reduce anxiety and rebuild trust.

**Empirical Support (hypothetical metrics for illustration):**

If authentication effort drops from $E = -2$ to $E = +1$ and post-submission clarity improves from $E = -1$ to $E = +2$:

$$\bar{E}_{\text{new}} = \frac{1 + 2}{2} = 1.5 \quad \text{(significant improvement from baseline } \bar{E} = -1.5\text{)}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do not confuse "User Flow" with "User Journey Map"** in your answer — this is a 2–3 mark deduction.
> 2. **Do not invent emotions** — every emotion must be tied to a research method (interview, survey, observation).
> 3. **Always close the journey map with HMWs** — a journey map without opportunities is considered a *descriptive dead-end* and loses marks.
> 4. **Quantify where possible** — KTU examiners reward numerical reasoning (emotion scores, variance, drop-off rates).
> 5. **Stick to the persona** — never generalize "users"; journey maps are *persona-specific*.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **User Journey Map = Experience Narrative** across time, layered with persona, actions, touchpoints, thoughts, emotions, pain points, and opportunities.
- ✅ **Seven canonical layers** — memorize the order: *Persona → Phases → Actions → Touchpoints → Thoughts → Emotions → Pain & Opportunity.*
- ✅ **Three primary types** — Current State, Future State, Day-in-the-Life. (Service Blueprint is an *extension*, not a substitute.)
- ✅ **Emotional curve** is plotted as a line graph; **variance** signals emotional volatility and design urgency.
- ✅ **HMWs (How Might We)** convert journey maps from *descriptive* to *prescriptive* — never skip them.
- ✅ **Key metrics:** CES, NPS, CSAT, Drop-off Rate, TSAT, Completion Rate.
- ✅ **Difference from User Flow:** Journey Map = experience; User Flow = task/system.
- ✅ **Always persona-bound** — never use generic "user" language.
- ✅ **Research-grounded** — every claim must trace back to a method (interview, observation, analytics).
- ✅ **Valuable for:** SaaS, healthcare, fintech, e-governance, IoT — multi-disciplinary applicability.

> **End of Notes — PECPT744 Module 2 — User Journey Mapping**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Wireframing and prototyping
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Wireframing and prototyping

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wireframing** is the process of creating a **two-dimensional skeletal outline** of a digital product's interface, focused on layout, information hierarchy, functionality, and user flow, **without** incorporating visual design elements such as color, typography, or imagery. It functions as the **low-fidelity blueprint** of the user interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Prototyping** is the process of building an **interactive, simulated model** of a product that allows designers and stakeholders to test user interactions, navigation flows, and behavioral responses before any code is written. It is a **mid-to-high fidelity functional representation** of the final product.

These two practices together form the **Design Translation Layer** of the UX Design Thinking process — converting abstract user research insights and information architecture into **tangible, testable artifacts**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Building a House Analogy** 🏠
>
> Imagine you are an architect designing a house for a client.
>
> - **Wireframe** = The **blueprint drawing** on paper. It shows the position of walls, doors, windows, rooms, and how someone will move from the kitchen to the bedroom. It is **black and white**, has no paint colors, no furniture, no decorations. Its only job is to communicate **structure and flow**.
>
> - **Prototype** = The **3D scale model** of the house. The client can actually open the door, walk through the hallway, switch on the lights (simulated), and experience the spaces. It looks much closer to the real thing, and the client can **interact with it**.
>
> - The **final coded product** = The actual constructed house.
>
> You cannot build a house without a blueprint, and you cannot confidently start construction without a model. **Wireframing and prototyping play these exact roles in UX Design.**

---

### 1.3 Key Distinctions at a Glance

| Aspect | Wireframe | Prototype |
|---|---|---|
| **Fidelity** | Low fidelity (Lo-Fi) | Mid to High fidelity (Mi-Fi / Hi-Fi) |
| **Visual Polish** | None / Minimal | Realistic, branded |
| **Interactivity** | Static (mostly) | Clickable, interactive |
| **Primary Purpose** | Structure & layout | Behavior & experience testing |
| **Audience** | Designers, stakeholders early on | Stakeholders, users, developers |
| **Time Investment** | Hours to a few days | Days to weeks |
| **Anchors** | Information architecture | User flow validation |

> [!TIP]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU examiners specifically test the **difference between low-fidelity and high-fidelity wireframes**, and the **transition from static design to interactive prototype**. Always mention **fidelity** in your answers.

---

### 1.4 Standard Fidelity Levels (Bold Emphasis on Constants)

The standard fidelity spectrum used across the UX industry and KTU 2024 evaluations:

- **Low-Fidelity (Lo-Fi):** Sketches, paper wireframes, grayscale blocks. Focus: **Layout & content placement.**
- **Mid-Fidelity (Mi-Fi):** Digital wireframes with real content, basic typography sizing, grayscale boxes for images. Focus: **Information hierarchy & flow.**
- **High-Fidelity (Hi-Fi):** Pixel-accurate, fully styled, interactive prototypes with real data and micro-interactions. Focus: **Usability testing & stakeholder approval.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

### 2.1 The Wireframing Process — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]
> Wireframing is **not random drawing**. It follows a **structured 6-step methodology** that KTU examiners love to test.

1. **Define the Scope:** Identify which screen or flow is being wireframed (e.g., a login screen, a checkout page).
2. **Gather Requirements:** Pull from user research, personas, and use cases established in earlier modules.
3. **Map the User Flow:** Sequence the screens logically so the wireframe fits into a larger journey.
4. **Sketch the Layout (Box Model):** Apply the **Rule of Thirds** and **F-Pattern / Z-Pattern** reading principles to position elements.
5. **Add Content & Annotations:** Insert placeholder text (Lorem Ipsum), labels, and designer notes explaining interactive elements.
6. **Review & Iterate:** Validate with stakeholders and prepare for prototyping.

**Why these steps matter:** Each step reduces ambiguity. Skipping scope definition leads to redesigns costing **3x–5x** the original effort.

---

### 2.2 Types of Wireframes

| Type | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Paper Wireframe** | Hand-drawn sketches on paper or whiteboard | Early ideation, team brainstorming |
| **Digital Low-Fi** | Created in tools like Balsamiq or simple shapes in Figma | Stakeholder review of structure |
| **Digital Mid-Fi** | Includes real content, accurate spacing, type sizes | User testing of layout |
| **Annotated Wireframe** | Wireframe with callout notes explaining interactions | Handoff to developers |

---

### 2.3 Prototyping Methodologies

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept:** Prototypes differ by **dimensionality** and **interactivity level**.

#### 2.3.1 By Dimensionality
- **2D Prototypes:** Flat screens connected by flow arrows (e.g., clickable PDFs, InVision).
- **3D Prototypes:** Spatial simulations (e.g., VR walkthroughs, AR overlays).

#### 2.3.2 By Interactivity / Fidelity
- **Low-Fidelity Prototypes:** Paper prototypes, click-through PDF flows. Fast to build, ideal for **early-stage concept validation**.
- **High-Fidelity Prototypes:** Fully interactive digital simulations in Figma, Adobe XD, Axure, or Proto.io. Used for **realistic user testing and stakeholder sign-off**.

#### 2.3.3 By Technical Approach
- **Throwaway / Rapid Prototypes:** Built to learn, then discarded.
- **Evolutionary Prototypes:** Iteratively refined into the final product.
- **Incremental Prototypes:** Multiple prototypes developed in parallel for different modules.
- **Extreme Prototypes:** Used in web development — combines HTML prototypes with simulated back-end functionality.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Term** | **Definition** | **KTU Significance** |
|---|---|---|
| **Fidelity** | Level of detail and functional accuracy in a design artifact | Most repeated term in exams |
| **Wireframe** | Static structural blueprint of a UI | Foundation of every design |
| **Prototype** | Interactive simulation of a product | Used for usability testing |
| **Mockup** | Static high-fidelity visual design (no interactivity) | Often confused with Hi-Fi prototype |
| **Information Architecture** | Structural design of content and navigation | Pre-requisite to wireframing |
| **User Flow** | Sequence of steps a user takes to complete a task | Guides prototype interaction design |
| **Annotation** | Designer notes explaining wireframe behavior | Critical for developer handoff |
| **Micro-interaction** | Small, contained animation within a UI (e.g., button hover) | Tested only in Hi-Fi prototypes |
| **Heuristic Evaluation** | Expert review using Nielsen's 10 usability principles | Conducted on prototypes |
| **Usability Testing** | Real users attempting tasks on a prototype | Final validation stage |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industry, wireframes and prototypes are the **contractual bridge** between design intent and engineering implementation. They:
- Reduce **development rework** by catching UX issues before code is written.
- Serve as **legal artifacts** in design system governance.
- Enable **A/B testing** in product analytics workflows.
- Act as **documentation** for QA teams building test plans.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Note:** Companies like Google, Airbnb, and Flipkart run **continuous prototyping sprints** where prototypes are tested weekly with real users before any code is committed.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Process Analysis & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> Since this topic is **design-oriented** rather than mathematical, the "derivation" below is a **methodological breakdown** — tracing the conceptual transition from raw concept to validated prototype. This is exactly how KTU examiners expect "process" questions to be answered.

---

### 3.1 Analytical Matrix: From Concept to Prototype

| **Stage** | **Artifact** | **Key Activity** | **Output** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1. Research** | User interviews, surveys, analytics | Understand user needs | Personas, user stories |
| **2. Information Architecture** | Sitemap, card sorting results | Organize content | Navigation hierarchy |
| **3. User Flow Mapping** | Flowchart of task completion | Sequence user actions | Step-by-step flow diagram |
| **4. Low-Fi Wireframe** | Sketches, basic boxes | Define layout & structure | Static structural blueprint |
| **5. Mid-Fi Wireframe** | Grayscale digital wireframe | Refine spacing & content | Refined structural blueprint |
| **6. High-Fi Wireframe / Mockup** | Pixel-perfect static design | Apply visual design | Visual design specification |
| **7. Low-Fi Prototype** | Click-through of static screens | Validate flow logic | Working flow model |
| **8. High-Fi Prototype** | Fully interactive simulation | Usability testing | Validated, near-final product simulation |
| **9. Handoff** | Specs, assets, prototype link | Developer implementation | Production code |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction Logic (How a Prototype is "Derived" from a Wireframe)

**Step 1: Selection of the Source Wireframe**
The designer selects a **mid-fidelity wireframe** as the base. The reasoning: it contains real content proportions but no distracting visual styling, allowing the designer's focus to be on **interaction logic**.

**Step 2: Identification of Interactive Zones**
The designer annotates every element that should respond to user input — buttons, links, form fields, dropdowns, swipeable carousels. This annotation is a **mapping step**: it defines the **input vocabulary** of the prototype.

**Step 3: Definition of Interaction Triggers**
Each interactive zone is assigned a **trigger event** — click, hover, tap, swipe, scroll, keyboard input. This mirrors the **event-driven model** used in software engineering.

**Step 4: Linking to Target Screens**
Each trigger is linked to the next screen in the user flow. This forms a **directed graph** of screens, where nodes are screens and edges are user actions.

**Step 5: Simulation of Micro-Interactions**
Transitions, hover states, loading spinners, and error messages are added. These simulate the **dynamic behavior** of the final application.

**Step 6: Validation via Usability Testing**
The prototype is exposed to **real users** who perform **representative tasks**. Success metrics: **task completion rate, time-on-task, error rate, and SUS (System Usability Scale) score**.

**Step 7: Iteration and Handoff**
Based on usability findings, the prototype is refined, and the validated version becomes the **blueprint for development**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Framework — Choosing the Right Fidelity

A critical decision-making model for KTU questions:

$$
\text{Fidelity} = f(\text{Stage of Design}, \text{Audience}, \text{Testing Goal})
$$

Where:
- **Stage of Design** → Early stage = Low fidelity; Late stage = High fidelity
- **Audience** → Internal team = Low fidelity; Client/Executive = High fidelity
- **Testing Goal** → Concept validation = Low fidelity; Behavioral validation = High fidelity

This formula (conceptual) is often tested as: *"When would you use a low-fidelity prototype over a high-fidelity one?"* — Answer: **When you need to validate concepts cheaply and quickly, without being distracted by visual polish.**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Wireframing-to-Prototyping Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Research and Personas] --> B[Information Architecture]
    B --> C[User Flow Mapping]
    C --> D[Low Fidelity Wireframe]
    D --> E[Mid Fidelity Wireframe]
    E --> F[High Fidelity Mockup]
    F --> G[Interactive Prototype]
    G --> H[Usability Testing]
    H --> I{Feedback Loop}
    I -->|Issues Found| D
    I -->|Approved| J[Developer Handoff]
    J --> K[Final Product]

    style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style D fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style E fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style F fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style G fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style H fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c
    style K fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

---

### 4.2 The Fidelity Spectrum

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Paper Sketch] --> L[Low Fi Wireframe]
    L --> M[Mid Fi Wireframe]
    M --> HM[High Fi Mockup]
    HM --> LP[Low Fi Prototype]
    LP --> HP[High Fi Prototype]
    HP --> CODE[Production Code]

    style P fill:#f5f5f5,stroke:#616161
    style L fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0
    style M fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1
    style HM fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
    style LP fill:#ffccbc,stroke:#bf360c
    style HP fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style CODE fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#004d40
```

---

### 4.3 Prototyping Methodology Map (Block-Level Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Dimensional[BY DIMENSIONALITY]
        D2[2D Prototypes]
        D3[3D Prototypes]
    end

    subgraph Fidelity[BY FIDELITY]
        LoF[Low Fi Prototype]
        HiF[High Fi Prototype]
    end

    subgraph Approach[BY APPROACH]
        Thr[Throwaway]
        Evo[Evolutionary]
        Inc[Incremental]
        Ext[Extreme]
    end

    subgraph Tools[BY TOOLS]
        Paper[Paper and Pen]
        Digital[Digital Tools]
        Code[Code Based]
    end

    Dimensional --> Fidelity
    Fidelity --> Approach
    Approach --> Tools

    style Dimensional fill:#e1f5fe,stroke:#01579b
    style Fidelity fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100
    style Approach fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c
    style Tools fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20
```

---

### 4.4 Wireframe Annotations Example Block

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Screen[Login Screen Wireframe]
        H[Header Logo Block]
        T[Title Text Welcome Back]
        E1[Email Input Field]
        E2[Password Input Field]
        B1[Login Button]
        B2[Forgot Password Link]
        F[Footer Sign Up Prompt]
    end

    H --> T
    T --> E1
    E1 --> E2
    E2 --> B1
    B2 --> F

    style Screen fill:#fafafa,stroke:#424242
    style B1 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20
    style B2 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above block diagram represents a **functional architecture of a wireframe**, showing how UI elements are arranged in a vertical hierarchy — a common layout pattern taught in KTU Module 2.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define wireframing. List any four characteristics of a low-fidelity wireframe.**

**Model Answer:**
Wireframing is the process of creating a simplified, two-dimensional skeletal representation of a digital product's interface. It focuses on layout, structure, content placement, and user flow without including visual design elements such as color, typography, or imagery.

**Four characteristics of a low-fidelity wireframe:**
1. Rendered in **black and white or grayscale** — no color or branding applied.
2. Uses **placeholder content** such as Lorem Ipsum text and gray image blocks.
3. Created **quickly** using pen-and-paper or simple digital tools like Balsamiq.
4. Focuses on **structure and layout** rather than visual aesthetics, enabling rapid iteration.

*(Examiner's Note: Marks awarded for the definition (1) + each valid characteristic (½ × 4 = 2).)*

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a wireframe, a mockup, and a prototype.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Wireframe | Mockup | Prototype |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fidelity** | Low to Mid | High (visual only) | Mid to High |
| **Interactivity** | None | None | Fully interactive |
| **Purpose** | Layout & structure | Visual appearance | Behavior & user testing |
| **Tools** | Balsamiq, paper | Photoshop, Figma | Figma, Adobe XD, Axure |
| **Stakeholder Stage** | Early reviews | Design sign-off | Usability testing |

A **wireframe** shows **what goes where**. A **mockup** shows **how it looks**. A **prototype** shows **how it works**.

---

### 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **step-by-step process of wireframing** with a suitable example of an e-commerce checkout screen. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **different types of prototypes** based on fidelity and technical approach. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a):**

The wireframing process for an e-commerce checkout screen consists of six sequential steps:

1. **Define the Scope:** The goal is to wireframe the checkout screen where users enter shipping details, select payment mode, and confirm the order.

2. **Gather Requirements:** From earlier user research, we know users need (a) order summary, (b) shipping address form, (c) payment options (UPI, Card, COD), and (d) a "Place Order" CTA.

3. **Map the User Flow:** The checkout screen sits between the Cart screen (previous) and the Order Confirmation screen (next).

4. **Sketch the Layout:**
   - **Top:** Page header with logo and secure-checkout indicator.
   - **Middle (Two-column layout):** Left column = Shipping & Payment forms; Right column = Sticky Order Summary.
   - **Bottom:** "Place Order" button (full-width on mobile).

5. **Add Content & Annotations:** Use real field labels ("Full Name," "PIN Code"), placeholder values ("123456"), and annotations such as *"On click → opens payment selector"* and *"If PIN is invalid → show inline error."*

6. **Review & Iterate:** Validate with the product team, then move to mid-fidelity for stakeholder review.

> [!TIP]
> **Pro Tip:** Mentioning **Z-pattern reading** and **mobile-first responsive considerations** earns extra marks.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Prototypes are classified on two axes:**

**By Fidelity:**
- **Low-Fidelity Prototypes** — Paper prototypes, click-through PDFs, simple Figma frames linked by hotspots. Used for **early concept testing** and **internal team reviews**. Pros: Fast, cheap, encourages feedback. Cons: Limited realism.
- **High-Fidelity Prototypes** — Pixel-accurate, interactive simulations built in Figma, Adobe XD, or Proto.io. Include animations, conditional logic, and real content. Used for **usability testing, stakeholder approval, and investor demos**. Pros: Realistic user behavior data. Cons: Time-intensive.

**By Technical Approach:**
- **Throwaway Prototypes** — Built quickly to answer specific design questions, then discarded. Common in exploratory UX research.
- **Evolutionary Prototypes** — Continuously refined until they become the foundation of the production system.
- **Incremental Prototypes** — Multiple prototypes developed independently for separate modules, then integrated.
- **Extreme Prototypes** — Used in web development, combining HTML/CSS front-end prototypes with a simulated back-end.

**Conclusion:** The choice of prototype type depends on the **stage of the design process, the audience, and the validation goal**.

---

#### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]` *(Internal Choice Alternative)*

**(a)** Describe the **transition from wireframe to high-fidelity prototype**, listing the key activities at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **role of prototyping in usability testing** with reference to **heuristic evaluation** and the **System Usability Scale (SUS)**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(a):**

The transition from wireframe to high-fidelity prototype follows a **5-stage evolution**:

1. **Low-Fi Wireframe → Mid-Fi Wireframe:** Replace placeholder text with real content, refine spacing using a **4px or 8px grid system**, define actual font sizes (e.g., H1 = 24px, Body = 16px), and introduce grayscale for visual hierarchy.

2. **Mid-Fi Wireframe → Hi-Fi Mockup:** Apply brand colors, typography, iconography, imagery, and spacing. This is now a **static visual design** — a pixel-perfect representation of the final product.

3. **Hi-Fi Mockup → Lo-Fi Prototype:** Identify interactive zones and link screens using **hotspots** in tools like Figma. At this stage, only basic click-through is functional.

4. **Lo-Fi Prototype → Hi-Fi Prototype:** Add **micro-interactions** (button hover states, page transitions, form validation feedback), **conditional logic** (if-else flows), and **dynamic content** (variable data, animations).

5. **Hi-Fi Prototype → Developer Handoff:** Export specs, generate CSS code snippets, document interaction patterns, and share the prototype URL for QA testing.

> [!TIP]
> Examiners reward answers that mention **specific tool names** (Figma, Adobe XD) and **design system tokens** (color variables, spacing scales).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Role of Prototyping in Usability Testing:**

A high-fidelity prototype is a **testable artifact** that allows UX researchers to measure how real users interact with a product before development.

**1. Heuristic Evaluation:**
Conducted by UX experts using **Nielsen's 10 Usability Heuristics** (e.g., visibility of system status, match between system and real world, user control and freedom, consistency and standards). The prototype is reviewed to identify usability violations **without needing real users**, providing a **quick expert-driven audit**.

**2. System Usability Scale (SUS):**
A standardized **10-item questionnaire** with 5-point Likert scales, developed by John Brooke in 1996. After using the prototype, real users rate statements like *"I thought the system was easy to use."* The scores yield a single SUS value between **0 and 100**:
- **> 80** = Excellent
- **68–80** = Good
- **< 68** = Needs Improvement

**3. Task-Based Testing:**
Users are given representative tasks (e.g., *"Complete the checkout process"*). Metrics measured:
- **Task Completion Rate** (success %)
- **Time on Task** (efficiency)
- **Error Rate** (mistakes per task)
- **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** for satisfaction

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often **confuse mockups with prototypes** or **omit fidelity classification** in their answers. KTU examiners specifically deduct **1–2 marks** when a student uses the terms "wireframe" and "prototype" interchangeably. **Always state the fidelity level** (Lo-Fi, Mid-Fi, Hi-Fi) explicitly.

---

### 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Wireframe = static structure**; **Prototype = interactive behavior**.
- ✅ Always specify **fidelity level** — Lo-Fi, Mid-Fi, or Hi-Fi.
- ✅ **Paper wireframes** are valid Lo-Fi artifacts; examiners accept them as examples.
- ✅ **Annotations** on wireframes are critical for developer handoff.
- ✅ The wireframing process follows: **Scope → Requirements → User Flow → Sketch → Annotate → Review**.
- ✅ Prototype types: **Throwaway, Evolutionary, Incremental, Extreme** — know the differences.
- ✅ Tools: **Figma, Adobe XD, Balsamiq, Axure, Sketch, Proto.io, InVision**.
- ✅ **Heuristic Evaluation** = Expert review using Nielsen's 10 principles.
- ✅ **SUS (System Usability Scale)** = 10-item questionnaire yielding a score of 0–100; >80 is excellent.
- ✅ High-fidelity prototypes enable **real user testing** with measurable metrics (task success, time-on-task, error rate).
- ✅ Mention **mobile-first** and **responsive design** considerations for full marks in design questions.
- ✅ Always use a **grid system (4px/8px)** when describing wireframe-to-mockup transitions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: CPS-specific UX Design Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** CPS-specific UX Design Considerations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS)** are engineered systems that are built from, and depend upon, the seamless integration of computation, networking, and physical processes. Embedded computers and networks monitor and control the physical processes, usually with feedback loops where physical processes affect computations and vice versa.

**CPS-specific UX Design Considerations** refer to the specialized set of usability, interaction, and experience design principles that must be applied when designing user experiences for Cyber-Physical Systems. These considerations extend beyond traditional software UX by accounting for real-time physical interactions, safety-critical operations, sensor-actuator feedback loops, environmental context, and the tight coupling between digital intelligence and physical outcomes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** CPS UX is the practice of designing interactions for systems where computational logic directly influences the physical world, and physical phenomena directly inform computational decisions — necessitating UX principles that account for **safety, real-time responsiveness, context-awareness, and trust calibration**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Autonomous Car Cockpit

Imagine a **conventional car dashboard** versus an **autonomous vehicle's HMI (Human-Machine Interface)**.

In a conventional car, the driver's UX is direct: turn the steering wheel, the wheels turn. The mental model is simple, and the consequences of UX failure are usually recoverable.

In an **autonomous vehicle**, the user no longer directly manipulates the physical system. Instead, they communicate with a CPS that interprets intent (voice, gesture, touch), consults sensors (LiDAR, radar, cameras), and acts on the physical world. A single UX flaw — say, an unclear handover request during a traffic merge — can have **fatal consequences**.

> **The key insight:** Traditional UX fails *softly* (a button does the wrong thing). CPS UX can fail *catastrophically* (a misinterpreted command causes a robotic arm to injure a worker).

## 1.3 Core Distinguishing Metrics of CPS UX

The following quantitative and qualitative metrics are unique to CPS interaction design:

- **Time-to-Engage (TtE):** Time between user initiation and system acknowledgment (must be < **200 ms** for safety-critical tasks).
- **Mode Awareness Index (MAI):** User's understanding of the system's current operational state (target ≥ **0.85** on 0–1 scale).
- **Trust Calibration Error (TCE):** Discrepancy between user's trust level and system's actual reliability.
- **Latency Tolerance (LT):** Maximum acceptable delay before user perceives the system as broken (typically **1500 ms** for non-critical, **300 ms** for safety-critical).
- **Physical Safety Boundary (PSB):** Spatial envelope within which the system guarantees no harm to the user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters for KTU exams:** Examiners expect students to clearly distinguish CPS UX from traditional software UX. A common pitfall is treating CPS as "just an app on a device" — KTU evaluators deduct marks when the physical-world consequences are not addressed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Pillars of CPS UX Design

CPS UX is structured around seven interrelated design pillars. Each pillar addresses a specific failure mode inherent in coupling computation with physical processes.

### Pillar 1: Safety-Critical Design
CPS operates in environments where UX failure translates to **physical harm**. The design must incorporate:
- Fail-safe states (graceful degradation)
- Emergency stop affordances
- Predictable error recovery
- Redundant feedback channels

### Pillar 2: Real-Time Responsiveness
Unlike traditional apps where a 2-second delay is annoying, CPS delays can be lethal. The UX must convey system status **within perceptual thresholds** (under 100ms for haptics, under 250ms for visual feedback).

### Pillar 3: Context-Awareness
CPS must interpret:
- **User context** (intent, expertise, stress level)
- **Environmental context** (lighting, noise, location)
- **System context** (battery, sensor health, network)

### Pillar 4: Multi-Modal Interaction
Users cannot rely on a single input channel when hands are occupied, eyes are busy, or the environment is hostile. CPS UX must support:
- **Touch** (capacitive, haptic)
- **Voice** (natural language, noise-robust)
- **Gesture** (vision-based, radar-based)
- **Gaze** (eye-tracking)
- **Haptic feedback** (vibration, force)

### Pillar 5: Trust Calibration
A user who **over-trusts** an autonomous system may neglect supervision; a user who **under-trusts** may disable useful features. The UX must continuously communicate system confidence and limitations.

### Pillar 6: Privacy & Security
CPS collects intimate data (location, biometrics, health). UX must make privacy trade-offs visible and give users meaningful control.

### Pillar 7: Physical-Digital Continuity
The mental model must seamlessly bridge the virtual representation and the physical reality. Inconsistencies between digital display and physical state destroy trust.

## 2.2 The CPS UX Interaction Loop

CPS interaction follows a continuous loop, unlike the discrete user-action/response of traditional software:

1. **User Intent Formation** → 2. **Input Capture** → 3. **Sensor Fusion** → 4. **System Reasoning** → 5. **Actuation** → 6. **Physical World Response** → 7. **Feedback to User** → (loop back to 1)

Each transition has its own UX requirements, and **failures at any node cascade**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the high-yield formula sheet for solving numerical and analytical problems on CPS UX in KTU 2024 ESE.

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Application Context | Units / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = a + b \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{W} + 1\right)$ | Fitts' Law | Time to acquire a target in CPS interface | $T$ in ms, $D$ distance, $W$ width |
| 2 | $H = - \sum p_i \log_2 p_i$ | Shannon Entropy (Hick's Law basis) | Information content of CPS alerts | $H$ in bits |
| 3 | $T_{react} = c + d \cdot H$ | Hick-Hyman Law | Reaction time to CPS system prompts | $T_{react}$ in ms |
| 4 | $TCE = \vert T_{user} - T_{system} \vert$ | Trust Calibration Error | Mismatch between user trust and system reliability | $0$ to $1$ (lower is better) |
| 5 | $MAI = \frac{N_{correct}}{N_{total}}$ | Mode Awareness Index | Proportion of correctly identified system states | $0$ to $1$ (target $\geq 0.85$) |
| 6 | $TtE = t_{ack} - t_{init}$ | Time-to-Engage | Latency of system acknowledgment | ms, safety-critical $< 200$ ms |
| 7 | $W_{critical} = S \times P \times R$ | Risk Weighting | Severity $\times$ Probability $\times$ Recoverability | Risk-priority score |
| 8 | $U_{CPS} = \alpha E + \beta S + \gamma C + \delta T$ | Composite UX Score | Effectiveness, Safety, Comfort, Trust | Normalized $0$ to $1$ |
| 9 | $L_{total} = L_{input} + L_{process} + L_{output}$ | End-to-End Latency | Total CPS response delay | ms, target $< 300$ ms safety-critical |
| 10 | $P_{fail} = 1 - \prod (1 - p_i)$ | Series Failure Probability | Cascading CPS component failure | $0$ to $1$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulas are used in automotive HMI design (ISO 26262), medical device UX (IEC 62366), and industrial robotics safety standards. KTU examiners expect students to apply them to scenario-based questions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Application

In the **automotive industry**, CPS UX principles govern the design of ADAS (Advanced Driver Assistance Systems). The combination of Fitts' Law, Hick's Law, and Trust Calibration Error is used to design lane-change warning systems where the driver has approximately **2.5 seconds** to perceive, interpret, and act. Similarly, **surgical robotics** (e.g., da Vinci systems) applies CPS UX to ensure that surgeon intent is translated to physical actuation with sub-millimeter precision while maintaining clear visual, auditory, and haptic feedback channels.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Composite CPS UX Score ($U_{CPS}$)

The **Composite CPS UX Score** is a weighted aggregate that quantitatively expresses the holistic quality of a CPS interaction. Let us derive it from first principles.

**Step 1 — Define the four foundational quality dimensions:**

- **Effectiveness (E):** The accuracy and completeness with which users achieve specified goals in the CPS environment.
- **Safety (S):** The system's ability to operate without causing physical harm to users or the environment.
- **Comfort (C):** The subjective experience of physical and cognitive ease during interaction.
- **Trust (T):** The calibrated alignment between user confidence and system reliability.

**Step 2 — Normalize each dimension to the interval $[0, 1]$:**

$$ E = \frac{N_{success}}{N_{total}}, \quad 0 \leq E \leq 1 $$

$$ S = 1 - P_{harm}, \quad \text{where } P_{harm} \text{ is probability of physical harm} $$

$$ C = 1 - \frac{L_{reported}}{L_{max}}, \quad L_{reported} \in [0, L_{max}] $$

$$ T = 1 - TCE = 1 - \vert T_{user} - T_{system} \vert $$

**Step 3 — Apply domain-specific weights $\alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta$:**

These weights must satisfy the constraint of a probability simplex:

$$ \alpha + \beta + \gamma + \delta = 1, \quad \alpha, \beta, \gamma, \delta \geq 0 $$

**Step 4 — Compose the weighted aggregate:**

$$ U_{CPS} = \alpha E + \beta S + \gamma C + \delta T $$

**Step 5 — Apply KTU Worked Example:**

A medical infusion pump CPS has the following measured parameters:

$$ E = 0.95, \quad S = 0.99, \quad C = 0.80, \quad T = 0.88 $$

The domain weights for medical CPS (safety-dominant) are:

$$ \alpha = 0.15, \quad \beta = 0.55, \quad \gamma = 0.10, \quad \delta = 0.20 $$

**Step 6 — Verify weight constraint:**

$$ 0.15 + 0.55 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 7 — Compute the score:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPS} &= (0.15)(0.95) + (0.55)(0.99) + (0.10)(0.80) + (0.20)(0.88) \\
U_{CPS} &= 0.1425 + 0.5445 + 0.0800 + 0.1760 \\
U_{CPS} &= 0.9430
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Interpret the result:**

A score of **0.9430** indicates an excellent CPS UX, primarily driven by high safety (the largest weighted component). KTU expects you to comment on the dominance pattern — here, safety contributes **57.7%** of the total score, justifying the high weight.

## 3.2 Derivation: Trust Calibration Error (TCE) under System Performance Variation

The **Trust Calibration Error** measures how aligned the user's trust is with the system's actual capability.

**Step 1 — Define user trust at time $t$:**

User trust evolves dynamically based on observed system performance:

$$ T_{user}(t) = T_0 + \alpha \int_0^t R(\tau) \, d\tau - \beta \int_0^t E(\tau) \, d\tau $$

Where:
- $T_0$ = initial user trust
- $R(\tau)$ = observed reliability at time $\tau$
- $E(\tau)$ = observed error at time $\tau$
- $\alpha$ = trust accumulation rate
- $\beta$ = trust decay rate on error

**Step 2 — Define system trust (objective reliability):**

$$ T_{system}(t) = \mathbb{E}[\text{success rate at time } t] $$

**Step 3 — Compute TCE at any time $t$:**

$$ TCE(t) = \vert T_{user}(t) - T_{system}(t) \vert $$

**Step 4 — Worked Example — KTU Style:**

A drone navigation CPS is tested. User begins with $T_0 = 0.50$. Over 5 trials, reliability observations are $R = \{0.9, 0.8, 1.0, 0.7, 0.9\}$ and errors are $E = \{0.1, 0.2, 0.0, 0.3, 0.1\}$. Assume $\alpha = 0.3, \beta = 0.5$.

**Step 5 — Compute cumulative reliability and error:**

$$ \int_0^5 R(\tau) \, d\tau = 0.9 + 0.8 + 1.0 + 0.7 + 0.9 = 4.3 $$

$$ \int_0^5 E(\tau) \, d\tau = 0.1 + 0.2 + 0.0 + 0.3 + 0.1 = 0.7 $$

**Step 6 — Compute user trust at $t = 5$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{user}(5) &= 0.50 + (0.3)(4.3) - (0.5)(0.7) \\
T_{user}(5) &= 0.50 + 1.29 - 0.35 \\
T_{user}(5) &= 1.44
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Clamp to [0, 1] (trust is bounded):**

$$ T_{user}(5) = \min(1.44, 1.00) = 1.00 $$

**Step 8 — Compute system trust (average reliability):**

$$ T_{system} = \frac{4.3}{5} = 0.86 $$

**Step 9 — Compute TCE:**

$$ TCE(5) = \vert 1.00 - 0.86 \vert = 0.14 $$

**Step 10 — Interpret:**

A TCE of **0.14** indicates the user has slightly **over-trusted** the system (trust 1.00 vs. actual 0.86). The CPS UX design should expose reliability more transparently to reduce this gap.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: CPS UX Failures by Pillar

| Pillar | Common Failure Mode | User Impact | Design Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Safety | Silent emergency stop | User unaware of hazard | Audible + visual + haptic alert triad |
| Real-Time | > 1s system lag | User assumes failure, takes unsafe action | Loading states with confidence intervals |
| Context-Awareness | Misinterprets environment | Wrong action taken | Sensor-fusion cross-validation |
| Multi-Modal | Voice misheard in noise | Wrong command executed | Confirm-back protocol |
| Trust Calibration | Over-trust | User disengages supervision | Visible reliability indicators |
| Privacy | Unclear data usage | User disables features | Transparent consent flow |
| Physical-Digital Continuity | Digital state ≠ physical state | User acts on wrong assumption | Live video/AR overlay of state |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 CPS UX Design Framework — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following diagram maps the **seven pillars of CPS UX** as a functional architecture. Each block represents a design consideration, and the arrows represent information flow and constraint propagation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[User Intent] --> B[Input Capture Layer]
    B --> C[Sensor Fusion Engine]
    C --> D[System Reasoning Core]
    D --> E[Actuation Layer]
    E --> F[Physical World Response]
    F --> G[Feedback to User]
    G --> A

    H[Safety Critical Design] -.-> B
    H -.-> D
    H -.-> E

    I[Real Time Responsiveness] -.-> C
    I -.-> D
    I -.-> G

    J[Context Awareness] -.-> C
    J -.-> D

    K[Multi Modal Interaction] -.-> B
    K -.-> G

    L[Trust Calibration] -.-> G
    L -.-> D

    M[Privacy and Security] -.-> C
    M -.-> B

    N[Physical Digital Continuity] -.-> F
    N -.-> G

    classDef pillar fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef loop fill:#5f1f3a,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class H,I,J,K,L,M,N pillar
    class A,B,C,D,E,F,G loop
```

**Interpretation:** The outer loop (red) is the **continuous CPS interaction cycle**. The seven pillars (blue) are **cross-cutting concerns** that constrain the design of each loop node. For example, *Safety* influences Input Capture, System Reasoning, and Actuation; *Privacy* influences Sensor Fusion and Input Capture.

## 4.2 CPS UX Risk Assessment Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Low Severity Low Probability] --> Q1[Accept Risk]
    P2[Low Severity High Probability] --> Q2[Monitor and Design Safeguards]
    P3[High Severity Low Probability] --> Q3[Redundant Safeguards]
    P4[High Severity High Probability] --> Q4[Redesign or Reject]

    classDef low fill:#2d6a4f,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef med fill:#f4a261,stroke:#fff,color:#000
    classDef high fill:#9d0208,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class Q1 low
    class Q2 med
    class Q3,P3 high
    class Q4 high
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric with letter prefixes (`P1`, `Q1`, etc.). Labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.3 CPS UX Design Lifecycle (Sequential View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[1 Contextual Inquiry] --> A2[2 Task Modeling]
    A2 --> A3[3 Risk Hazard Analysis]
    A3 --> A4[4 Interaction Prototyping]
    A4 --> A5[5 Safety Validation]
    A5 --> A6[6 Field Deployment]
    A6 --> A7[7 Continuous Monitoring]
    A7 -.feedback loop.-> A1

    classDef phase fill:#264653,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    class A1,A2,A3,A4,A5,A6,A7 phase
```

The **feedback loop** from step 7 back to step 1 is critical in CPS UX — unlike traditional software where a "ship and forget" mentality is common, CPS must continuously evolve based on real-world incident data.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Q: Define Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) and explain how UX design for CPS differs from traditional software UX. List any three distinguishing considerations.**

**Model Answer:**

Cyber-Physical Systems (CPS) are integrated systems combining computation, networking, and physical processes with feedback loops between cyber and physical components.

**Three distinguishing UX considerations for CPS:**

1. **Safety-criticality:** CPS UX failures can cause physical harm, unlike software UX failures that typically cause only data or productivity loss. The design must prioritize fail-safe states.

2. **Real-time responsiveness:** CPS interactions must occur within strict perceptual and safety thresholds (e.g., < 200 ms for safety-critical acknowledgment), far tighter than typical software response expectations.

3. **Physical-digital continuity:** CPS UX must ensure that the digital interface representation is consistent with the physical state, so users can build accurate mental models of the actual physical world.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often write generic UX answers ("good usability, easy to learn") without addressing the **physical-world consequence** dimension. This is a 3-mark question; you must list **three CPS-specific** considerations, not generic HCI principles.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Q: What is Trust Calibration Error (TCE) in CPS UX? Why is it important to minimize TCE in safety-critical systems?**

**Model Answer:**

Trust Calibration Error (TCE) is the absolute difference between a user's subjective trust in a CPS and the system's actual reliability.

$$ TCE = \vert T_{user} - T_{system} \vert $$

**Importance of minimizing TCE:**

A high TCE indicates either **over-trust** (user relies on a system that is unreliable, leading to disengagement from supervision) or **under-trust** (user disables or ignores a reliable system, losing its benefits). In safety-critical contexts such as surgical robots or autonomous vehicles, an over-trust TCE of even 0.20 can result in fatal outcomes because the user may not intervene when the system fails. Minimizing TCE ensures that user vigilance matches system capability.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **seven pillars of CPS UX design** with a real-world example of how each pillar manifests in an **autonomous vehicle's HMI**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Compute the **composite CPS UX score** for an autonomous vehicle's emergency braking HMI given:
- Effectiveness $E = 0.92$
- Safety $S = 0.98$
- Comfort $C = 0.78$
- Trust $T = 0.85$
- Weights: $\alpha = 0.10, \beta = 0.60, \gamma = 0.10, \delta = 0.20$

Comment on which dimension dominates the score and why this is appropriate for the domain.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Seven Pillars Explanation [7 Marks]

| Pillar | Manifestation in Autonomous Vehicle HMI |
|---|---|
| **1. Safety-Critical Design** | The HMI must have a fail-safe state where the vehicle can be brought to a controlled stop (minimum risk maneuver) if the user becomes unresponsive. The "Emergency Stop" button is a fail-safe affordance. |
| **2. Real-Time Responsiveness** | Lane-departure warnings must appear within 150 ms of crossing the lane marking. Audio-visual-haptic alerts are designed to be perceived within 100 ms. |
| **3. Context-Awareness** | The HMI dims at night, increases font size when the driver is detected as elderly, and adjusts alert volume based on cabin noise (sensor fusion of microphone + camera). |
| **4. Multi-Modal Interaction** | The driver can use voice ("Set destination to airport"), touch (center console), and gesture (wave hand to dismiss notification). Haptic feedback is provided via steering wheel vibration for lane departure. |
| **5. Trust Calibration** | The HMI shows a visible reliability indicator (e.g., "Autopilot confidence: 87%") so the driver can appropriately calibrate supervision effort. |
| **6. Privacy & Security** | The HMI clearly indicates when the in-cabin camera is recording and allows the driver to disable location tracking. |
| **7. Physical-Digital Continuity** | A live camera feed on the dashboard shows what the car "sees" so the driver can verify the digital representation matches physical reality. |

**[Award full 7 marks when all seven pillars are mapped with concrete autonomous-vehicle HMI examples.]**

#### Part (b) — Composite Score Calculation [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Verify the weight constraint:**

$$ 0.10 + 0.60 + 0.10 + 0.20 = 1.00 \quad \checkmark $$

**Step 2 — Apply the formula:**

$$ U_{CPS} = \alpha E + \beta S + \gamma C + \delta T $$

**Step 3 — Substitute values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
U_{CPS} &= (0.10)(0.92) + (0.60)(0.98) + (0.10)(0.78) + (0.20)(0.85) \\
U_{CPS} &= 0.092 + 0.588 + 0.078 + 0.170 \\
U_{CPS} &= 0.928
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Identify the dominant dimension:**

The **Safety dimension contributes 0.588 out of 0.928 = 63.4%** of the total score.

**Step 5 — Justify the dominance:**

This dominance is **appropriate** for an autonomous vehicle's emergency braking HMI because physical safety is the paramount concern in the automotive domain. A 60% weight on safety reflects regulatory priorities (ISO 26262, ASIL-D classification) and the principle that **safety considerations should override comfort and convenience in life-critical CPS**. KTU expects students to explicitly comment on the appropriateness of weight selection.

**[Award full 7 marks when calculation, weight verification, and domain-justified commentary are present.]**

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **real-time responsiveness** and **multi-modal interaction** pillars of CPS UX. Explain why these two pillars are particularly critical for **medical CPS** (e.g., robotic-assisted surgery systems).
