> **FIDO2 / WebAuthn** is the modern gold standard — it is the only method that is *cryptographically phishing-resistant*. KTU 2024 scheme expects awareness of this standard.

### 2.5 Authorization Models Relevant to UX

While engineers implement these, the **UX designer must surface the outcomes** clearly to the user:

- **Role-Based Access Control (RBAC):** Permissions are tied to a *role* (Admin, Editor, Viewer). UX must clearly indicate which role the user holds and what it permits.
- **Attribute-Based Access Control (ABAC):** Permissions depend on *attributes* (department, time of day, location). UX must explain *why* access was denied (e.g., "This file is restricted to the Finance department").
- **Principle of Least Privilege (PoLP):** Grant only the minimum access needed. UX implication: progressive disclosure of features (show advanced tools only when the user's role requires them).

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

In the industry, this knowledge is applied when:
- Designing **fintech apps** (UPI, net banking) where RBI mandates 2FA.
- Building **healthcare portals** under HIPAA/DISHA where patient data access must be audited.
- Creating **government e-governance platforms** (DigiLocker, UMANG) where Aadhaar-based authentication is integrated.
- Designing **IoT device pairing flows** where the UX must guide a non-technical user through secure Wi-Fi provisioning.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Design Process & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The 7-Step Design Process for Secure Authentication Flows

The KTU 2024 module expects students to follow a **structured, human-centered design process**. The steps are derived from the **Double Diamond** model (Discover, Define, Develop, Deliver) combined with security-specific additions.

**Step 1 — User Research & Persona Development**
Identify the *types* of users (novice, expert, elderly, visually impaired) and their security contexts (public kiosk, personal phone, shared device). For each persona, document:
- Device literacy
- Threat awareness
- Accessibility needs (screen readers, motor impairments)

**Step 2 — Threat Modeling (STRIDE-per-Interaction)**
Apply the **STRIDE** framework to *each interaction point* in the login flow:

$$\text{STRIDE} = \{S, T, R, I, D, E\}$$

where:
- $S$ = Spoofing (fake credentials)
- $T$ = Tampering (modifying data in transit)
- $R$ = Repudiation (user denies an action)
- $I$ = Information Disclosure (data leak)
- $D$ = Denial of Service (locking out legitimate users)
- $E$ = Elevation of Privilege (gaining unauthorized access)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> UX must defend against *Denial of Service* against the **legitimate user** — e.g., a forgotten-password flow that locks the account after 3 wrong attempts is a **self-inflicted DoS**.

**Step 3 — Choose Authentication Factors Based on Risk**
Use a **risk-scoring matrix** to decide which factors to require. The KTU analytical matrix is:

$$
\text{Risk Score} = w_1 \cdot A + w_2 \cdot V + w_3 \cdot S + w_4 \cdot D
$$

where:
- $A$ = Asset sensitivity (1–5)
- $V$ = Action being performed (view, edit, delete) (1–5)
- $S$ = Session novelty (new device, new location) (1–5)
- $D$ = Data volume being accessed (1–5)
- $w_i$ = context-specific weights

> The KTU 2024 board exam may ask you to apply this scoring to a sample scenario (e.g., "user logs in from a new device to transfer ₹50,000 — what risk score and which factors?").

**Step 4 — Design the Login Screen (Affordance Mapping)**
- Place the primary action (Sign In) where the eye naturally lands (top-right or center on mobile).
- Show password requirements *before* the user types (not after failing).
- Use **inline validation** rather than a single submit-and-pray error.
- Offer "Show password" as a toggle (an icon, not a label) to reduce typo errors.

**Step 5 — Design the Recovery Flow**
Recovery is *part of* authentication, not separate from it. Design:
- Self-service password reset via verified email/phone.
- Account recovery codes (downloadable, one-time use).
- Human-assisted recovery for high-value accounts (with identity verification steps).
- Clear messaging: "We will never call you to ask for your password."

**Step 6 — Authorization Surfacing (Permission UX)**
The user must always know:
- *What* they can do (visible UI elements).
- *Why* they cannot do something (when an action is hidden, show a tooltip: "This feature is restricted to Admins").
- *How to request more access* (a clear escalation path).

**Step 7 — Accessibility & Inclusive Security Audit**
Verify against **WCAG 2.2**:
- All form fields have `<label>` elements (for screen readers).
- Color is not the *only* signal of error (also use icons and text).
- Biometric fallback is always available (for users who cannot use fingerprint).
- Sufficient contrast (minimum 4.5:1) for all security indicators.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Worked Example — Risk-Based Authentication Decision

> **Scenario:** A user named *Priya* logs into her net banking app at 2:00 AM from a new device in a different city to transfer ₹75,000.

**Step 1: Score each factor on a 1–5 scale.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 5 \quad \text{(banking is high-sensitivity)} \\
V &= 4 \quad \text{(transfer is a high-impact action)} \\
S &= 5 \quad \text{(new device + new city)} \\
D &= 4 \quad \text{(amount is significant)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply weights (typical enterprise weights).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
w_1 &= 0.30, \quad w_2 = 0.25, \quad w_3 = 0.30, \quad w_4 = 0.15 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the risk score.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Risk Score} &= (0.30 \times 5) + (0.25 \times 4) + (0.30 \times 5) + (0.15 \times 4) \\
&= 1.50 + 1.00 + 1.50 + 0.60 \\
&= 4.60
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4: Map the score to an authentication requirement.**

$$
\text{Threshold Mapping} = \begin{cases} \text{Password only} & \text{if } \text{Score} \leq 2.0 \\ \text{Password + OTP} & \text{if } 2.0 < \text{Score} \leq 3.5 \\ \text{Password + OTP + Biometric} & \text{if } \text{Score} > 3.5 \end{cases}
$$

Since $4.60 > 3.5$, the system should require **Password + OTP + Biometric** for this transaction. The UX should explain *why* the extra step is being asked: "We've added an extra check because you're signing in from a new device."

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is explanation important?** Without context, the user perceives it as *annoying friction*. With context, it becomes *justified security* — and trust in the system increases.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparing UX Outcomes of Authentication Methods

| Evaluation Criterion | Password | SMS OTP | TOTP App | Biometric | Passkey (FIDO2) |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Setup time (user effort) | Low | Medium | High | Low | Medium |
| Login time (per use) | ~5 s | ~15 s | ~10 s | ~2 s | ~3 s |
| Phishing resistance | Low | Low | Medium | High | Very High |
| Works offline | Yes | No | Yes (after setup) | Yes | Yes |
| Accessible to elderly | Medium | High | Low | Medium | Medium |
| Recoverable if lost | Yes (reset) | Yes (new SIM) | Hard (re-enroll) | Very Hard | Yes (new device) |
| GDPR/DPDP compliance complexity | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Low |

> This matrix is a *frequently-asked* KTU 2024-style analytical question. A 7-mark answer can be built by selecting 4–5 criteria and comparing 3 methods with justification.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 End-to-End Authentication & Authorization Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    userA[User Opens App] --> sessionA{Session Active?}
    sessionA -- Yes --> dashboardA[Show Dashboard with Role-Based UI]
    sessionA -- No --> loginA[Show Login Screen]
    loginA --> credA[User Enters Credentials]
    credA --> validateA{Credentials Valid?}
    validateA -- No --> errorA[Show Inline Error + Attempts Left]
    errorA --> loginA
    validateA -- Yes --> riskA[Calculate Risk Score]
    riskA --> lowR{Risk Low?}
    lowR -- Yes --> grantA[Grant Access Token]
    lowR -- No --> mfaA[Trigger MFA Challenge]
    mfaA --> mfaV{MFA Verified?}
    mfaV -- No --> lockA[Optional Temporary Lock + Notify]
    mfaV -- Yes --> grantA
    grantA --> authzA[Authorization Engine Checks Role/Permissions]
    authzA --> allowA{Allowed?}
    allowA -- Yes --> dashboardA
    allowA -- No --> denyA[Show Access Denied with Reason + Escalation Link]
    dashboardA --> idleA{Inactivity Timer Expired?}
    idleA -- Yes --> logoutA[Auto Logout with Warning]
    idleA -- No --> dashboardA
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** Start at `userA` (top). Each diamond is a decision point; each rectangle is a UI state the designer must prototype. This is the *minimum* flow any secure app should implement.

### 4.2 Decision Tree — Which Authentication Factor to Choose?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[New App or Feature] --> q1{User has a smartphone?}
    q1 -- No --> q2{Is it enterprise B2B?}
    q2 -- Yes --> ssoA[Use SSO with Hardware Key FIDO2]
    q2 -- No --> pwdA[Password plus Security Questions]
    q1 -- Yes --> q3{Is data high sensitivity?}
    q3 -- No --> lowA[Passwordless Magic Link or Social Login]
    q3 -- Yes --> q4{Does user need offline access?}
    q4 -- Yes --> totpA[TOTP Authenticator plus Password]
    q4 -- No --> q5{Is device biometric available?}
    q5 -- Yes --> bioA[Biometric plus Push Notification]
    q5 -- No --> smsA[SMS OTP plus Password]
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Authorization Layer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    uiA[User Interface Layer] --> gateA[Authorization Gate Middleware]
    gateA --> policyA[Policy Decision Point PDP]
    policyA --> policyB[Policy Enforcement Point PEP]
    policyB --> resA[Resource Server Data APIs]
    policyA -. reads .-> storeA[Policy Store Roles and Permissions]
    policyA -. logs .-> auditA[Audit Log for Compliance]
    resA --> uiA
    auditA --> adminA[Admin Dashboard for Security Review]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer takeaway:** The user never *sees* the PDP or PEP — but the UX surfaces their *outcome* (allowed or denied) and the *audit log* (the user can view "My recent activity" in settings). Designing this audit-history view is a high-value UX task.

### 4.4 Trust Signal Placement on a Login Screen (Wireframe Schematic)

```
+--------------------------------------------------+
|  [Logo]                  [Need Help?]   [🌐 EN]  |
+--------------------------------------------------+
|                                                  |
|           Welcome back. Sign in to continue.      |
|                                                  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
|  |  Email or phone                            |  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
|  |  Password                          [👁 Show] |  |
|  +--------------------------------------------+  |
|  [ ] Remember this device   Forgot password?     |
|                                                  |
|  [        Sign In (Secure 🔒)        ]           |
|                                                  |
|  ---- or continue with ----                      |
|  [G  Google]  [ Apple ]  [ Microsoft ]           |
|                                                  |
|  New here? Create an account                     |
|                                                  |
|  🔒 256-bit encryption  |  🛡 GDPR Compliant     |
+--------------------------------------------------+
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Annotations a KTU student should add in their design portfolio:**
> - The **"Show"** password icon reduces typos (error prevention heuristic).
> - The **"Remember this device"** checkbox is a UX-level risk reduction (lowers future friction).
> - The **trust badges** (🔒 256-bit, 🛡 GDPR) address the user's emotional need for safety.
> - The **social login** offers an *alternative* path, supporting the "Recognition rather than recall" heuristic.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*Differentiate between **authentication** and **authorization**. Provide one real-world example of each from a mobile banking application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Authentication | Authorization |
|---|---|---|
| Question answered | "Who are you?" | "What are you allowed to do?" |
| When it happens | At login / re-verification | Continuously, after login |
| Mechanism | Password, OTP, biometric | Role check, permission check |
| Mobile banking example | User enters PIN + fingerprint to log in | Logged-in customer can view balance but cannot approve a loan without an admin role |

*(1 mark for clear definition of each, 1 mark for the question each answers, 1 mark for the example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*List any **three authentication factors** and explain why using two passwords is **not** considered Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Knowledge factor** — something the user knows (e.g., password, PIN).
2. **Possession factor** — something the user has (e.g., smartphone receiving OTP, hardware token).
3. **Inherence factor** — something the user is (e.g., fingerprint, face).

Two passwords are **not** MFA because both belong to the *same* factor category (knowledge). MFA, by definition, requires credentials from **two or more *different* categories**. If one password is compromised (e.g., via phishing), the second password offers no additional protection since it shares the same vulnerability surface.

*(1 mark per factor, 1 mark for the explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students frequently lose marks by **listing authentication methods without UX justification**. Always tie your answer to the *user experience* (e.g., friction, accessibility, trust).
> - Another common mistake is **confusing authentication with encryption**. Authentication is *identity verification*; encryption is *data protection*. They are related but distinct.
> - In 14-mark questions, the KTU 2024 scheme expects **both theory and a diagram or example**. A question answered with only text and no visual/supporting example typically caps at 8–9 marks.
> - Always cite **WCAG, GDPR, or NIST** standards where relevant — this signals CO attainment at the highest Bloom's level (Evaluate / Create).

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* Explain the **three authentication factors** with suitable UX examples. Discuss how each factor impacts **accessibility, security, and user friction** in a digital product. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* Design a **secure login flow for a healthcare patient portal** that complies with accessibility (WCAG 2.2) and data-privacy norms. Describe each screen state, the trust signals used, and the recovery flow. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question A**

**(a) The Three Authentication Factors (7 marks)**

**1. Knowledge Factor (Something you KNOW) — 2 marks**
- Examples: Passwords, PINs, security questions, pattern locks.
- **UX Strength:** Universally understood, no extra hardware needed, low cost.
- **UX Weakness:** Forgettable (causing lockouts), weak user choices (e.g., "123456"), vulnerable to phishing and shoulder-surfing.
- **Accessibility concern:** Cognitively demanding for users with memory impairments; security questions often rely on outdated personal information.

**2. Possession Factor (Something you HAVE) — 2 marks**
- Examples: SMS/email OTP, TOTP authenticator app, hardware security key (FIDO2/YubiKey), smart card.
- **UX Strength:** Cannot be guessed by observation; rotates frequently (especially TOTP, OTP).
- **UX Weakness:** Device loss creates a *recovery nightmare*; SMS interception via SIM-swap; dependency on network/battery.
- **Accessibility concern:** Hardware keys require fine motor control; OTP entry on small screens is difficult for users with motor impairments.

**3. Inherence Factor (Something you ARE) — 2 marks**
- Examples: Fingerprint, face recognition, iris scan, voice authentication, behavioral biometrics (keystroke dynamics).
- **UX Strength:** Fastest perceived login (often < 1 second), cannot be forgotten, hard to share.
- **UX Weakness:** Cannot be *reset* — if compromised, the user is permanently affected. Environmental factors (wet fingers, dim light, masks) cause failure.
- **Accessibility concern:** Users with physical differences, elderly users with worn fingerprints, and individuals who are blind or deaf face exclusion. **A biometric-only system violates WCAG 2.2 unless an equivalent alternative is provided.**

**Synthesis (1 mark):** A mature UX design must **combine** factors (MFA) and always provide a **non-biometric fallback**. The choice of factor should be driven by the risk-score of the action, as shown in the analytical matrix in Section 3.3.

---

**(b) Secure Login Flow for a Healthcare Patient Portal (7 marks)**

**Design Goal:** Allow patients to access lab reports, prescriptions, and book appointments, while complying with the **Digital Information Security in Healthcare Act (DISHA)** principles and **WCAG 2.2**.

**Screen 1 — Welcome / Sign-in Choice (1.5 marks)**
- Large, high-contrast logo and headline: *"Access your health records securely."*
- Three options: **Sign in with Password**, **Sign in with Aadhaar (OTP-based, if permitted)**, **Biometric (if mobile app)**.
- Visible trust signals: 🔒 *"End-to-end encrypted"* | 🛡 *"DISHA-compliant"* | 🇮🇳 *"Government of India approved standards"*.
- "New patient? Register" link in plain language.
- Language toggle (English / Malayalam / Hindi) for inclusivity.

**Screen 2 — Credential Entry (1.5 marks)**
- Email/phone field with inline validation (green tick on valid format).
- Password field with **show/hide toggle** (eye icon), live strength meter, and *visible requirements* (uppercase, number, special character).
- "Remember this trusted device" checkbox (with a tooltip explaining the security implication).
- Forgot password link clearly placed *before* the Sign In button (per error-prevention heuristic).

**Screen 3 — MFA Challenge (Conditional, 1 mark)**
- Triggered only if risk score > 3.5 (e.g., new device).
- OTP sent to verified phone; 6-digit input with auto-advance and paste support.
- "Resend OTP in 30s" with a countdown timer (visibility of system status).
- On failure: clear message, never reveal whether the *email* or *password* was wrong (prevents user enumeration attacks).

**Screen 4 — Dashboard with Role-Based UI (1 mark)**
- Patient sees: Reports, Prescriptions, Appointments, Profile.
- Each sensitive action (downloading a report) shows a small 🔒 icon and an "Activity logged" toast notification (builds trust, supports audit).
- No admin or doctor-only features are visible (principle of least privilege).

**Recovery Flow (1 mark)**
- "Forgot password" → verified email link → identity check (last 4 digits of Aadhaar OR security question) → new password setup with strength enforcement.
- For high-value recovery (e.g., changing phone number): in-person verification at the hospital reception is offered as an escalation path.
- Always show a list of "Recent login activity" so the user can detect unauthorized access.

**Accessibility Checklist (1 mark)**
- All fields have ARIA labels.
- Tab order is logical; focus indicators are highly visible.
- Color is *never* the only error signal (icon + text + color).
- Works with screen readers (NVDA, TalkBack tested).
- Session timeout warns at 60 s and 10 s before auto-logout (avoids data loss for slow users).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]**
*(a)* With a neat block diagram, explain the **architecture of an authorization system** in a modern web application. Differentiate between **RBAC** and **ABAC** with UX implications. **(7 marks)**

*(b)* A fintech startup wants to allow users to transfer up to ₹1,00,000 per day. Apply the **risk-based authentication decision process** to design an adaptive authentication flow. Show the calculation of risk score and the UX messaging for each level. **(7 marks)**

---

### **Model Solution — Question B**

**(a) Authorization Architecture (7 marks)**

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**
```
[User Interface] → [Authorization Gate / Middleware] → [Policy Decision Point (PDP)]
                                                              ↓
                                                  [Policy Store (Roles, Permissions, Attributes)]
                                                              ↓
                                                  [Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)]
                                                              ↓
                                                  [Resource Server / Database]
                                                  [Audit Log] → [Admin Dashboard]
```
- The **User Interface** sends a request tagged with the user's identity and intended action.
- The **Authorization Gate** intercepts *every* request before it reaches data.
- The **Policy Decision Point (PDP)** consults the **Policy Store** to decide *allow* or *deny*.
- The **Policy Enforcement Point (PEP)** applies the decision; the request is either fulfilled or rejected with a clear reason.
- Every decision is logged for **audit and compliance** (visible to the user as "My Activity History").

**RBAC vs ABAC (4 marks):**

| Aspect | RBAC (Role-Based) | ABAC (Attribute-Based) |
|---|---|---|
| Basis of decision | User's *role* | User's, resource's, and environment's *attributes* |
| Example | "Admins can delete users" | "Doctors in the Cardiology dept can view records of patients admitted to Cardiology between 9 AM–5 PM" |
| UX implication | Simpler UI; roles are usually visible ("Logged in as: Admin") | More context-aware; UX must explain *why* access was denied with the specific attribute (e.g., "Access is restricted to your department") |
| Scalability | Becomes rigid with many roles | Scales well with many fine-grained conditions |
| Best suited for | Small teams, clear hierarchies | Healthcare, finance, multi-tenant SaaS |

---

**(b) Risk-Based Authentication for Fintech (7 marks)**

**Context:** Daily transfer limit is ₹1,00,000. Risk-based authentication must apply different friction levels depending on context.

**Step 1: Risk Score Formula (1 mark)**

$$
\text{Risk Score} = w_1 A + w_2 V + w_3 S + w_4 D
$$

**Step 2: Assign Weights and Score a Sample (2 marks)**

For a user transferring ₹75,000 from a *known* device during *normal hours*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= 3 \text{ (financial, but not the maximum limit)} \\
V &= 4 \text{ (transfer is high-impact)} \\
S &= 1 \text{ (trusted device, normal location)} \\
D &= 3 \text{ (₹75,000 is significant)} \\
w_1 = 0.25, \quad w_2 = 0.30, \quad w_3 = 0.20, \quad w_4 = 0.25
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{Risk Score} = (0.25 \times 3) + (0.30 \times 4) + (0.20 \times 1) + (0.25 \times 3)
$$

$$
= 0.75 + 1.20 + 0.20 + 0.75 = 2.90
$$

**Step 3: Map Score to Required Authentication Level (2 marks)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Score} \leq 2.0 &\rightarrow \text{Password + Biometric} \\
2.0 < \text{Score} \leq 3.5 &\rightarrow \text{Password + Biometric + OTP} \\
\text{Score} > 3.5 &\rightarrow \text{Password + Biometric + OTP + Video KYC}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since the score is **2.90**, the system should require **Password + Biometric + OTP**.

**Step 4: UX Messaging for Each Level (2 marks)**

- **Low risk (≤ 2.0):** *"Confirm transfer with your fingerprint."* (Single tap, no OTP)
- **Medium risk (2.0–3.5):** *"For your security, please verify with fingerprint and the 6-digit code we just sent to your phone."* (Explains *why* an extra step is needed — builds trust)
- **High risk (> 3.5):** *"We've detected unusual activity. To protect your account, we need a quick video verification (under 60 seconds) before completing this transfer."* (Justifies the highest friction; offers estimated time)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Designer principle applied here:** The user is never left wondering *why* an extra step appeared. Each level of friction is paired with a *plain-language explanation* of the security reason. This converts friction from *annoyance* into *reassurance*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**
> - ✅ **Authentication** verifies *identity*; **Authorization** verifies *permission*. Never use them interchangeably.
> - ✅ The **three factors** are Knowledge, Possession, and Inherence. **MFA** requires *different* categories.
> - ✅ The **friction-security trade-off** is central to secure UX design. Use **risk-based authentication** to apply friction *proportionally* to the risk.
> - ✅ The **STRIDE** framework helps identify threats at each interaction point in a flow.
> - ✅ **Biometrics are not secret** — fingerprints and faces can be photographed. They must always be paired with a *liveness check* and a *fallback method*.
> - ✅ **WCAG 2.2 compliance** is non-negotiable: every security mechanism must have an accessible alternative.
> - ✅ **Trust signals** (encryption badges, compliance logos) must be placed *visibly* but *without cluttering* the primary action.
> - ✅ **Recovery flows are part of authentication**, not separate. Always design a self-service recovery path with escalation.
> - ✅ **Dark patterns** in security (e.g., hidden unsubscribe, forced MFA) damage long-term trust and may violate **DPDP Act 2023** and **GDPR**.
> - ✅ **FIDO2 / WebAuthn / Passkeys** represent the modern phishing-resistant standard — mention them wherever relevant for high marks.
> - ✅ Always cite **CO attainment** in your answers: this topic maps to **CO4: Design for Trust, Privacy, and Security** in the PECPT744 syllabus.
> - ✅ For 14-mark questions, the KTU 2024 scheme expects **theory + diagram + example + standards citation** — missing any of these costs marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: UX design for secure data transmission and storage
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** UX design for secure data transmission and storage

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**UX Design for Secure Data Transmission and Storage** is the specialised discipline of User Experience (UX) design that focuses on creating intuitive, trustworthy, and friction-appropriate interfaces for systems that handle the secure transmission (in transit) and secure storage (at rest) of sensitive user data. It synthesises principles from **Human-Computer Interaction (HCI)**, **Information Security (InfoSec)**, **Privacy Engineering**, and **Behavioural Psychology** to ensure that security mechanisms (authentication, encryption, authorisation, consent) are not only technically robust but also cognitively accessible and behaviourally effective for end users.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, this topic sits at the intersection of **Module 4 – Trust, Privacy, and Security in UX**, and addresses the following Course Outcomes (COs):

- **CO4 (Module 4):** *Design user experiences that establish trust, ensure privacy compliance, and integrate security seamlessly without compromising usability.*
- **CO5 (Module 5 – Project Integration):** *Apply ethical and secure design patterns in capstone UX projects.*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** The 2024 NEP-aligned syllabus explicitly mandates that students understand **Privacy by Design (PbD)**, **GDPR-aligned consent flows**, **authentication UX heuristics**, and **trust indicator design** as examinable cognitive outcomes under Bloom's Levels L2 (Understand), L3 (Apply), and L4 (Analyse).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bank Vault with a Friendly Receptionist"

Imagine walking into a high-security bank vault. The vault itself is engineered with **reinforced steel, biometric scanners, and encrypted locks** (this is the *technical security layer*). However, the experience of entering it is mediated by a **friendly receptionist** who:

1. Greets you warmly (builds **trust**).
2. Explains *why* she needs your ID before letting you in (provides **transparency**).
3. Uses clear, jargon-free language ("Please place your finger here") instead of shouting cryptographic protocols (ensures **usability**).
4. Tells you exactly what happens to your fingerprint after you leave (offers **privacy assurance**).
5. Doesn't ask you 10 times for the same document (avoids **friction fatigue**).

This **receptionist is the "UX layer" of security**. The vault (encryption, TLS, hashing) is invisible and works silently in the background. If the receptionist is rude, confusing, or intimidating, customers will avoid the bank — no matter how strong the vault is. This is precisely the paradox **UX Design for Secure Data Transmission and Storage** resolves: *technical security is necessary but not sufficient*; the user-facing layer must equally inspire confidence and enable smooth interaction.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Definition (Board-Examiner Standard):**
> *"UX Design for secure data transmission and storage is the practice of engineering user interfaces that make cryptographic, authentication, and privacy mechanisms perceivable, understandable, and actionable by non-technical users, while preserving the underlying technical integrity of data in transit (e.g., TLS/SSL) and data at rest (e.g., AES-256 encryption)."*

## 1.3 The Three Pillars Framework

The KTU 2024 curriculum groups this topic under three interlocking pillars:

| **Pillar** | **Technical Substrate (Invisible to User)** | **UX Surface (Visible to User)** |
|---|---|---|
| **Transmission Security** | TLS 1.3, HTTPS, certificate pinning, end-to-end encryption (E2EE) | Padlock icon, URL bar verification, "Encrypted in transit" badge, connection status indicators |
| **Storage Security** | AES-256 at-rest encryption, salted+hashed passwords (bcrypt, Argon2), HSMs, secure enclaves | Privacy dashboards, "Manage my data" controls, account deletion flows, consent receipts |
| **Access Security** | OAuth 2.1, OpenID Connect, MFA, biometrics, RBAC, zero-trust | Login screens, MFA prompts, permission dialogs, session timeout warnings, password strength meters |

## 1.4 Why This Topic Matters — KTU 2024 Industry Context

Modern UX failures in security are not hypothetical — they have caused real-world breaches:

- **Equifax (2017):** Technical encryption was adequate, but UX-driven patching failures exposed 147 million records.
- **Facebook (2018–2019):** Confusing privacy settings led to massive data exposure despite robust storage encryption.
- **Twitter/X (2020):** Social engineering bypassed MFA, partly due to confusing MFA UX that users turned off.

The KTU 2024 Scheme recognises that **up to 95% of cybersecurity incidents are caused by human error** (Verizon DBIR, 2023). UX designers are therefore the **first line of behavioural defence**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Metric to Remember:**
> - **W3C Web Trust Indicator Adoption Rate: 76%** of users look for the padlock icon before entering sensitive data.
> - **Baymard Institute Finding:** 18% of shoppers abandon carts due to lack of visible trust signals.
> - **NIST Recommendation:** Maximum **5 failed login attempts** before temporary lockout (balances security with UX).

## 1.5 Key Terminology (KTU-Examiner-Ready Glossary)

- **Authentication (AuthN):** Verifying *who* the user is (password, fingerprint, OTP).
- **Authorization (AuthZ):** Verifying *what* the user is allowed to do (role-based access).
- **Data in Transit:** Data moving across networks (protected by TLS).
- **Data at Rest:** Data stored in databases/filesystems (protected by encryption-at-rest).
- **Privacy by Design (PbD):** Proactive embedding of privacy into system architecture from the outset.
- **Trust Signal:** Any visual/UX cue (padlock, badge, certificate) that communicates security to users.
- **Friction (in Security UX):** Deliberate, calibrated user effort added to thwart attackers without alienating legitimate users.
- **Dark Pattern:** Deceptive UI design that tricks users into insecure or privacy-violating actions (banned under GDPR).
- **Consent Flow:** Interactive UX process for obtaining informed, specific, and revocable user consent (GDPR Art. 7).

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations — The Five-Layer Security UX Model

The KTU 2024 Scheme endorses a **Five-Layer Model** for analysing UX in secure systems. Each layer has a corresponding design responsibility.

### **Layer 1: Perception (The Trust Signal Layer)**

This is the *outermost* layer — what the user *sees*. It includes:

- **Visual indicators:** Padlock icon, HTTPS prefix, EV (Extended Validation) green bar, security badges (Norton, McAfee, BBB).
- **Auditory indicators:** "Beep" on successful biometric scan, voice prompts for 2FA.
- **Haptic indicators:** Smartphone vibration on successful fingerprint match.

**Why it matters:** Perceived security ≠ actual security, but **perceived security drives user behaviour**. Fogg's Trust Model formalises this:

$$T = \frac{C + R + I}{S}$$

Where:
- $T$ = **Trustworthiness** (subjective user trust score)
- $C$ = **Credibility** (how believable the site is)
- $R$ = **Reliability** (consistency of experience)
- $I$ = **Intimacy** (emotional connection / familiarity)
- $S$ = **Self-orientation** (user's self-interest focus; **denominator**, so higher $S$ reduces $T$)

> **KTU Insight:** A site with high $C$ and $R$ but also high $S$ (e.g., users feel the site is "only after my money") will have low $T$. Security UX must *lower* $S$ through transparency.

### **Layer 2: Authentication (The "Who Are You" Layer)**

UX design must address **three authentication sub-challenges**:

1. **Memory burden** — passwords are cognitively expensive.
2. **Entry friction** — typing passwords on mobile is error-prone.
3. **Recovery friction** — account lockouts are a primary support cost driver.

**Modern UX patterns for authentication:**

| **Pattern** | **Security Strength** | **UX Friction** | **KTU Use Case** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Password only | Low (NIST SP 800-63B: outdated) | Medium | Legacy systems |
| Password + SMS OTP | Medium | High | Banking apps |
| Password + TOTP (Google Authenticator) | High | High | Enterprise SaaS |
| Passwordless (Magic Link via email) | Medium | Low | Consumer apps (Slack, Notion) |
| Biometric (Fingerprint/Face ID) | High | **Very Low** | Mobile-first apps |
| Passkey (FIDO2/WebAuthn) | **Very High** (phishing-resistant) | **Very Low** | Modern web apps (Apple, Google) |
| Adaptive MFA (risk-based) | High | Context-dependent | Zero-trust architectures |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Exam Buzzword:** *"Passkeys"* (FIDO2/WebAuthn standard) are explicitly mentioned in the 2024 syllabus as the **future of authentication UX** — phishing-resistant, device-bound, and frictionless.

### **Layer 3: Authorization (The "What Can You Do" Layer)**

UX must make **permissions legible**. A user granting "Read access to your contacts" should see a clear icon, plain-English description, and a one-tap revoke option. Poor AuthZ UX is what created the **Facebook Cambridge Analytica scandal** — users granted broad permissions without understanding the implications.

**Key AuthZ UX patterns:**
- **Principle of Least Privilege (POLP):** Default to minimum permissions.
- **Just-in-Time Permission Requests:** Request access *only when* the feature is used (Android 6.0+ model).
- **Plain-Language Scopes:** Replace `oauth_scope=read:user` with "View your basic profile information".

### **Layer 4: Transmission (The "Data in Transit" Layer)**

When data leaves the user's device, UX must communicate **two things**:
1. *Is this connection safe?* → Visible TLS indicators.
2. *Is this message end-to-end encrypted?* → E2EE status (e.g., WhatsApp's "Encrypted" banner).

**Critical UX rule:** *Never display TLS errors in raw technical language.* A user seeing "ERR_CERT_AUTHORITY_INVALID" has zero actionable next step. Translate to: *"We couldn't verify this site's security certificate. Your data may be at risk. [Learn more] [Go back to safety]"*

### **Layer 5: Storage (The "Data at Rest" Layer)**

UX must provide **user-facing controls** for stored data:
- **Privacy dashboard** (Google Account, Apple ID model).
- **Data export** (GDPR Art. 20 — Right to Data Portability).
- **Right to be forgotten** (GDPR Art. 17 — Account deletion flow).
- **Granular consent management** (toggle individual data categories).

## 2.2 The Privacy by Design (PbD) Framework — Ann Cavoukian's 7 Principles

Ann Cavoukian's framework is **explicitly tested** in KTU 2024 exams. The seven foundational principles are:

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventive not Remedial** — Design privacy in *before* the breach.
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting** — Maximum privacy without user action required.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design** — Not bolted on as an add-on.
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum** — Security AND usability, not either/or.
5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection** — From collection to deletion.
6. **Visibility and Transparency** — Keep practices open and verifiable.
7. **Respect for User Privacy** — User-centric, with strong defaults and consent.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** When asked "Explain Privacy by Design," always cite **at least 4 of the 7 principles** by name and provide a *concrete UX example* (e.g., "Signal app's default disappearing messages = Privacy as Default").

## 2.3 The Security-Usability Trade-Off Equation

There is no universal "correct" amount of friction. KTU endorses a **Risk-Weighted Friction** model:

$$F_{optimal} = \alpha \cdot R_{action} \cdot (1 - C_{user})$$

Where:
- $F_{optimal}$ = **Optimal friction** (cognitive/effort cost imposed on user)
- $R_{action}$ = **Risk score of the action** (e.g., 0.1 for viewing a public post, 0.9 for transferring ₹50,000)
- $C_{user}$ = **User's confidence/sophistication** score (0 to 1)
- $\alpha$ = **Calibration constant** (organisation-specific, typically 1.0–2.0)

**Interpretation:**
- High-risk action ($R_{action} \approx 0.9$) + Low-confidence user ($C_{user} \approx 0.2$) → **High friction** justified (MFA + OTP + cooling period).
- Low-risk action ($R_{action} \approx 0.1$) + High-confidence user ($C_{user} \approx 0.9$) → **Low friction** (single tap, no MFA).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Formula / Metric** | **Variables Explained** | **Standard Value / Unit** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Fogg Trust Model | $T = (C + R + I) \div S$ | $C$ = Credibility, $R$ = Reliability, $I$ = Intimacy, $S$ = Self-orientation | Score: 0–10 (subjective) |
| Optimal Friction | $F = \alpha \cdot R \cdot (1 - C)$ | $\alpha$ = constant, $R$ = risk, $C$ = user confidence | Dimensionless index |
| Password Entropy | $E = L \cdot \log_{2}(N)$ | $L$ = length, $N$ = character pool size | **Bits** (NIST min: 30 bits) |
| Brute-Force Time | $t = 2^{E} \div G$ | $E$ = entropy, $G$ = guesses/sec | **Seconds** |
| Hash Iteration Cost | $H_{time} = N_{iter} \cdot t_{hash}$ | $N_{iter}$ = bcrypt rounds, $t_{hash}$ = single hash time | **Milliseconds** (bcrypt: 250–500 ms target) |
| Session Timeout (NIST) | $T_{session} \leq 30 \text{ min}$ (inactive), $12 \text{ hr}$ (absolute) | Inactivity vs absolute | **Minutes / Hours** |
| Failed Login Lockout | $N_{lockout} = 5$ attempts | Per NIST SP 800-63B | **Count** |
| Consent Withdrawal Latency | $L_{withdraw} \leq 30 \text{ days}$ | GDPR Art. 7(3) | **Days** |
| Data Breach Notification | $T_{notify} \leq 72 \text{ hours}$ | GDPR Art. 33 | **Hours** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Specific:** The **72-hour breach notification** rule under GDPR is a frequently asked 3-mark question. Memorise it with the article reference.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles taught in this module are deployed in:

- **Fintech apps (PhonePe, Google Pay):** Adaptive MFA — friction scales with transaction amount.
- **Healthcare (Aarogya Setu, MyHealthRecord):** Biometric + OTP for accessing medical records.
- **Enterprise SaaS (Slack, Notion):** SSO + passkey integration.
- **Government (DigiLocker, UMANG):** Aadhaar-linked biometric authentication with consent receipts.
- **E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart):** Visible trust badges, address anonymisation, "Secure checkout" indicators.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Fogg Trust Model Applied to a Banking App

The KTU 2024 board frequently asks students to **apply the Fogg Trust Model** to a real scenario. Below is the full worked derivation.

### **Scenario:** *Design a Trust Model for "SecureBank" mobile app, a Kerala-based digital banking startup with 100,000 users.*

**Step 1: Identify and Quantify the Four Variables**

We measure each variable on a **0–10 scale** based on user research (surveys, A/B tests, heuristic evaluation).

| **Variable** | **Definition** | **SecureBank Score (out of 10)** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $C$ (Credibility) | How believable / authoritative the site appears | **8.5** | RBI license displayed; press coverage; clear "About Us" page |
| $R$ (Reliability) | Consistency of experience over time | **9.0** | 99.9% uptime SLA; no crashes in last 6 months |
| $I$ (Intimacy) | Emotional connection / personalisation | **6.0** | Basic name greeting; no advanced personalisation yet |
| $S$ (Self-orientation) | User's perception that site serves *itself*, not user | **3.5** (lower is better) | Minimal ads; transparent fee structure |

**Step 2: Apply the Fogg Trust Formula**

$$T = \frac{C + R + I}{S}$$

**Substituting values:**

$$T = \frac{8.5 + 9.0 + 6.0}{3.5}$$

**Sum the numerator terms:**

$$C + R + I = 8.5 + 9.0 + 6.0 = 23.5$$

**Perform the division:**

$$T = \frac{23.5}{3.5}$$

$$T = 6.7142857...$$

**Round to two decimal places:**

$$T \approx 6.71$$

**Step 3: Interpretation**

A Trust score of **6.71 / 10** is "**Good**" (industry benchmark for a 1-year-old fintech is 6.0–7.0). To improve it to **8.0+** (industry-leading), the UX team would need to:

- **Increase $I$ (Intimacy):** Add personalised insights ("You spent ₹2,300 on food delivery this month — here are 3 ways to save").
- **Decrease $S$ (Self-orientation):** Add a "Why we need this data" tooltip on every permission request.
- **Maintain $C$ and $R$:** Continue RBI compliance and uptime investments.

**Step 4: Sensitivity Analysis**

To understand which lever moves the needle most, we compute **partial derivatives** (conceptual):

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial C} = \frac{1}{S} = \frac{1}{3.5} \approx 0.286$$

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial S} = -\frac{C + R + I}{S^{2}} = -\frac{23.5}{12.25} \approx -1.918$$

**Conclusion:** A 1-unit decrease in $S$ improves $T$ by **1.92 points**, whereas a 1-unit increase in $C$ improves $T$ by only **0.29 points**. **Design priority: reduce self-orientation cues first.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** This worked example demonstrates both *quantitative reasoning* and *design intuition* — exactly what KTU 2024 examiners reward with full marks (14/14).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Password Entropy & Brute-Force Time

This derivation is mandatory for the **"Network Security" cross-cutting questions** in KTU's UX + Cybersecurity interface.

### **Problem:** *A user creates the password "Kerala@2026" of length 10 characters. Calculate its entropy and the time to crack it via brute force, assuming an attacker GPU performs $10^{10}$ guesses/second.*

**Step 1: Identify the Character Pool**

"**Kerala@2026**" uses:
- Lowercase (a–z): 26 characters
- Uppercase (K, R): 2 additional character types (subset of 26)
- Digits (2, 0, 2, 6): 10 character types
- Special (@): 1 of 32 ASCII special characters

**Effective character pool (uppercase + lowercase + digits + specials):**

$$N = 26 + 26 + 10 + 32 = 94 \text{ characters}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Entropy Formula**

$$E = L \cdot \log_{2}(N)$$

Where $L = 10$ (length) and $N = 94$.

**Compute $\log_{2}(94)$:**

$$\log_{2}(94) = \frac{\ln(94)}{\ln(2)} = \frac{4.5433}{0.6931} \approx 6.5546$$

**Substitute:**

$$E = 10 \times 6.5546 = 65.546 \text{ bits}$$

**Step 3: Compute Average Brute-Force Time**

Average time = (Total keyspace / 2) ÷ Guess rate

$$t = \frac{2^{E}}{2 \cdot G} = \frac{2^{65.546}}{2 \times 10^{10}}$$

**Compute $2^{65.546}$:**

$$2^{65.546} \approx 5.15 \times 10^{19}$$

**Divide by $2G = 2 \times 10^{10}$:**

$$t = \frac{5.15 \times 10^{19}}{2 \times 10^{10}} = 2.575 \times 10^{9} \text{ seconds}$$

**Convert to years:**

$$t = \frac{2.575 \times 10^{9}}{60 \times 60 \times 24 \times 365} = \frac{2.575 \times 10^{9}}{31{,}536{,}000} \approx 81.65 \text{ years}$$

**Conclusion:** The password "Kerala@2026" provides approximately **65.5 bits of entropy** and would take roughly **82 years** to crack at $10^{10}$ guesses/second — well above the NIST minimum of 30 bits.

## 3.3 KTU 2024 Analytical Matrix — Authentication Pattern Comparison

This is the **single most important comparison table** for the 14-mark question. Memorise the structure.

| **Dimension** | **Password** | **SMS OTP** | **TOTP App** | **Biometric** | **Passkey (FIDO2)** |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Security Strength** | Low | Medium | High | High | **Very High** |
| **UX Friction** | Medium | High | High | **Very Low** | **Very Low** |
| **Phishing Resistance** | None | None | Partial | N/A (device-bound) | **Full** |
| **MFA Capable?** | Standalone | Yes (2nd factor) | Yes (2nd factor) | Yes | Yes (primary) |
| **Offline Capable?** | Yes | No (network) | Yes | Yes | Yes |
| **Recovery Complexity** | Low | High (SIM swap risk) | Medium | Low | Medium |
| **Accessibility (WCAG)** | Good | Poor (hearing, screen readers) | Fair | **Excellent** | Good |
| **Cost to Implement** | Very Low | Low (₹/SMS) | Low (free apps) | Medium (hardware) | Medium |
| **User Familiarity** | Universal | High | Medium | High (mobile) | Low (emerging) |
| **Best Use Case** | Legacy systems | Banking fallback | Enterprise | Mobile-first | Modern web/mobile |
| **GDPR Implications** | None | Phone number = PII | None | Biometric = **Sensitive PII (Art. 9)** | None |

## 3.4 Worked Example — Applying "Privacy by Design" to a UX Scenario

### **Scenario:** *Design a consent flow for a Kerala-based e-commerce app that collects (a) name, (b) email, (c) location, and (d) browsing history for personalised ads.*

**Applying Cavoukian's 7 Principles:**

| **Principle** | **UX Implementation** |
|---|---|
| 1. Proactive not Reactive | Privacy team embedded from day 1 of product design (not added post-launch) |
| 2. Privacy as Default | Location tracking is **OFF** by default; "Find stores near me" is opt-in |
| 3. Privacy Embedded into Design | Granular toggle per data type, not one master switch |
| 4. Full Functionality (Positive-Sum) | Personalised ads work *without* sharing browsing history with third parties (on-device ML) |
| 5. End-to-End Security | TLS in transit + AES-256 at rest; auto-purge browsing data after 90 days |
| 6. Visibility & Transparency | Public privacy dashboard, plain-English policy, source code audit |
| 7. Respect for User | "Delete my account and all data" button, no friction, processed in 30 days |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Exam Pattern:** When asked "Apply PbD to a scenario," always **name the principle**, then give a **concrete UX element** that implements it. Generic answers lose 2–3 marks.
