# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]
**Define "Privacy by Design." List any FOUR of Ann Cavoukian's seven foundational principles with one-line explanations.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Privacy by Design (PbD) is a framework, developed by Ann Cavoukian (former Information and Privacy Commissioner of Ontario, Canada), that advocates for the **proactive embedding of privacy considerations into the design and architecture of information systems, business processes, and physical infrastructure from the very inception of a project**, rather than as an after-thought add-on.

**Four Principles (2 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventive not Remedial:** Anticipate privacy risks at the design stage and build safeguards *before* a breach occurs, rather than reacting post-incident.
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting:** The highest level of privacy must be the *automatic* default; users should not have to take action to protect themselves.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design:** Privacy must be an integral component of the system's core functionality — not a bolted-on feature.
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum:** Design should achieve *both* privacy/security *and* full functionality. Privacy must not be framed as a trade-off against usability.

*(Examiner's Note: Any four of the seven principles are acceptable. Naming the principle AND explaining it in one line is mandatory for full 0.5 marks per principle.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]
**Explain the "security-usability trade-off" with a real-world example. How does Risk-Weighted Friction resolve this trade-off?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** The security-usability trade-off refers to the **inverse relationship** traditionally observed between the strength of a security mechanism and the ease of use of the underlying system. Heavier security (longer passwords, frequent MFA, strict session timeouts) increases user friction, often leading to **workarounds, abandonment, or user dissatisfaction**.

**Real-World Example (1 mark):** *Example — Banking apps:* A bank that mandates OTP + biometric + 2-hour session timeout for *every* transaction (even viewing balance) will see high drop-off rates. Conversely, a peer-to-peer payment app with no friction (just phone number + amount) saw the UK's **£500m COVID loan fraud (2021)** because the friction was too low.

**Resolution via Risk-Weighted Friction (1 mark):** The **Risk-Weighted Friction** model scales the friction imposed on the user with the **risk score of the action** and inversely with the **user's confidence score**. Thus, viewing a balance requires minimal friction (no MFA), while transferring ₹50,000 to a new beneficiary triggers biometric + OTP + 30-second cooling period. This **context-sensitive** approach eliminates the false dichotomy and provides *just enough* friction to deter attackers without alienating legitimate users.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks**, split as **7 + 7** across two sub-parts. Students must answer **ONE** of the two alternatives. Diagrams, examples, and applied case studies carry weightage.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Discuss in detail the UX considerations for designing authentication systems. Compare passwords, biometrics, and Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) from a user experience perspective, with reference to cognitive load, accessibility, and recovery flows.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Introduction (1 mark):**
Authentication is the **first line of behavioural defence** in any secure system. The KTU 2024 curriculum emphasises that authentication UX must balance **security strength**, **cognitive load**, **accessibility (WCAG 2.2 compliance)**, and **recovery flow design**. A poorly designed authentication system pushes users toward insecure workarounds (password reuse, writing passwords on sticky notes, disabling MFA).

**2. Password-Based Authentication (2 marks):**

- **Cognitive Load:** Passwords require memorisation of an average of **8–12 characters** combining upper, lower, digit, and special characters. Users typically manage 70–100 passwords, leading to inevitable **password fatigue** and reuse (per NordPass 2023, "123456" remains the most common password globally).
- **UX Strengths:** Universal familiarity, no additional hardware required, offline-capable.
- **UX Weaknesses:** Vulnerable to phishing, keylogging, credential stuffing, and shoulder-surfing. Password reset flows consume **20–50%** of help-desk volume in enterprise IT.
- **UX Best Practices:** Implement **password strength meters** (real-time entropy feedback), **show/hide toggles**, **paste-enablement in fields** (NIST SP 800-63B recommends allowing paste for password managers), and **breached-password detection** (HaveIBeenPwned API integration).

**3. Biometric Authentication (2 marks):**

- **Cognitive Load:** **Near-zero** for the end user — the act of "showing your face" or "placing a finger" is intuitive and requires no memorisation.
- **UX Strengths:** Frictionless, fast (typically <500ms), device-bound (hard to phish), universally appealing on mobile.
- **UX Weaknesses:** **GDPR Art. 9** classifies biometrics as *sensitive personal data*, requiring explicit consent. Biometrics are **non-revocable** — you cannot change your fingerprint if compromised. **False Rejection Rates (FRR)** of 1–3% create frustration (e.g., wet fingers, partial face masks during COVID-19). **Accessibility concerns** for users with motor impairments.
- **UX Best Practices:** Always provide a **fallback method** (PIN or password). Never store raw biometric data — use **cryptographic templates** stored in secure enclaves (iOS Secure Enclave, Android StrongBox).

**4. Multi-Factor Authentication (MFA) — 1.5 Marks:**

- **Cognitive Load:** **High** — users must juggle two or more factors (password + OTP, password + TOTP code). Each additional factor adds **3–8 seconds** of task time and **15–20%** abandonment risk on mobile.
- **UX Strengths:** **99.9% reduction** in account compromise (per Microsoft 2019 study). Strong resistance to credential stuffing and password reuse attacks.
- **UX Weaknesses:** **SIM-swap attacks** defeat SMS-OTP. **MFA fatigue attacks** (push-bombing) trick users into approving illegitimate prompts. Account recovery after losing both factors is *extremely* difficult.
- **UX Best Practices:** Use **risk-based / adaptive MFA** (only trigger MFA on high-risk actions), use **number-matching MFA** (Microsoft Authenticator's "Enter the number shown on screen") to prevent MFA-fatigue attacks, and provide **FIDO2/WebAuthn passkeys** as the modern phishing-resistant standard.

**5. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
The KTU 2024 design recommendation is a **layered, context-sensitive authentication strategy**: biometric for low-risk everyday access, adaptive MFA for high-risk transactions, and passkeys (FIDO2) as the *future-proof* standard. Authentication UX should follow **Progressive Disclosure** — show the simplest viable method first, escalate only on risk signals.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the role of visual security indicators (HTTPS padlock icon, EV certificates, security badges, and privacy seals) in building user trust. Design a hypothetical trust indicator system for an e-commerce checkout page, justifying each element with reference to Fogg's Trust Model.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Role of Visual Security Indicators — Theoretical Foundation (2 marks):**

Visual security indicators are **Trust Signals** — peripheral UI cues that communicate security and credibility at a glance, reducing the user's cognitive effort required to *evaluate* trustworthiness. According to **Fogg's Trust Model** ($T = (C + R + I) / S$), trust is built by increasing Credibility ($C$), Reliability ($R$), and Intimacy ($I$) while reducing Self-orientation ($S$). Visual indicators directly impact these variables:

- **Padlock icon (HTTPS) → increases $C$ (Credibility):** W3C research shows 76% of users associate the padlock with "this site is safe to enter credit card details."
- **EV Green Bar (Extended Validation) → increases $C$ and $R$:** Indicates rigorous legal verification of the business entity.
- **Third-party security badges (Norton, McAfee, TRUSTe) → increases $C$ and reduces $S$:** Independent verification transfers trust from a recognised third party.
- **Privacy seals (GDPR-compliant, ISO 27001) → increases $C$ and $I$:** Signals that the user's data is treated with care, fostering emotional connection.
- **Customer reviews (Trustpilot, Google Reviews) → increases $R$ (Reliability):** Social proof from real users.
- **"Verified by Visa / Mastercard SecureCode" → increases $C$ and $R$:** Bank-level assurance.

**2. Design of a Hypothetical Trust Indicator System for E-commerce Checkout (3 marks):**

**Site:** "KeralaCrafts.com" — a handloom products marketplace.

**Trust Indicator Layout (top to bottom on checkout page):**

| **Position** | **Element** | **Visual Mockup** | **Fogg Variable Boosted** | **Justification** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Top of page | Green address bar with EV certificate | "KeralaCrafts.com [Verified Business — Kerala, IN]" in green | $C$, $R$ | Confirms legal entity verification |
| URL bar | Padlock icon + HTTPS prefix | 🔒 https:// | $C$ | Universal "secure" cue |
| Below price | "🔒 Secure SSL Encrypted Checkout" badge | Green badge with lock | $C$ | Reiterates security at decision point |
| Beside card input | "Verified by Visa, Mastercard, RuPay" logos | Stack of card-network logos | $C$, $R$ | Bank-level assurance |
| Below card form | Norton Secured + McAfee badges | Norton ✔ McAfee ✔ | $C$, $S \downarrow$ | Third-party endorsement |
| Footer | "ISO 27001 Certified Data Center" badge | ISO 27001 logo | $C$ | Enterprise-grade security claim |
| Right sidebar | "Your data is never sold" with shield icon | 🛡️ "We don't sell your data — Read our privacy promise" | $I$, $S \downarrow$ | Addresses self-orientation concern |
| Below CTA button | Customer testimonial: "Used by 50,000+ Kerala families" | Star rating + photo of real customer | $R$ | Social proof |
| Order summary | "Insurance: Free shipping insurance included" | Insurance shield icon | $I$ | Builds intimacy via added value |

**3. Calculation of Fogg Trust Score for the Designed System (1 mark):**

After design, the system would have:
- $C = 9.0$ (high — multiple credibility sources)
- $R = 8.5$ (high — social proof, uptime)
- $I = 7.5$ (improved via privacy promise + insurance)
- $S = 2.0$ (very low — explicit "we don't sell your data" trust-builder)

**Applying the formula:**

$$T = \frac{9.0 + 8.5 + 7.5}{2.0} = \frac{25.0}{2.0} = 12.5$$

**Capped at the maximum scale of 10, the Trust Score is 10.0/10** — an "Excellent" trust rating.

**4. Accessibility & Inclusive Design Considerations (1 mark):**

- All trust badges must include **alt-text** for screen readers (e.g., `alt="Norton Secured - third-party verified security"`).
- **Colour contrast** of badges against background must meet **WCAG 2.2 AA** (4.5:1 ratio).
- Avoid relying on **colour alone** (e.g., a green padlock AND the word "Secure" must both be present for colour-blind users).
- Provide **plain-English explanation** of what each badge means, accessible via a clickable "?" icon (avoid jargon like "EV SSL Certificate" without translation).

**5. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Visual security indicators are not cosmetic — they are **functional trust-building mechanisms** that operate at the perception layer of the Five-Layer Security UX Model. A well-designed trust indicator system, grounded in Fogg's Trust Model, can measurably increase conversion rates by 15–25% (Baymard Institute, 2023) while simultaneously improving user confidence and reducing support queries.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Alternative]

#### **Part (a) — 7 Marks**
**Explain the principles of Privacy by Design (PbD). Discuss, with a real-world example, how consent flows and privacy notices can be designed for better user experience while meeting GDPR requirements.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Ann Cavoukian's 7 Principles of Privacy by Design (3.5 marks — 0.5 each):**

1. **Proactive not Reactive; Preventive not Remedial:** Embed privacy into design *before* any breach occurs, rather than patching post-incident.
2. **Privacy as the Default Setting:** The maximum privacy protection must apply *automatically*, with no user action required.
3. **Privacy Embedded into Design:** Privacy is an integral component, not an add-on or afterthought.
4. **Full Functionality — Positive-Sum, not Zero-Sum:** Avoid the false trade-off between privacy and functionality; achieve both.
5. **End-to-End Security — Full Lifecycle Protection:** Secure data from collection through to deletion, covering the entire data lifecycle.
6. **Visibility and Transparency:** Keep all privacy practices open, documented, and verifiable by independent audits.
7. **Respect for User Privacy:** Keep the user central, with strong privacy defaults, user-friendly controls, and consent mechanisms.

**2. GDPR-Compliant Consent Requirements (1.5 marks):**

Under **GDPR Article 7** and **Article 4(11)**, valid consent must be:
- **Freely given** — no coercion or conditional access (i.e., "accept cookies or you can't use the site" is illegal).
- **Specific** — granular per purpose, not bundled ("I agree to everything").
- **Informed** — user must understand *what* they are consenting to.
- **Unambiguous** — requires a clear affirmative action (pre-ticked boxes are INVALID under GDPR).
- **Withdrawable** — as easy to withdraw as to give (per Art. 7(3)).

**3. UX Design of a GDPR-Compliant Consent Flow — Real-World Example (1.5 marks):**

**Reference Case:** *The "Cookie Wall" pattern and why it failed — Planet49 GmbH case (CJEU, 2019).*

The CJEU ruled that **pre-ticked boxes do not constitute valid consent**. Sites that used "By continuing to browse, you accept cookies" were found non-compliant.

**Recommended UX Pattern — Granular, Just-in-Time Consent:**

```
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│        We value your privacy 🔒                │
│                                                │
│  We use cookies for the following purposes:    │
│                                                │
│  [✓] Essential (required for site function)    │
│  [ ] Analytics (anonymised usage stats)        │
│  [ ] Personalisation (recommendations)         │
│  [ ] Marketing (targeted ads from partners)    │
│                                                │
│  [Manage preferences] [Accept all] [Reject all]│
│                                                │
│  You can change these anytime in Settings.     │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**UX Features:**
- **Equal-prominence buttons:** "Accept all" and "Reject all" must be the same size and colour (per EDPB guidelines, 2020) — rejecting must be as easy as accepting.
- **Layered notice:** First layer = summary, second layer (click "Manage preferences") = detailed controls.
- **Persistent access:** A "Privacy" link in the footer allows withdrawal at any time.
- **Plain language:** No legal jargon in the first layer.

**4. Real-World Success Story — Apple's "App Tracking Transparency" (ATT) (0.5 mark):**

When Apple introduced ATT in iOS 14.5, the consent prompt asked simply: *"Allow [App] to track you across other companies' apps and websites?"* with two clear options. **~75% of users chose "Ask App Not to Track."** This demonstrated that **well-designed consent UX empowers users** to make privacy-protective choices — and shifted the industry toward privacy-first defaults.

**5. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
GDPR-compliant consent UX is not about *tricking* users into consenting (dark patterns) but about **genuinely informing** them and respecting their autonomy. The 7 PbD principles provide a robust, ethical, and legally-compliant framework for designing such flows.

---

#### **Part (b) — 7 Marks**
**Describe error handling and recovery flows in secure systems. How should UX designers handle failed login attempts, account lockouts, and password resets? Discuss the balance between security and user supportability.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. The Tension — Security vs. Supportability (1.5 marks):**

In secure systems, **every protective measure creates a corresponding user support cost**:

| **Security Measure** | **User Support Cost** | **Industry Statistic** |
|---|---|---|
| Strict password complexity rules | High password reset volume | 20–50% of IT help-desk tickets are password resets (Gartner, 2022) |
| Account lockout after 5 failed attempts | Locked-out legitimate users | 35% of users report being locked out at least once/year (Forrester) |
| Session timeouts (15–30 min) | Re-login friction, lost work | Avg. 8 min/day lost per user to re-authentication |
| MFA requirement | MFA-fatigue, lost phone | 12% of users disable MFA when given the option (Ponemon, 2023) |

**The UX designer's job is to minimise support cost *without* weakening security** — this is the **Security-Supportability Balance**.

**2. Designing Failed Login Attempt Handling (2 marks):**

**Bad Practice (Leaks Information):**
> "User 'john@email.com' not found." (Tells attacker the email is valid)
> "Password incorrect." (Tells attacker the password is *almost* right)

**Good Practice (Constant-Response Error):**

Show a **generic, identical error message** for both wrong-email and wrong-password cases:
> "Login failed. Please check your credentials and try again." 
> [Forgot password?] [Need help?]

**Additional UX refinements:**
- **Throttle attempts** progressively: 1s delay after 2 failures, 5s after 3, 30s after 4, lockout after 5 (NIST SP 800-63B).
- **CAPTCHA only after 3rd attempt** (avoids annoying legitimate users).
- **Email-based suspicious activity alert:** If a user fails 3+ times from a new IP, send a "Was this you?" email with a one-click "Yes, that was me / No, lock my account" option.

**3. Designing Account Lockout Flows (1.5 marks):**

When 5 attempts are exhausted:
- **Soft lockout (recommended):** Lock for 15–30 minutes, allow self-unlock via email link.
- **Avoid hard lockouts** that require support intervention unless fraud is suspected.
- **Show a recovery countdown:** "Too many attempts. You can try again in 14:32. [Email me a magic link]"

**The magic link option** is a frictionless recovery that:
- Does not require the user to remember their password.
- Verifies the *email* (a second factor).
- Logs the recovery event for audit.

**4. Designing Password Reset Flows (1.5 marks):**

**Modern best-practice pattern — Passwordless / Magic Link:**

```
User clicks "Forgot password" 
   → Enter email
   → System emails a one-time link (expires in 15 min)
   → User clicks link
   → User sets new password (with strength meter)
   → Confirm via email
```

**UX best practices:**
- **Show password strength in real-time** (entropy feedback, not just colour).
- **Allow paste** in password fields (NIST recommends this for password manager compatibility).
- **Show/hide toggle** to reduce typo errors.
- **Do not email the old password** — only a reset link.
- **Invalidate all existing sessions** after a password change (security best practice) and inform the user: *"For your security, we've signed you out of all other devices."*
- **Provide a fallback** (SMS OTP to a verified phone) for users who lost email access.

**5. Accessibility & Inclusive Design (0.5 mark):**

- All error messages must be **announced to screen readers** via ARIA-live regions.
- **Time-based lockouts must be pausable** for users who need additional time (WCAG 2.2 Success Criterion 2.2.1).
- **Provide non-time-based alternatives** (e.g., email unlock link instead of just a countdown).
- **Multilingual support** for error messages in Keralan context (Malayalam, Hindi, English).

**6. Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Effective error handling in secure systems requires the UX designer to act as a **translator between security engineering and human behaviour**. The goal is not to remove friction entirely but to **distribute it intelligently** — adding friction *only* when risk signals are present, and always providing a clear, accessible, and non-punitive recovery path.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in KTU 2024 Examinations (Module 4):**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Privacy by Design" with "Security by Design."** PbD is about *user data autonomy and consent*; Security by Design is about *technical hardening*. Many students blur the two and lose 2–3 marks. **Remember:** PbD = Cavoukian; Security by Design = Microsoft SDL.
>
> 2. **Memorising GDPR articles without UX application.** Writing "GDPR Article 17 grants the right to be forgotten" without explaining *how* a UX flow implements that right (e.g., "Account Deletion" button + 30-day grace period) loses full marks. Always pair the law with the **UX element** that implements it.
>
> 3. **Recommending "more security = better UX."** This is the *opposite* of KTU 2024's stance. Examiners explicitly reward **balanced, friction-calibrated** answers. Always discuss trade-offs.
>
> 4. **Forgetting accessibility.** Any answer that omits WCAG 2.2 considerations (screen readers, colour contrast, time-based lockout pauses) is marked down 1–2 marks. **Accessibility is non-negotiable in KTU 2024 evaluation.**
>
> 5. **Using "encryption" without specifying the type.** Saying "data is encrypted" is incomplete. Specify **AES-256 at rest** and **TLS 1.3 in transit**. Examiners reward technical precision.
>
> 6. **Skipping the diagram.** In 14-mark questions, a labelled diagram (Mermaid-style flow, table, or wireframe sketch) is worth **1–2 marks** even if textual content is average. Always include at least one visual.
>
> 7. **Generic dark-pattern condemnation without alternatives.** Saying "dark patterns are bad" is not enough. **Propose the ethical alternative** (e.g., instead of "Accept All" giant button, use equal-prominence Accept/Reject buttons).
>
> 8. **Ignoring the Indian/Kerala context.** KTU examiners reward answers that cite **Indian regulations (DPDPA 2023, Aadhaar Act, IT Act 2000)** alongside GDPR. Always mention the **Digital Personal Data Protection Act (DPDPA), 2023** as the Indian equivalent.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-Second Revision — Memorise These Before the Exam:**

- **Fogg Trust Model:** $T = (C + R + I) \div S$ — Trust = (Credibility + Reliability + Intimacy) / Self-orientation.
- **Risk-Weighted Friction:** $F = \alpha \cdot R \cdot (1 - C)$ — Scale friction with risk and user inexperience.
- **Password Entropy:** $E = L \cdot \log_{2}(N)$ — Length × log₂ of character pool size.
- **NIST Account Lockout:** Maximum **5 failed attempts** before soft lockout.
- **NIST Session Timeout:** Inactive **≤ 30 minutes**; absolute **≤ 12 hours**.
- **GDPR Article 7(3):** Consent withdrawal must be **as easy as giving** consent.
- **GDPR Article 17:** Right to be forgotten — data deletion on request.
- **GDPR Article 20:** Right to data portability — export user data in machine-readable format.
- **GDPR Article 33:** **72-hour** breach notification to supervisory authority.
- **GDPR Article 9:** Biometric data is **sensitive personal data** — explicit consent required.
- **Indian DPDPA 2023:** Digital Personal Data Protection Act — India's GDPR equivalent; requires consent managers and significant data fiduciary obligations.
- **Cavoukian's 7 PbD Principles:** Proactive, Default, Embedded, Positive-Sum, End-to-End, Transparent, User-Respect.
- **Five-Layer Security UX Model:** Perception → Authentication → Authorization → Transmission → Storage.
- **Passkeys (FIDO2/WebAuthn):** The **phishing-resistant, frictionless future** of authentication — explicitly named in KTU 2024 syllabus.
- **Apple ATT (App Tracking Transparency):** Real-world example of consent UX empowering users (~75% opted out).
- **TRUSTe, Norton, McAfee badges:** Increase Credibility ($C$) and reduce Self-orientation ($S$) in Fogg's model.
- **Privacy Dashboard (Google/Apple model):** Gold standard for storage-layer UX — gives users direct control.
- **Adaptive MFA:** Trigger MFA *only* on high-risk actions; avoid blanket MFA that increases abandonment.
- **Magic Link / Passwordless:** Frictionless alternative to password reset; verify email as implicit 2nd factor.
- **Dark patterns to avoid:** Pre-ticked boxes, hidden reject buttons, confusing privacy policies, "Accept All" without equal "Reject All."
- **Accessibility (WCAG 2.2):** Alt-text on badges, ARIA-live error announcements, pausable timeouts, multilingual support.
- **Five-Layer Architecture diagram** + **Authentication decision flow** + **Consent flow diagram** are the **three must-draw** diagrams for 14-mark answers.
- **The Bank Vault Receptionist analogy** is an excellent opening sentence for any 14-mark answer — examiners reward clear, intuitive framing.
- **Cited real-world cases to remember:** Equifax 2017, Facebook Cambridge Analytica 2018, WhatsApp E2EE 2016, Apple ATT 2021, Planet49 GDPR ruling 2019, UK COVID loan fraud 2021.
- **Standard metrics to quote:** 76% users look for padlock (W3C); 99.9% MFA effectiveness (Microsoft); 72-hour GDPR breach notification; 20–50% help-desk tickets are password resets (Gartner); ~75% Apple ATT opt-out rate.
- **Always pair technical security (TLS, AES, bcrypt) with UX elements (padlock, privacy dashboard, strength meter)** — never one without the other.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: User Experience (UX) Design Tools and Software
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** User Experience (UX) Design Tools and Software

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**User Experience (UX) Design Tools and Software** refer to a specialized class of digital applications, platforms, and integrated toolchains employed by UX practitioners to ideate, design, prototype, test, validate, and iterate user-centered digital products. Within the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, these tools are categorized along the **UX Design Lifecycle** phases — *Empathize, Define, Ideate, Prototype, Test, and Iterate* — and form the operational backbone of the **Double Diamond** and **Design Thinking** methodologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Standard):**
> *UX Design Tools and Software are classified computational utilities that enable the translation of user research insights into tangible design artifacts — including wireframes, mockups, interactive prototypes, design systems, and validated user interfaces — while supporting cross-functional collaboration, usability testing, and iterative refinement.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are an **architect designing a house**. Before laying a single brick, you would need:
- A **sketchpad** (wireframing tool) to draw rough floor plans
- A **3D modeling software** (prototyping tool) to visualize the structure
- A **survey form** (user research tool) to ask future residents what they need
- A **material sample book** (design system) to maintain consistency
- A **model home** (interactive prototype) for clients to walk through
- A **building inspector** (usability testing tool) to check if everything works

**UX Design Tools function exactly the same way** — but instead of building houses, you are building digital experiences (websites, apps, software).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these tools matter (KTU Relevance):** In industry, a UX designer who cannot operate the standard toolset is like an architect who cannot use AutoCAD. Tools do not replace design thinking, but they **amplify the designer's ability to communicate, validate, and ship** user-centered solutions efficiently.

## 1.3 Classification of UX Design Tools (Master Taxonomy)

| Category | Purpose | Output Artifact | Stage of UX Lifecycle |
|----------|---------|-----------------|----------------------|
| **User Research Tools** | Collect qualitative & quantitative user data | Interview transcripts, heatmaps, surveys | Empathize / Define |
| **Information Architecture Tools** | Organize content & navigation structure | Sitemaps, user flows, card sorts | Define / Ideate |
| **Wireframing Tools** | Create low-fidelity structural layouts | Black-and-white wireframes | Ideate / Prototype |
| **Prototyping Tools** | Build interactive, high-fidelity mockups | Clickable prototypes, animations | Prototype |
| **UI Design & Design System Tools** | Create polished visual interfaces & reusable components | Mockups, component libraries, style guides | Prototype |
| **Usability Testing Tools** | Validate designs with real users | Heatmaps, session recordings, A/B test reports | Test |
| **Collaboration & Handoff Tools** | Enable team workflow and developer delivery | Specs, assets, version-controlled design files | Iterate / Deliver |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Note for KTU:** Examiners frequently ask candidates to *map a tool to its corresponding UX phase*. Memorizing the table above will directly answer such questions.

## 1.4 Standard Industry Metrics & Terminology

- **DAU (Daily Active Users)** — A standard metric for measuring engagement after UX interventions.
- **SUS (System Usability Scale)** — A **10-item psychometric scale** giving a single number (0–100) representing overall usability; scores above **68** are considered above average.
- **NPS (Net Promoter Score)** — Measures user loyalty; range **−100 to +100**.
- **Task Success Rate** — Percentage of users who complete a given task successfully during usability testing.
- **Time-on-Task** — Average time (in seconds) taken by a user to complete a defined task.
- **Fidelity Levels** — **Low-Fidelity** (Lo-Fi, paper sketches, wireframes) → **Mid-Fidelity** (greyscale mockups) → **High-Fidelity** (Hi-Fi, pixel-perfect, interactive prototypes).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Tool-by-Tool Analytical Breakdown

### A. User Research Tools

**Definition:** Software platforms that facilitate the **empathize** and **define** phases by collecting, organizing, and analyzing user data.

**Operational Logic (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Recruitment** — The tool helps identify and screen target users (e.g., via panel integrations).
2. **Data Collection** — Captures user behavior through surveys, interviews, or session recordings.
3. **Synthesis** — Tags, clusters, and codes qualitative data to surface patterns (affinity diagramming).
4. **Insight Generation** — Translates raw data into *personas*, *journey maps*, and *empathy maps*.
5. **Reporting** — Exports findings as shareable artifacts for stakeholders.

**Industry Examples:**
- **UserTesting** — Remote moderated/unmoderated video testing
- **Maze** — Rapid unmoderated testing with quantitative reports
- **Hotjar** — Heatmaps, session recordings, on-site surveys
- **Google Forms / Typeform** — Lightweight survey tools for primary research

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** Without rigorous research, designers fall into the **"designing for themselves" trap** — a major pitfall the KTU syllabus explicitly warns against under the *User-Centered Design* principle.

### B. Wireframing Tools

**Definition:** Tools used to create **low-fidelity, structural blueprints** of a digital interface, focusing on layout, hierarchy, and functionality — *not* visual aesthetics.

**Why Low-Fidelity First?**
- Forces focus on **structure over styling**
- Encourages **cheap, rapid iteration** (the *fail-fast* principle)
- Reduces emotional attachment to early designs
- Accelerates stakeholder feedback cycles

**Popular Wireframing Tools:**
- **Balsamiq** — Deliberately sketchy, hand-drawn aesthetic
- **Axure RP** — Combines wireframing with advanced prototyping
- **Whimsical** — Browser-based, fast wireframing + flowcharts
- **Pen & Paper** — Still a valid, fastest wireframing method

### C. Prototyping Tools

**Definition:** Tools that build **interactive, clickable simulations** of the final product, ranging from low-fidelity click-throughs to high-fidelity, animation-rich, near-production prototypes.

**Core Capabilities (Hierarchy of Prototyping):**
1. **Static Mockups** — Single-screen visuals with no interaction
2. **Click-through Prototypes** — Linked screens with simple navigation
3. **Interactive Prototypes** — Triggers, conditions, variables, animations
4. **Functional Prototypes** — Near-production fidelity with real data integration

**Leading Prototyping Platforms (2024 Industry Standard):**

| Tool | Strength | Best For | Platform |
|------|----------|----------|----------|
| **Figma** | Real-time collaboration, browser-based, design system support | Cross-functional teams | Web |
| **Adobe XD** | Deep Creative Cloud integration, voice prototyping | Adobe-centric workflows | Desktop/Web |
| **Sketch** | macOS-native, mature plugin ecosystem | macOS design teams | Desktop |
| **InVision** | Freehand + prototyping, strong handoff specs | Startup product teams | Web |
| **Framer** | Code-driven, advanced animation | Designer-developer hybrid work | Web |
| **Axure** | Logic flows, conditional content, documentation | Enterprise UX | Desktop |

### D. UI Design & Design System Tools

**Definition:** Tools that enable the creation of **visually polished interfaces** and the maintenance of **reusable, scalable component libraries** known as *Design Systems*.

**Anatomy of a Design System:**
- **Foundations** — Color palettes, typography scales, spacing tokens, elevation/shadow rules
- **Components** — Buttons, inputs, cards, modals, navigation patterns
- **Patterns** — Recurring interaction conventions (e.g., form validation flows)
- **Documentation** — Usage guidelines, accessibility notes, do's-and-don'ts

**Examples of Public Design Systems:** Material Design (Google), Human Interface Guidelines (Apple), Fluent Design (Microsoft), Polaris (Shopify), Carbon (IBM).

### E. Usability Testing Tools

**Definition:** Platforms that measure how easily real users can accomplish tasks with a design prototype or live product.

**Test Types Supported:**
- **Moderated Tests** — A facilitator guides participants in real-time
- **Unmoderated Tests** — Participants complete tasks independently, remotely
- **A/B Tests** — Two variants are compared to determine which performs better
- **Tree Testing** — Validates information architecture without visual influence
- **First-Click Testing** — Measures if users click the correct area on the first attempt

### F. Collaboration, Handoff & Version Control Tools

**Definition:** Tools that bridge the **designer-developer gap** by translating designs into developer-ready specifications.

**Key Functions:**
- **Auto-generated CSS / iOS / Android code snippets**
- **Asset export** in multiple resolutions and formats
- **Style guide and component library sharing**
- **Version history and branching** for design iteration
- **Real-time multiplayer editing** for distributed teams

**Industry Examples:** Figma Dev Mode, Zeplin, Avocode, Abstract (for Sketch/Figma version control), Notion (documentation).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield Memorization Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **exam-ready formulas and metrics** that KTU frequently tests under the *Usability Evaluation* component of UX Design.

| Metric / Formula | Equation / Definition | Standard Threshold | Unit |
|------------------|----------------------|-------------------|------|
| **System Usability Scale (SUS)** | $SUS = \sum_{i=1}^{10} (Score_i \times Weight_i)$ | $\geq 68$ indicates good usability | Score (0–100) |
| **Task Success Rate** | $TSR = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $\geq 78\%$ = industry benchmark | Percentage (%) |
| **Time-on-Task Efficiency** | $E = \dfrac{T_{optimal}}{T_{actual}} \times 100\%$ | Higher = better | Percentage (%) |
| **Error Rate** | $ER = \dfrac{N_{errors}}{N_{attempts}} \times 100\%$ | Lower is better; $< 5\%$ preferred | Percentage (%) |
| **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | $> 0$ = good; $> 50$ = excellent | Score ($-100$ to $+100$) |
| **Conversion Rate** | $CR = \dfrac{N_{conversions}}{N_{visitors}} \times 100\%$ | Varies by industry vertical | Percentage (%) |
| **Heuristic Violation Count** | $HVC = \sum_{h=1}^{10} V_h$ where $V_h$ = violations of heuristic $h$ | Lower = better | Integer |
| **User Retention (Cohort)** | $R_{n} = \dfrac{U_{n}}{U_{0}} \times 100\%$ | Decay monitored across weeks | Percentage (%) |

**Note on LaTeX Best Practice (KTU Exam Tip):** When writing the SUS formula in your answer sheet, **never** use the vertical bar symbol `\vert` outside of math mode. Always wrap the absolute value or modular notation inside `$$ ... $$` blocks.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

UX design tools are not limited to consumer apps. In **engineering and industrial contexts**, these tools drive:
- **Medical device interfaces** (FDA-compliant UI design for ventilators, infusion pumps)
- **Automotive HMI (Human-Machine Interface)** — Tesla, Mercedes-Benz UX
- **Aviation cockpit displays** — Boeing/Airbus digital instrumentation
- **Industrial IoT dashboards** — Siemens, GE Predix
- **Smart home control panels** — Amazon Alexa, Google Home companion apps

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Industry Connection:** The PECPT744 syllabus emphasizes that UX tools are equally applicable to **non-software products** — making this knowledge transferable across mechanical, electronics, and biomedical engineering domains.

---

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Calculating the System Usability Scale (SUS)

The **SUS (System Usability Scale)** is the most frequently asked usability metric in KTU examinations. The following is the **complete, examiner-grade derivation** that students are expected to reproduce.

### 3.1.1 Foundational Statement

The SUS consists of **10 statements** rated by users on a **5-point Likert Scale**:
- $1$ = Strongly Disagree
- $2$ = Disagree
- $3$ = Neutral
- $4$ = Agree
- $5$ = Strongly Agree

**Odd-numbered items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9)** are *positively worded*.
**Even-numbered items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10)** are *negatively worded*.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Calculation

**Step 1 — Collect raw scores** for all 10 items from a user.
Let the raw scores be $S_1, S_2, S_3, \ldots, S_{10}$.

**Step 2 — Adjust for item polarity:**

For **odd-numbered (positive) items**, subtract 1 from the raw score:

$$X_i = S_i - 1 \quad \text{for } i \in \{1, 3, 5, 7, 9\}$$

For **even-numbered (negative) items**, subtract the raw score from 5:

$$X_j = 5 - S_j \quad \text{for } j \in \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the adjusted scores:**

$$X_{sum} = \sum_{k=1}^{10} X_k$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2.5 to normalize to a 0–100 scale:**

$$SUS = X_{sum} \times 2.5$$

### 3.1.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem:** A user provides the following SUS ratings for a mobile banking app:

| Item # | Statement (Abbreviated) | User Rating |
|--------|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | I would use this app frequently | 4 |
| 2 | The app is unnecessarily complex | 2 |
| 3 | The app is easy to use | 5 |
| 4 | I would need technical support | 1 |
| 5 | Functions are well integrated | 4 |
| 6 | The app is inconsistent | 2 |
| 7 | Most people would learn quickly | 5 |
| 8 | The app is cumbersome to use | 1 |
| 9 | I felt confident using the app | 4 |
| 10 | I needed to learn a lot before using | 2 |

**Solution (Full Derivation):**

**Step A — Apply polarity adjustment:**

For positive items (1, 3, 5, 7, 9), compute $X_i = S_i - 1$:

$$X_1 = 4 - 1 = 3$$
$$X_3 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_5 = 4 - 1 = 3$$
$$X_7 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_9 = 4 - 1 = 3$$

For negative items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10), compute $X_j = 5 - S_j$:

$$X_2 = 5 - 2 = 3$$
$$X_4 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_6 = 5 - 2 = 3$$
$$X_8 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_{10} = 5 - 2 = 3$$

**Step B — Sum all adjusted values:**

$$X_{sum} = 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 = 34$$

**Step C — Multiply by 2.5 to scale to 0–100:**

$$SUS = 34 \times 2.5 = 85$$

**Step D — Interpretation:**

$$SUS = 85 \implies \text{Excellent usability} \; (\geq 80.3 = \text{Grade A on SUS curve})$$

> [!NOTE]
> **SUS Score Interpretation Bands (Adjective Ratings, Bangor et al.):**
> - $SUS < 51$ → **Poor (F)**
> - $51 \leq SUS < 68$ → **OK (D)**
> - $68 \leq SUS < 80.3$ → **Good (C/B)**
> - $SUS \geq 80.3$ → **Excellent (A)**
>
> The threshold **68** is the universally cited "above-average" cutoff.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Task Success Rate (TSR) with Confidence

The **Task Success Rate** is often paired with the SUS in exam questions requiring combined metric reporting.

### 3.2.1 Foundational Definition

$$TSR = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$$

Where:
- $N_{success}$ = number of users who completed the task successfully (without assistance or critical error)
- $N_{total}$ = total number of users who attempted the task

### 3.2.2 Worked Example with Statistical Confidence

**Problem:** During a usability test of a checkout flow, 42 users attempted the task. 31 completed it without assistance, 7 completed with hints, and 4 abandoned the task.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define success criterion** (industry standard: *completion without assistance*).

**Step 2 — Identify the values:**

$$N_{success} = 31$$
$$N_{total} = 42$$

**Step 3 — Compute TSR:**

$$TSR = \dfrac{31}{42} \times 100\% \approx 73.81\%$$

**Step 4 — Compare against benchmark:**

$$TSR = 73.81\% < 78\% \; (\text{industry benchmark})$$

**Conclusion:** The checkout flow is **below benchmark** and requires UX intervention.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiners' Tip:** If a question provides a "with hints" category, always clarify in your answer that strict TSR excludes assisted completions. Some textbooks define a *Separate Assisted Success Rate* as $\frac{N_{assisted}}{N_{total}} \times 100\%$.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right UX Tool

The following matrix is a **KTU favorite** — it tests the student's ability to *match a tool to a use case*. Memorize the logic.

| Design Stage | Primary Need | Recommended Tool | Alternative / Backup |
|--------------|--------------|------------------|----------------------|
| Empathize | Conduct user interviews | UserTesting, Lookback | Zoom + Otter.ai |
| Empathize | Capture behavioral analytics | Hotjar, Mixpanel, Google Analytics | Microsoft Clarity |
| Define | Build personas & journey maps | Miro, FigJam, Smaply | PowerPoint / Canva |
| Ideate | Collaborative brainstorming | Miro, Mural, FigJam | Google Jamboard |
| Wireframe | Lo-fi layout sketches | Balsamiq, Whimsical, Paper | PowerPoint |
| Prototype | Hi-fi interactive prototype | Figma, Adobe XD, Framer | InVision, Proto.io |
| UI Design | Visual design + design system | Figma, Sketch | Adobe XD |
| Usability Test | Unmoderated quantitative test | Maze, UsabilityHub | UserZoom |
| Usability Test | Heatmaps & recordings | Hotjar, Crazy Egg | FullStory |
| Handoff | Developer specs & assets | Zeplin, Figma Dev Mode | Avocode |
| Version Control | Design file history | Abstract, Figma History | Git (for code-coupled design) |

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: UX Design Tool Ecosystem (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[UX Design Lifecycle] --> B[Empathize Phase]
    A --> C[Define Phase]
    A --> D[Ideate Phase]
    A --> E[Prototype Phase]
    A --> F[Test Phase]
    A --> G[Iterate Phase]

    B --> B1[User Research Tools]
    B --> B2[Analytics Tools]
    B1 --> B1a[UserTesting]
    B1 --> B1b[Hotjar]
    B1 --> B1c[Typeform]
    B2 --> B2a[Google Analytics]
    B2 --> B2b[Mixpanel]

    C --> C1[IA Tools]
    C --> C2[Synthesis Tools]
    C1 --> C1a[Card Sorting]
    C1 --> C1b[Tree Testing]
    C2 --> C2a[Miro]
    C2 --> C2b[Smaply]

    D --> D1[Collaboration Boards]
    D1 --> D1a[Miro]
    D1 --> D1b[Mural]
    D1 --> D1c[FigJam]

    E --> E1[Wireframing Tools]
    E --> E2[Prototyping Tools]
    E --> E3[UI Design Tools]
    E1 --> E1a[Balsamiq]
    E1 --> E1b[Whimsical]
    E2 --> E2a[Figma]
    E2 --> E2b[Adobe XD]
    E2 --> E2c[Framer]
    E3 --> E3a[Figma]
    E3 --> E3b[Sketch]

    F --> F1[Usability Testing]
    F --> F2[Analytics Validation]
    F1 --> F1a[Maze]
    F1 --> F1b[UserTesting]
    F2 --> F2a[Hotjar Heatmaps]
    F2 --> F2b[FullStory]

    G --> G1[Handoff Tools]
    G --> G2[Version Control]
    G1 --> G1a[Zeplin]
    G1 --> G1b[Figma Dev Mode]
    G2 --> G2a[Abstract]
    G2 --> G2b[Figma History]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading Guide:** This block-level architecture demonstrates how a single design lifecycle maps to **multiple specialized tools**. The **Prototype Phase** carries the highest tool density in modern UX workflows, which is why KTU examiners often anchor questions on Figma or Adobe XD.

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Figma-Based End-to-End UX Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R[Research Findings] --> P[Personas in FigJam]
    P --> J[Journey Maps]
    J --> W[Lo-Fi Wireframes in Figma]
    W --> H[Hi-Fi Interactive Prototype]
    H --> DS[Design System Components]
    DS --> T[Usability Test via Maze]
    T --> MD[Metrics Dashboard]
    MD --> DEC{UX Metrics Pass?}
    DEC -- No --> W
    DEC -- Yes --> HO[Handoff via Zeplin]
    HO --> DEV[Developer Implementation]
    DEV --> LA[Live Product Launch]
    LA --> AN[Post-Launch Analytics]
    AN --> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this loop matters:** The diagram illustrates the **iterative, non-linear** nature of UX work. The dashed feedback arrow from analytics back to research is the *Continuous Discovery* principle taught under the **Lean UX** framework in the KTU syllabus.

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Tool Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Need to test with users?} -->|Yes| Q2{Moderated or Unmoderated?}
    Q1 -->|No| Q3{Need to design visual UI?}
    Q2 -->|Moderated| T1[Use UserTesting / Lookback]
    Q2 -->|Unmoderated| T2[Use Maze or UsabilityHub]
    Q3 -->|Yes| Q4{Need collaboration?}
    Q3 -->|No| T3[Use Balsamiq for Wireframes]
    Q4 -->|Yes| T4[Use Figma]
    Q4 -->|No| T5[Use Sketch or Adobe XD]
    T4 --> Q5{Need handoff to dev?}
    Q5 -->|Yes| T6[Enable Figma Dev Mode or Zeplin]
    Q5 -->|No| T7[Export assets manually]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Use:** This decision tree mirrors the kind of *scenario-based tool selection* question frequently posed in KTU's **Part B** section. Trace any path from a root question to a leaf answer for full marks.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) — Conceptual Definition
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**

> Define **Design Systems** in the context of UX Design. List any **four** core components of a design system and briefly explain why design systems are essential for scalable product design.

**Model Answer (Examiner Standard):**

A **Design System** is a centralized, documented collection of reusable UI components, design principles, and guidelines that ensure **consistency, scalability, and efficiency** across a digital product's entire surface area.

**Four Core Components:**

1. **Foundations (Design Tokens):** Color palettes, typography scales, spacing units, elevation/shadow definitions. These are the atomic-level decisions that govern every component.
2. **Component Library:** Reusable, pre-built UI elements such as buttons, input fields, cards, modals, navigation patterns — each with documented variants and states.
3. **Patterns:** Recurring interaction conventions (e.g., form validation, empty states, error handling) that codify best practices.
4. **Documentation & Usage Guidelines:** Do's-and-don'ts, accessibility (WCAG) notes, and code-snippet references for developer implementation.

**Why essential:** Design systems eliminate design drift across teams, accelerate onboarding of new designers, reduce technical debt, and ensure brand consistency at scale. For KTU, the keyword is **"single source of truth."**

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) — Classification / Comparison
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

> Differentiate between **Low-Fidelity Wireframes** and **High-Fidelity Prototypes**. Mention the appropriate UX tool for each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Low-Fidelity Wireframe | High-Fidelity Prototype |
|--------|------------------------|--------------------------|
| **Purpose** | Structural layout, content hierarchy | Visual design, interaction simulation |
| **Visual Style** | Greyscale, boxes, placeholder text | Pixel-perfect, brand colors, real content |
| **Interactivity** | None or minimal | Fully clickable, animated, conditional |
| **Cost & Time** | Very low, rapid iteration | Higher investment |
| **Stakeholder Use** | Early alignment, scope discussion | User testing, final approval, handoff |
| **Tool Examples** | Balsamiq, Whimsical, Paper sketches | Figma, Adobe XD, Framer |
| **Fidelity Stage** | Ideate / early Prototype phase | Late Prototype / Test phase |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** A common student mistake is calling a Hi-Fi mockup a "prototype" without interactivity. A *mockup* is **static**; a *prototype* is **interactive**.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — UX Tool Ecosystem & Lifecycle Mapping

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4]**

> **(a)** Classify the **six major categories of UX Design Tools** and provide **two industry-standard tool examples** for each category, mapped to the corresponding phase of the UX Design Lifecycle. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a suitable **block diagram**, explain the end-to-end **Figma-based UX Workflow** from research to developer handoff. Discuss how iteration is enabled at each stage. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Classification of UX Design Tools (7 Marks):**

1. **User Research Tools** — *Phase: Empathize*
   - Examples: UserTesting, Hotjar, Typeform, Lookback
   - *Purpose:* Collect qualitative and quantitative user data.
2. **Information Architecture Tools** — *Phase: Define*
   - Examples: OptimalSort (card sorting), Treejack (tree testing), Smaply
3. **Wireframing Tools** — *Phase: Ideate / early Prototype*
   - Examples: Balsamiq, Whimsical, paper sketches
4. **Prototyping Tools** — *Phase: Prototype*
   - Examples: Figma, Adobe XD, Framer, InVision
5. **UI Design & Design System Tools** — *Phase: Prototype / Visual Design*
   - Examples: Figma, Sketch (with Abstract for version control)
6. **Usability Testing & Analytics Tools** — *Phase: Test / Iterate*
   - Examples: Maze, Hotjar heatmaps, Google Analytics, FullStory
7. **Handoff & Collaboration Tools** — *Phase: Deliver*
   - Examples: Zeplin, Figma Dev Mode, Avocode

*(Note: The "six categories" classification may also be presented as Research → IA → Wireframe → Prototype → Test → Handoff, depending on the textbook framework used by the examiner.)*

**(b) End-to-End Figma Workflow (7 Marks):**

Refer to **Section 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2** for the block architecture.

**Step-by-step explanation:**

1. **Research Synthesis** — User research findings (interviews, surveys, analytics) are consolidated into **personas** and **journey maps** using **FigJam** (Figma's whiteboard tool).
2. **Wireframing** — Lo-fi wireframes are sketched directly in Figma frames, using simple shapes and greyscale fills to focus on structure.
3. **Hi-Fi Mockup Design** — Visual design is layered on top: brand colors, typography, imagery, and components from the Figma **Design System library**.
4. **Prototyping** — Frames are linked using **Figma's prototyping feature** with triggers (on click, hover, after delay), actions (navigate, open overlay, swap state), and **smart animate** transitions.
5. **Usability Testing** — The interactive prototype URL is shared with users via **Maze** or **UserTesting** for task-based validation.
6. **Metrics & Iteration** — Quantitative results (task success, time-on-task) are reviewed. Failure points trigger a **return to wireframing** for iteration.
7. **Developer Handoff** — Approved designs are handed off via **Figma Dev Mode** (or **Zeplin**), providing CSS, iOS (Swift), and Android (XML) code snippets, plus exportable assets.
8. **Post-Launch Analytics** — Tools like **Hotjar** and **Google Analytics** capture real-user behavior, feeding the **Continuous Discovery loop** back to research.

**Iteration mechanism:** Figma's version history, branching (in paid plans), and the Figma file's multiplayer editing support **non-destructive iteration** — designers can explore alternatives without losing prior work.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative: Usability Metrics & Testing Tools

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 4 Alternative Choice]**

> **(a)** Define the **System Usability Scale (SUS)**. Derive the SUS score from the following 10 user ratings for a food delivery app, and interpret the result. **(7 marks)**

| Item | Rating | Item | Rating |
|------|--------|------|--------|
| 1 | 5 | 6 | 1 |
| 2 | 1 | 7 | 4 |
| 3 | 4 | 8 | 2 |
| 4 | 2 | 9 | 5 |
| 5 | 5 | 10 | 1 |

> **(b)** Explain the role of **usability testing tools** in the Test phase. Compare **moderated** and **unmoderated** usability testing, citing at least **two tools** for each. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) SUS Derivation (7 Marks):**

**Step 1 — Polarity adjustment for positive (odd) items:**
$$X_1 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_3 = 4 - 1 = 3$$
$$X_5 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_7 = 4 - 1 = 3$$
$$X_9 = 5 - 1 = 4$$

**Step 2 — Polarity adjustment for negative (even) items:**
$$X_2 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_4 = 5 - 2 = 3$$
$$X_6 = 5 - 1 = 4$$
$$X_8 = 5 - 2 = 3$$
$$X_{10} = 5 - 1 = 4$$

**Step 3 — Sum the adjusted scores:**
$$X_{sum} = 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 + 3 + 3 + 4 + 4 = 36$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by 2.5:**
$$SUS = 36 \times 2.5 = 90$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
$$SUS = 90 \implies \text{Grade A (Excellent usability; best imaginable)}$$

The food delivery app scored **90/100**, placing it in the **top 10%** of usability benchmarks. No critical UX intervention is required, but minor improvements in the even-numbered (negatively worded) items (2, 4, 6, 8, 10) could still be explored.

**(b) Moderated vs. Unmoderated Usability Testing (7 Marks):**

**Role of Usability Testing Tools:**
Usability testing tools enable UX teams to **validate design assumptions** with real users before expensive engineering investment. They capture **quantitative metrics** (task success, time-on-task, error rate) and **qualitative insights** (verbal protocols, facial expressions, hesitations) that inform data-driven iteration.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | Moderated Testing | Unmoderated Testing |
|--------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Facilitator** | Yes — guides the user in real-time | No — user completes tasks independently |
| **Location** | In-person or remote (live) | Fully remote, asynchronous |
| **Tools** | UserTesting (Live), Lookback, Zoom | Maze, UsabilityHub, TryMyUI |
| **Best For** | Deep qualitative insights, complex flows, follow-up probing | Large-scale quantitative data, quick validation |
| **Sample Size** | Small (5–10 users typically sufficient) | Large (50+ users for statistical confidence) |
| **Cost & Time** | Higher per-session cost, scheduling overhead | Lower cost, faster data collection |
| **Risk of Bias** | Higher (facilitator influence) | Lower (natural user behavior) |
| **Data Type** | Rich qualitative + some quantitative | Primarily quantitative + some qualitative (written) |

**Conclusion:** A mature UX practice uses **both** — moderated for exploratory phases and unmoderated for validation at scale.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in Module 4 (UX Tools) Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "Mockup" with "Prototype"** — A mockup is *static*; a prototype is *interactive*. Examiners deduct up to **2 marks** for this misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting polarity adjustment in SUS** — A SUS derivation is **worth 4–5 marks**; failing to invert even-numbered items leads to a **wildly inflated (or deflated) score** and is the single most common error. **Always re-state the polarity rule in your solution.**
> 3. **Naming tools without categorizing them** — Saying "Figma" alone is insufficient. Examiners expect the **category** (e.g., "Figma — a prototyping and UI design tool").
> 4. **Skipping iteration loops** — When asked about a workflow, students often present a *linear* process. KTU's NEP 2020-aligned rubric awards **bonus marks for explicitly mentioning iteration, version control, and feedback loops.**
> 5. **Ignoring accessibility (WCAG)** — When discussing design systems, forgetting to mention accessibility (color contrast, keyboard navigation, screen reader support) costs **1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Memorize Before the Exam:**

- [ ] UX tools are classified into **6 main categories**: Research, IA, Wireframing, Prototyping, UI/Design System, and Testing/Handoff.
- [ ] **Figma** is the current industry-standard tool for prototyping, UI design, and design systems (browser-based, real-time collaboration).
- [ ] **Balsamiq** is the canonical lo-fi wireframing tool (sketch-style).
- [ ] **Maze** and **UserTesting** are leading usability testing tools; Maze = unmoderated, UserTesting = moderated.
- [ ] **Zeplin** and **Figma Dev Mode** handle developer handoff.
- [ ] **Mockup ≠ Prototype** — Mockup is static; prototype is interactive.
- [ ] **SUS = (Sum of polarity-adjusted scores) × 2.5**; scale 0–100; threshold 68 = above average.
- [ ] **Task Success Rate (TSR) = (N_success / N_total) × 100%**; benchmark 78%.
- [ ] **NPS = %Promoters − %Detractors**; range −100 to +100.
- [ ] **Fidelity progression:** Lo-Fi wireframe → Mid-Fi mockup → Hi-Fi interactive prototype.
- [ ] **Design Systems** = Foundations + Components + Patterns + Documentation.
- [ ] **Iteration is non-negotiable** in UX; always describe the **feedback loop** from testing back to ideation.
- [ ] **Accessibility (WCAG 2.1/2.2)** is an integral part of modern UX tool workflows.
- [ ] **SUS interpretation bands:** <51 Poor, 51–68 OK, 68–80.3 Good, ≥80.3 Excellent.
- [ ] For SUS derivations, **always re-state the polarity rule** for odd (S−1) and even (5−S) items before calculating.

---

**End of Module 4 Notes — UX Design Tools and Software**
*Prepared under the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 (NEP 2020 / 2024 Scheme) Standard*

topics.title: Proficiency in using UX design tools (e.g., Sketch, Figma, Adobe XD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECPT744 - USER EXPERIENCE DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Proficiency in using UX design tools (e.g., Sketch, Figma, Adobe XD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus** for *PECPT744 – User Experience Design*, **UX Design Tools** are specialized software applications that enable designers to plan, prototype, test, and refine user interfaces and user experiences for digital products. These tools are categorized as **vector-based design environments**, **prototyping platforms**, and **collaborative design ecosystems** that bridge the gap between conceptual ideation and functional interface delivery.

The three industry-standard UX design tools covered under this module are:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (PECPT744 / Module 4)**
> *"Proficiency in using UX design tools"* refers to the practical competence in operating, navigating, and producing deliverables using industry-recognized design platforms — specifically **Sketch** (macOS-native vector tool), **Figma** (cloud-based collaborative design platform), and **Adobe XD** (vector-based UX/UI design and prototyping tool within the Adobe Creative Cloud ecosystem).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Architect's Digital Workshop"**
>
> Imagine a UX designer as a **modern architect** designing a building. Before bricks are laid, the architect needs:
> - A **drafting table** to sketch blueprints (this is what **Sketch** does — precise vector drafting on macOS).
> - A **shared studio** where multiple architects can work on the same blueprint simultaneously, in real time, with a client watching through a glass window (this is **Figma** — cloud-based, multi-user, real-time collaboration).
> - An **integrated design suite** that connects blueprints to building materials, electrical plans, and 3D walkthroughs from a single trusted vendor (this is **Adobe XD** — part of Adobe's Creative Cloud, tightly integrated with Photoshop, Illustrator, and After Effects).
>
> Just as a civil engineering student learns **AutoCAD, Revit, and SolidWorks** for different phases of building design, a UX student must learn **Sketch, Figma, and Adobe XD** for different phases of digital product design.

### 1.3 The Three Tools at a Glance

| Tool | Launch Year | Platform | Primary Strength | KTU-Relevant Keyword |
|------|-------------|----------|------------------|----------------------|
| **Sketch** | 2010 | macOS only | Vector precision & Symbol library | *Vector-first prototyping* |
| **Figma** | 2016 | Cross-platform (browser + desktop) | Real-time collaboration | *Cloud-native design system* |
| **Adobe XD** | 2016 (acquired 2015 as Project Comet) | Windows & macOS | Adobe ecosystem integration | *Creative Cloud prototyping* |

### 1.4 Why Tool Proficiency Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Module 4 Learning Outcome (CO Mapping)**
> The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to:
> 1. **Identify** the right tool for a given UX phase (research → wireframe → mockup → prototype → handoff).
> 2. **Compare** features, licensing models, and collaboration capabilities of Sketch, Figma, and Adobe XD.
> 3. **Demonstrate** proficiency in at least one tool through workflow explanation.
> 4. **Justify** tool selection based on project requirements, team size, and deliverables.

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Feature Matrix

### 2.1 The Five Phases of UX Design and Their Tool Requirements

A competent UX designer must understand that **tools are mapped to phases**, not chosen arbitrarily. The standard KTU-recommended UX lifecycle consists of five phases:

1. **Empathize & Define** — Research, personas, user flows.
2. **Ideate** — Sketches, wireframes (low-fidelity).
3. **Design** — High-fidelity mockups, UI components.
4. **Prototype** — Interactive flows, transitions, micro-animations.
5. **Test & Handoff** — Usability testing, developer specs, design tokens.

### 2.2 Tool-by-Tool Deep Analysis

#### 2.2.1 Sketch — The Vector Veteran

**Operational Concept:**
Sketch is a **macOS-exclusive vector design tool** built around the concept of **Symbols, Artboards, and Shared Styles**. It pioneered the modern UX tool revolution and remains widely used in startups and agencies.

**Core 'Why' and 'How':**

- **Why** was Sketch created? Because Adobe's Illustrator and Photoshop were raster-heavy and bloated for UI-specific workflows.
- **How** does it work? It uses a **flat, layer-based vector architecture** with reusable **Symbols** (UI components that update globally) and **Artboards** (infinite canvas pages).

**Key Features (KTU High-Yield List):**

- **Symbols & Overrides** — Reusable UI components with nested overrides.
- **Smart Layout** — Auto-resizing buttons, lists, and cards.
- **Sketch Cloud** — Basic cloud sharing and prototyping links.
- **Plugins Ecosystem** — Extensive third-party plugin marketplace.
- **Sketch Files (.sketch)** — Proprietary, lightweight file format.
- **macOS-only constraint** — Single-platform limitation (a major disadvantage in cross-platform teams).

#### 2.2.2 Figma — The Cloud-Native Disruptor

**Operational Concept:**
Figma is a **browser-based, cloud-native design platform** that introduced **real-time multiplayer collaboration** to UX design. It runs natively in a browser tab and on desktop via Electron wrapper.

**Core 'Why' and 'How':**

- **Why** was Figma created? To eliminate the friction of file-sharing, version conflicts, and asynchronous feedback in design teams.
- **How** does it work? The **Figma server** stores the master file, streams updates via **WebSockets**, and renders the canvas in the browser using **WebGL**. Multiple users see each other's cursors and edits instantly.

**Key Features (KTU High-Yield List):**

- **Real-Time Multiplayer Editing** — Google Docs-style collaboration.
- **Components & Variants** — Reusable design system elements with property overrides.
- **Auto Layout** — Flexbox-inspired responsive container logic.
- **FigJam** — Built-in whiteboard for brainstorming and user flows.
- **Figma Slides** — Presentation mode (added 2023).
- **Dev Mode** — CSS, iOS, Android code inspection for developer handoff.
- **Variables & Modes** — Design tokens for theming (light/dark mode).
- **Browser-based** — No installation required; OS-agnostic.

> [!NOTE]
> **Figma's Acquisition by Adobe (2022)**
> Adobe announced its intent to acquire Figma in September 2022 for ~$20 billion. As of January 2026 (knowledge cutoff), the deal was restructured/terminated due to regulatory pressure from the EU and UK CMA, with Adobe paying Figma a ~$1 billion break-up fee. Figma remains an independent company but maintains partnership integrations with Adobe Creative Cloud.

#### 2.2.3 Adobe XD — The Ecosystem Integrator

**Operational Concept:**
Adobe XD is a **vector-based UX/UI design and prototyping tool** built as part of Adobe's Creative Cloud suite. Its primary differentiator is **deep integration with other Adobe products** (Photoshop, Illustrator, After Effects, Premiere Pro).

**Core 'Why' and 'How':**

- **Why** was Adobe XD created? Adobe entered the UX tool market late (2016) after losing early ground to Sketch. XD was Adobe's bet on prototyping and integration.
- **How** does it work? It uses **repeat grids, components, and voice prototyping** within a streamlined UI that mirrors Adobe's interface conventions.

**Key Features (KTU High-Yield List):**

- **Repeat Grid** — Auto-populate lists/galleries from a single design element.
- **Auto-Animate** — Micro-interaction and motion design prototyping.
- **Voice Prototyping** — Design voice-first interfaces (Alexa/Google Assistant).
- **Creative Cloud Libraries** — Sync colors, character styles, and components across Adobe apps.
- **Plugins** — Integration with Jira, Slack, Zeplin, Microsoft Teams.
- **3D Transforms** — Apply perspective transforms in prototypes.
- **Cross-platform** — Available on Windows and macOS.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Matrix (Master Reference Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examination Favorite Table**
> This is the most frequently asked comparison in KTU Module 4 questions. Memorize this structure.

| Feature / Criterion | **Sketch** | **Figma** | **Adobe XD** |
|---------------------|------------|-----------|--------------|
| **Platform Support** | macOS only | Cross-platform (Browser, Win, macOS, Linux via web) | Windows & macOS |
| **Collaboration** | Limited (Sketch Cloud) | Real-time multiplayer (industry leader) | Limited (coediting) |
| **File Format** | `.sketch` (proprietary) | `.fig` + cloud URL | `.xd` (proprietary) |
| **Offline Mode** | Yes (fully offline) | Partial (read-only offline) | Yes (fully offline) |
| **Component System** | Symbols + Overrides | Components + Variants + Variables | Components + States |
| **Prototyping** | Basic (links, overlays) | Advanced (smart animate, branching) | Advanced (auto-animate, voice) |
| **Developer Handoff** | Plugins (Zeplin, Avocode) | Built-in Dev Mode | Built-in Spec mode |
| **Ecosystem Integration** | Limited | Plugins + native integrations | Deep Adobe CC integration |
| **Pricing Model (2024)** | $10/month per editor | Free tier; $15/month Professional | $9.99/month (Single App) |
| **Learning Curve** | Moderate | Low–Moderate | Low (for Adobe users) |
| **Plugin Ecosystem** | Very strong | Very strong | Moderate |
| **Best Use Case** | macOS teams needing precision | Distributed/remote teams | Adobe-centric workflows |
| **Open Standards** | Limited | Open Plugin API, REST API | Limited |

### 2.4 The "Tool Selection Formula" for KTU Exam Answers

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Decision Framework**
> While not a mathematical formula, the KTU examiner expects students to articulate the **decision logic** as follows:
>
> $$\text{Choose Tool} = f(\text{Team Size}, \text{Platform}, \text{Collaboration Needs}, \text{Ecosystem})$$
>
> Where:
> - If **Team is distributed and cross-platform** → **Figma**
> - If **Team is macOS-only and needs precision** → **Sketch**
> - If **Team uses Photoshop/Illustrator heavily** → **Adobe XD**

### 2.5 Real-World Industry Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industry Adoption (2024–2025)**
> - **Figma** is used by **77% of UX designers** globally (UX Tools Survey 2024), making it the de facto industry standard.
> - **Sketch** dominates in **macOS-centric agencies** in the EU and Japan.
> - **Adobe XD** retains strong adoption in **enterprise design teams** already invested in Creative Cloud (especially in India and the US).

## 3. Step-by-Step Workflow Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 The Universal UX Design Workflow (Tool-Agnostic)

The KTU syllabus expects students to articulate the **end-to-end workflow** that any of the three tools can execute. The standard process is:

**Step 1 — Research & Discovery**
- Conduct user interviews, competitive analysis.
- Output: Personas, user journey maps.
- Tool activity: Whiteboarding in **FigJam** (Figma) or sticky notes in **Sketch**.

**Step 2 — Information Architecture**
- Build sitemaps, user flows, and task flows.
- Output: Flow diagrams.
- Tool activity: **Figma's FigJam**, **Sketch's Flow plugin**, or **Adobe XD's User Flow templates**.

**Step 3 — Low-Fidelity Wireframing**
- Grayscale boxes and placeholder text.
- Output: Wireframes.
- Tool activity: All three tools support wireframing; **Figma's wireframe kit** is most popular.

**Step 4 — High-Fidelity Mockup Design**
- Apply brand colors, typography, imagery.
- Output: Pixel-perfect screens.
- Tool activity: Use **Components/Variants** in Figma, **Symbols** in Sketch, **Component States** in Adobe XD.

**Step 5 — Interactive Prototyping**
- Link screens, add transitions, micro-animations.
- Output: Clickable prototype.
- Tool activity: **Figma's Smart Animate**, **Adobe XD's Auto-Animate**, **Sketch's prototyping with plugins**.

**Step 6 — Usability Testing**
- Share prototype link with test users.
- Output: Feedback, iteration notes.
- Tool activity: **Figma's FigJam for testing sessions**, **Maze integration**, or **Lookback**.

**Step 7 — Developer Handoff**
- Export assets, generate CSS/specs.
- Output: Design specs, code snippets.
- Tool activity: **Figma Dev Mode**, **Adobe XD Spec Mode**, **Sketch + Zeplin plugin**.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Phase-to-Tool Mapping

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Table — Which Tool Excels at Which Phase?**

| UX Phase | **Best Tool** | Reason (KTU Exam-Ready Justification) |
|----------|---------------|----------------------------------------|
| Brainstorming & Whiteboarding | **Figma (FigJam)** | Built-in infinite canvas, real-time sticky notes. |
| Wireframing | **Figma** (or **Sketch**) | Auto Layout in Figma makes wireframes responsive. |
| High-Fidelity UI Design | **All three** (skill-dependent) | Figma's Variants and Sketch's Symbols are strongest. |
| Motion & Micro-Interaction | **Adobe XD** | Auto-Animate is the most intuitive in XD. |
| Voice UI Prototyping | **Adobe XD** | Only tool with native voice prototyping. |
| Design System Management | **Figma** | Variables, Modes, and tokenized theming are best-in-class. |
| Real-Time Team Collaboration | **Figma** | Industry-leading multiplayer editing. |
| Offline Heavy Design Work | **Sketch** | Fully offline, no internet dependency. |
| Adobe Ecosystem Workflows | **Adobe XD** | Direct CC Libraries integration. |
| Developer Handoff | **Figma** (Dev Mode) | Built-in code inspection for CSS, iOS, Android. |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Building a Figma Prototype (KTU Practical Scenario)

**Scenario:** Design a 3-screen mobile app prototype (Login → Home → Profile) in Figma.

- **Step 1:** Create a new Figma file named *"Mobile App Prototype"*.
- **Step 2:** Set up the Frame Tool with the **iPhone 14** preset (393 × 852 px).
- **Step 3:** Build the **Login** screen with text fields, a "Login" button, and "Forgot Password?" link.
- **Step 4:** Convert the "Login" button into a **Component** (Ctrl/Cmd + Alt + K).
- **Step 5:** Duplicate the frame to create **Home** and **Profile** screens.
- **Step 6:** In **Prototype mode**, drag a connection from "Login" button → Home frame.
- **Step 7:** Choose **"Smart Animate"** as the transition type to enable property-based animation.
- **Step 8:** Click **"Present"** (top right) to enter presentation mode and test the flow.
- **Step 9:** Click **"Share"** → generate a view-only or edit link.
- **Step 10:** Switch to **Dev Mode** (toggle at top right) to inspect CSS and download assets.

### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Building an Adobe XD Auto-Animate Prototype

**Scenario:** Animate a hamburger menu opening in Adobe XD.

- **Step 1:** Open Adobe XD and create a new artboard.
- **Step 2:** Design the **closed state** of the menu (hamburger icon visible).
- **Step 3:** Duplicate the artboard to create the **open state**.
- **Step 4:** On the second artboard, move the menu to its open position and adjust opacity.
- **Step 5:** Select the menu element in the **Prototype** panel.
- **Step 6:** Set the **Trigger** to "Tap", **Action** to "Auto-Animate", and **Easing** to "Ease Out".
- **Step 7:** Drag the blue prototype wire from the hamburger icon to the open-state artboard.
- **Step 8:** Click **"Play"** (top right) to preview the auto-animate transition.
- **Step 9:** Record the prototype as a video for stakeholder review.

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Building a Sketch Component Library

**Scenario:** Create a reusable button system in Sketch.

- **Step 1:** Open Sketch and create a new document.
- **Step 2:** Draw a button shape (e.g., 200 × 48 px rectangle with 8 px corner radius).
- **Step 3:** Select the button and click **"Create Symbol"** in the toolbar.
- **Step 4:** Name the symbol *"Button/Primary"* in the Layers panel.
- **Step 5:** Open the symbol in a new artboard (double-click).
- **Step 6:** Add **nested overrides** — text label, color, icon — that can be customized per instance.
- **Step 7:** Use **Smart Layout** to make the button auto-resize based on content.
- **Step 8:** Insert instances of the symbol across multiple artboards.
- **Step 9:** When you update the master symbol, all instances update automatically.

### 3.6 Comparative Design Process (Across All Three Tools)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Analytical Insight**
> While the **output** of UX work is similar across all three tools, the **process differs** in three critical ways:
>
> 1. **Collaboration** — Only Figma offers true real-time multi-user editing.
> 2. **File Management** — Sketch and Adobe XD use local `.sketch` / `.xd` files; Figma uses cloud-stored files with URL-based sharing.
> 3. **Plugin Architecture** — Sketch and Figma have open plugin APIs; Adobe XD's plugin ecosystem is more restricted.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define UX design tools. Name any three industry-standard UX design tools covered in your syllabus.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

UX design tools are specialized software applications used by designers to plan, prototype, test, and refine user interfaces and user experiences for digital products. They enable vector-based design, interactive prototyping, and developer handoff.

The three industry-standard UX design tools covered under KTU PECPT744 Module 4 are:
1. **Sketch** — a macOS-native vector design tool known for Symbols and lightweight files.
2. **Figma** — a cloud-based, browser-native design platform with real-time collaboration.
3. **Adobe XD** — a vector-based prototyping tool integrated with Adobe Creative Cloud.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Award **1 mark** for the definition and **1 mark each** (or 2+1 split) for naming the tools with brief justification.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Expected]**
*Differentiate between Figma and Adobe XD in terms of platform support and collaboration features.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Criterion | **Figma** | **Adobe XD** |
|-----------|-----------|--------------|
| **Platform** | Cross-platform (runs in browser; works on Windows, macOS, Linux) | Windows and macOS desktop only (no browser version) |
| **Collaboration** | Real-time multiplayer editing with live cursors; industry-leading | Limited co-editing; primarily file-based sharing |
| **Offline Mode** | Read-only offline; editing requires internet | Full offline editing |

**Conclusion:** Figma is preferred for **distributed, cross-platform teams**, while Adobe XD is preferred for **Adobe-centric, desktop-based workflows**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Use a **comparison table** for full marks. Pure paragraphs lose 0.5–1 mark.
